## Strategic Coaching Applications

### 200 Medley Relay Standards

**Championship-Level Splits (Men):**

| Stroke | Elite Split | Championship Split |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Backstroke** | Sub-20.5 | 20.5-21.5 |
| **Breaststroke** | Sub-23.0 | 23.0-24.0 |
| **Butterfly** | Sub-19.5 | 19.5-20.5 |
| **Freestyle** | Sub-18.0 | 18.0-19.0 |

**Texas Example:**
- Back: 20.32 ✅
- Breast: 22.83 ✅
- Fly: 19.33 ✅
- Free: 17.80 ✅ ELITE

**Reaction Time Standards:**
- Elite: 0.00-0.20s
- Acceptable: 0.20-0.30s
- **Danger zone:** >0.30s or negative (DQ risk)

### 800 Free Relay Benchmarks

**Championship Performance Tiers (Men):**

**Record Territory (Sub-6:00):**
- Average splits: 1:29-1:30 per 200
- First 100: 43.5-44.0
- Consistency: <2.0s variance

**NCAA Finals (Top 8, 6:00-6:10):**
- Average splits: 1:30-1:33 per 200
- First 100: 43.5-45.5
- Consistency: <3.0s variance

**Qualifying Standard (6:10-6:20):**
- Average splits: 1:33-1:35 per 200
- First 100: 44.0-46.0

### Training Applications

**"Sub-6:00 Builder" Set:**
1. 4×200 @ 1:30 base (relay starts, positions 2-4 have +0.3 flying start advantage)
2. Track first 100 split (goal: 43.5-44.5)
3. Negative split each 200 (second 100 faster than first)
4. Rest: 3:00 between swims
5. Mental focus: Position 3-4 swimmers must hold pace under fatigue

**Medley Relay Exchange Drill:**
- 8×50 @ relay exchange speed
- Measure reaction times with timing system
- Goal: Consistent 0.10-0.25s reactions
- Penalty for early takeoff (<0.00): Team does 10 burpees

---

## Coaching Observations

### What Wins NCAA Relays

**1. Zero Weak Links**
- California's 800 free: All four legs 1:29-1:31
- Texas medley: Every leg was championship-caliber

**2. The Anchor Matters**
- Chris Guiliano (Texas): 17.80 free split = race-winning
- Lucas Henveaux (Cal): 1:31.47 anchor held off Texas

**3. Relay Discipline**
- 3 DQs in men's medley (Florida, Michigan, Virginia Tech)
- Early takeoffs and stroke violations cost NCAA titles
- **Practice exchanges with video review**

### Red Flags to Avoid

❌ **Reaction Time Drift:** If your team averages >0.30s on exchanges, you're leaving 0.5-1.0s on the blocks  
❌ **Inconsistent Splits:** >3s variance between fastest/slowest legs = not a championship relay  
❌ **Weak Anchors:** Don't put your 4th-best swimmer in position 4. Pressure matters.

**Coach's Notes:**
> California's 5:59.75 shows what's possible when four elite swimmers execute perfectly. Average 1:29.94 per 200 - that's the new standard. Texas pushed them all the way with their American Record. If you want to win NCAA relays, you need DEPTH, not just 2-3 stars.

**Actionable Drill:**
**"Record Chase Relay"**
- 4×200 free @ goal relay pace
- Full relay starts with timing
- Track: (1) reaction times, (2) first 100 splits, (3) total time
- Goal: Consistency within 1.5s across all legs
- Simulate championship pressure: Time trial format, announce splits

# Dave Salo: Career Background and Achievements

**Full Name:** Dr. David Salo  
**Location:** Irvine, California (Orange County)  
**Current Roles:** Irvine Novaquatics Head Coach/GM, Orange Coast College Assistant Coach  
**Education:** Master's and PhD (academic background in exercise physiology)

## Summary

Dave Salo is one of the most accomplished and innovative swim coaches in history, known for revolutionizing sprint and breaststroke training. With an academic foundation (PhD), he challenged traditional training methodologies and developed race-pace training protocols that produced Olympic champions across all distances and strokes. His career spans club coaching (Irvine Novaquatics since 1990), college coaching (USC), and Olympic coaching, with a resume that includes world record holders and gold medalists.

---

## Career Timeline

### Early Background

**Swimmer:**
- Breaststroker
- Swam for John Urbanchek at Long Beach State
- Made first Junior Nationals in 200 breaststroke
- Long Course Santa Clara International meet (1976-77)
- Entered illegally (didn't have qualifying time)
- Made the time, avoided $100 fine
- This experience shaped his understanding of breaststroke

**Academic Training:**
- Master's degree
- PhD (exercise physiology focus)
- Worked in laboratory for ~6 years
- Academically trained vs. experientially trained
- Professors challenged his thinking about traditional methods

---

## Irvine Novaquatics (Nova)

**Started:** 1990  
**Role:** Head Coach / General Manager  
**Tenure:** 35+ years (ongoing)

### First Generation (1990s)

**Early Athletes:**
- Aaron Peirsol - Backstroke legend
- Amanda Beard - Breaststroke Olympic medalist
- Jason Lezak - Sprint legend
- Young athletes who "didn't know any better"
- "Just go fast all the time" approach

### Current Generation

**Pro Nova Group:**
- Post-graduate athletes
- Small group
- More sophisticated training
- "Sneak up on speed" approach
- Semi-retirement mode for Salo

---

## USC (University of Southern California)

**Tenure:** Multiple years (retired)  
**Role:** Head Coach  
**Status:** Retired from USC, now at Orange Coast College

### Key Accomplishments:

**Athletes Coached at USC:**
- Rebecca Soni - 200m breaststroke WR, Olympic gold (2012)
- Vlad Morozov - Sprint star
- Large roster (80-100 athletes between college team and pro group)

**Challenges:**
- Managing college team + elite pro group simultaneously
- Compliance rules
- Recruiting pressures (pre and post transfer-portal)
- International athletes with varying backgrounds

**Why He Left:**
*"I don't miss NCAA Division I swimming - the landscape of recruiting and that process... you've got to recruit juniors, you've got to recruit seniors, then the transfer portal and they don't have to sit out, you've got to keep track, you've got to keep recruiting your own kids... you're just kind of always on edge about that whole thing."*

---

## Orange Coast College

**Started:** ~2022-2023  
**Role:** Assistant Coach  
**Head Coach:** Anthony Eady (Salo's former swimmer at Nova)  
**Status:** Paid assistant position

### Why Assistant Coach?

**The Dream:**
> *"My entire career except for the five years I coached with Peter Daland I've been a head coach. It was kind of in the back of my mind a dream of being an assistant coach for a change."*

**What He Does:**
- Sink the clocks
- Fix lane lines ("really good at tight lane lines")
- Be a sounding board for Anthony
- Take practices when needed
- Allow head coach to focus on coaching
- Handle parental issues
- No recruiting pressure
- No compliance paperwork

**Inspiration:**
- John Urbanchek retired from Michigan
- Became Salo's volunteer assistant at USC
- "Great to have John on the deck and as a friend as a mentor"
- Salo wanted to do the same for a younger coach

---

## Olympic Coaching

**2004 Athens Olympics:**
- Olympic coaching staff
- Story of Michael Phelps (age 15) opening care package
- Glycogen drink vs. Mike & Ikes candies

**Other Olympic Involvement:**
- Multiple Olympic cycles
- Produced numerous Olympic medalists
- Gold medals across distances (50m to 1500m)

# Dave Salo: The Freedom of Being an Assistant Coach

**Category:** Coaching Philosophy, Career Transition, Assistant Coaching, Mentorship  
**Coach:** Dave Salo  
**Source:** SwimSwam Podcast - Why is Dave Salo an Assistant Coach at Orange Coast College

## Summary

After decades as a head coach at elite programs, Dave Salo made the intentional choice to become an assistant coach at Orange Coast College under his former swimmer Anthony Eddie. This interview reveals his philosophy on assistant coaching, the freedom it provides, and how veteran coaches can serve without ego while maintaining high standards.

---

## Why Become an Assistant?

### The Dream

> "It's always been kind of in the back of my mind—a dream of being an assistant coach for a change. My entire career except for the five years I coached with Peter Daland, I've been a head coach. Head coach responsibilities as you know are pretty enormous, and it was kind of cool to think about the idea of just being somebody's assistant."

**The Vision:**
- Come on deck
- Sink the clocks
- Fix the lane lines ("which I'm really good at—tight lane lines")
- Be there with experiential resources
- If you just need to get your watch out, that's good too

**Common Desire:**
> "There's a lot of us as retired or semi-retired head coaches [who] look forward to not having as much of the head coaching responsibilities."

### The Plan with Anthony Eddie

**John Urbanchek Parallel:**
- Swam for John at Long Beach State when John got into college coaching
- John is long-time friend
- When John retired from Michigan, moving back to Fullerton (close to USC)
- Asked John to be volunteer assistant at USC
- "Great to have John on the deck as a friend, as a mentor"

**Promise to Anthony:**
> "I told Anthony that at the point at which I would retire, I would certainly come back, and if he would have me, I'd be his volunteer assistant, which is what I suggested."

**No More Volunteers:**
- They don't accept volunteers anymore
- Only take paid positions
- "I said okay, pay me—that's good"

### Why Orange Coast College

**Perfect Fit:**
- Great beautiful new facility
- Anthony swam for Dave, similar ideas about coaching
- "I'm just there to help him out—young coach, good coach, very successful"
- "I thought I could be of service"

---

## Assistant Coach Responsibilities

### Not What You'd Expect

**Interviewer:** "What other kind of assistant responsibilities do you have to step back into?"

**Dave's Response:**
> "Anthony's never had really dedicated assistant coaches that are just swimming assistant coaches. I'm just trying to help him be better, help him take some things off his plate."

**Family First:**
- Wife expecting second child
- "Really important thing for him and his family"
- Always tried to create environment for assistants to be good family people, not just coaches
- "I don't have a wife or kids or dog or anything, so where I can pitch in to help—be a little bit easier for him"

**Specific Examples:**
- Can take care of practice when Anthony needs to get home to wife
- Taking son out to aquarium
- Anthony doesn't have to worry about getting to deck
- Can trust Dave because of coaching experience

### Adaptability

> "I'm tremendously adaptable, whatever the circumstances are with the athletes."

**Community College Spectrum:**
- Kids who barely can swim
- To bounce-backs from Division I
- "I can adapt to either end of the spectrum and probably be pretty effective"

**Relaxing:**
> "It's extremely relaxing. I love to coach, I love to create training design and implement my ideas about the methodology. It's just fun to be able to watch these kids get excited about training."

**Shared Methodology:**
> "Both Anthony and I employ the same kind of methodology for these community college kids—come in and have an experience that's really really positive."

---

## What Dave Doesn't Miss

### NCAA Division I Recruiting

> "The one thing I don't miss from NCAA Division I swimming is the landscape of recruiting and that process."

**Used to Love It:**
- Calling kids
- Talking about program
- Getting to know them

**Became an Endeavor:**
- Have to recruit juniors
- Have to recruit seniors
- Transfer portal—don't have to sit out
- Got to keep recruiting your own kids

**The Fear:**
> "We've all been in that position where you've had a kid that's come into your program, has done well, and they walk in your door and say 'well I'm transferring.' It's like WHAT?! You're just kind of always on edge about that whole thing. I don't miss that."

### Future Recruiting at OCC

**Top-Notch Athletes:**
- Urging Anthony to push towards recruiting really top-notch athletes
- California community college rules allow it
- Also allowed to consider international athletes

**Unique Opportunity:**
- Beautiful facility
- Dave's experiences
- Will intrigue international athletes
- "Great training environment—that's what we can provide at Orange Coast College"

---

## What Coaches Can Prove at This Stage

### Not About Proving Anything

> "I'm not trying to prove anything. I've had a great career, and I could—I don't need to do this. It's great to be at this point in time where you're financially fine, professionally you've had an exceptional career. Hall of fame coach, Olympic coach, and all that stuff. Now it's just being able to give back as much as I can."

### Role as Sounding Board

**Anthony is Bright:**
- Knows what he wants and how he wants to do it
- Bounces things off Dave
- "We have great conversations about swimming, great conversations about Elon Musk and his principles"
- "Really a bright kid—I shouldn't say kid, he's 40 years old"

**Just There:**
> "I'm just there to allow him—it's I go back to when I was coaching at USC."

### USC Philosophy on Assistant Coaches

**After First Two Years:**
> "I was so focused on the athletes and trying to take care of my coaches, and I got really uncomfortable about just what I was doing."

**The Talk:**
> "I said look, I have to coach the way I coach. I can't coach how you want to coach or what your needs are. I can't be distracted by what your needs are as coaches. You just have to know that I trust you, that I'm going to give you a lot of responsibility."

**Not Writing Everything:**
> "I'm not going to write every single workout you've got to do verbatim. You're going to get your hands in the mix. But I'm going to lead the troops, I'm going to run it the way I'm comfortable with. You just have to fall in line and figure it out."

**Be Prepared:**
> "If I say 'okay, in the next five minutes you're taking the sprinters, go do something with them, make them faster,' then they just have to trust that's how I operate. That's how I operate as a coach. I'm better that way."

**For Anthony:**
> "I'm there to help Anthony just be Anthony and to be able to say 'hey Dave, take the next set,' it's like no problem, I can take it. I don't have to ask him."

---

## Listening and Adapting

### Equipment Example

**Anthony's Comment:**
> "We got to start using that [new equipment], otherwise it was a waste of money to buy that equipment."

**Dave's Response:**
> "I took that as a directive to make sure okay, the next set we do, we're going to use that piece of equipment because I want there to be value to the equipment they purchased."

### Lane Line Example

**Anthony's Comment:**
> "Tomorrow I'm gonna get to that [broken lane line]."

**Dave's Action:**
> "I took it upon myself to take care of that day, so he didn't have to worry about it. He could come back and go 'oh good, I don't have to worry about that lane line.'"

**Philosophy:**
> "I'm really good at listening to what a coach says."

---

## Freedom of Assistant Coaching

### Ask Other Veterans

> "If you ask any of us that have gotten back into an assistance role after so many years of being head coaches—Dennis Dale comes to mind when he left Minnesota, went to Indiana, or Mark Bernardino who was at Virginia for a long time and he's still coaching as an assistant coach—you'll ask any of us:"

**The Answer:**
> "It's freeing. It's tremendous freedom to be able to just coach—not worry about recruiting to the extent that you do as a head coach, or giving up scholarships, or doing the compliance paperwork, or all the nonsense that goes along with especially college coaching."

### At Nova Too

**Same Approach:**
> "I do that with my club team too at Nova. I'm there to allow my coaching staff to focus more on the coaching."

**Parental Influences:**
> "When they have issues with parental influences and stuff like that, I can take the lead on that and put parents back where they need to be, and let the coaches coach—not be frustrated or worried about some parent peeking over their shoulder or whatever."

### Skill Set for Anthony

**At Orange Coast College:**
> "Let him just coach and do what he needs to do. Be there for his backup. Be there if he can't make practice because his wife's gonna have a baby, or just gotta go watch his little boy do T-ball. That's how my role is. I'm not trying to prove anything—just help Anthony continue the successes that he's developed at Orange Coast College."

---

## The Best Interview Answers

### Authentic, Not a Stepping Stone

**Interviewer's Observation:**
> "It sounds like all the best interview questions—or interview answers I should say—because that's exactly what I would hope young coaches would be able to understand as they step into assistant roles. They're just always so hungry to step up and do more and stuff that it's really hard for them to be good assistants, to be satisfied with where they're at."

**Industry Push:**
> "Especially with our industry, they're constantly being pushed: push push, you gotta get next, gotta be stepping next."

**Fortunate for Anthony:**
> "It's very fortunate to have a coach who: A) knows exactly how to step in and be a head coach if you need him in a situation where you're gonna have two kids—he's gonna need you, Dave. And B) this guy's not coming for my job. This guy's not in it for any of this. He appreciates the lack of responsibility as opposed to envies the position in that way."

**Why It Works:**
> "It makes the best interview answers because it's genuine and it's authentic. It's pretty cool to hear from a top-level guy who has all of the results you could possibly ask for—all the checkmarks on the resume, it's all happened—for you to be talking about the root role of it is cool."

---

## Creating Sandboxes

### For Coaches at Nova

> "I'm not a micromanager. I don't want to stand over them and say 'well what is that, why not this, why?' I just want to make sure they have their room."

**Communication:**
> "I would love to have continuous communication to some degree with them to make sure that if they run into problems, they have it."

### For Athletes

**The Reputation:**
> "You write the workout and then you let the athlete do it. You're there on deck and engaging, but it's not like you're constantly talking to them or talking them through it. It's much more like: here it is, here's the sandbox, now do what you can with it. Not because you're not hearing—I hear you're hearing and observing—but you're taking out your control of the entire pool so the kids can get some autonomy, the young adults and adults, the pros that you coach can get some autonomy over what they're doing."

**Dave's Response:**
> "I don't know if that's true of me actually. I do give my athletes a lot of choice in terms of the training, but I'm adamant about detail. Detail has to be done exactly the way I envision things."

---

## Detail-Oriented Coaching

### Choice Within Structure

> "Yeah, they get some room for choice."

**Never Writing Workouts in Advance:**
> "I don't write my workouts in advance of a workout. In particular with the group I'm coaching now, I'm never quite sure who's going to be in practice. To design a workout based on who may or may not come to practice doesn't make sense—it's not very fruitful."

### Driven by Stimuli

> "I'm very much driven by all kinds of different stimuli that's going to help create an environment where we can be really effective, really fast, really efficient."

**What Impacts Him:**
- What do kids look like when they get through warmup?
- Are they in good moods or not in very good moods?
- The weather—sometimes will dictate what a workout might look like
- Not worried about what tomorrow's practice is
- Really don't care about what yesterday's practice is

### Famous for Not Telling Reps

> "I'm famously known for not telling the kids how many repetitions of a set that we're doing. When they ask how many we're doing, I always tell them: you can't do anything about number 22, so don't worry about how many we're doing. Just work, focus on the one that we're on."

**They Get Used to It:**
> "Sometimes they've gotten used to that: 'he's not going to tell us how many we're doing.'"

**The Reason:**
> "I purposely don't do that. Not every set, but there are a number of sets that I don't have a certain number of repetitions that I want done because I need to see how this evolves—how the set evolves—and get a sense that we're getting where I want it to be."

**The Crescendo:**
> "Then there's kind of a crescendo of how a set is running to the point where: okay, now they're becoming disinterested or they're not putting in the same effort. Now it's time to pull back and do something else."

### Moment-by-Moment

> "I'm really reflective of how my athletes are performing on a moment-by-moment basis. Not even a macro cycle or a mini cycle. It's just day-to-day. It's minute to minute."

---

## Constant Communication

### Always Talking

> "If anybody watches me work on deck, they'll find that I'm constantly talking, constantly making communication with the athletes."

**General Pronouncements:**
> "A lot of times it's kind of a general pronunciation of what we're trying to do, just to keep reminding the kids what we're after. Sometimes it's individualized."

**Fear of Silence:**
> "I've always felt ill at ease if I'm not constantly coaching because I think it almost feels like I'm afraid that the kids will think I don't care because I'm not saying something or chirping out something or saying some profound statement about this set and how important it is."

**Responsive:**
> "I'm really responsive to my athletes on a moment-by-moment basis."

### The Painting Metaphor

> "I've got very certain elements of my training that I want to accomplish in every given workout. I look at it as I'm trying to put together a painting, and all these components of this painting have to be put in there."

**Components:**
- Power
- Strength
- Flexibility
- Mechanics
- All these different components go into performance

**Implementation:**
> "I'm trying to implement them all into my painting. Everything—one set leads to the next set leads to the next set leads to the next set. But like I said before, I don't plan that out way in advance."

### Each Workout Is Its Own Entity

> "I just look at each workout is in itself its own entity. It's cumulative in the sense that you've got workouts going from day one to day 144. But I look at it: put yesterday aside, today is different. It's going to be just as hard as yesterday and tomorrow will feel like we never get a break."

**The Truth:**
> "It's like no, your body's tremendously adaptable and plastic, and it's got some capacities that we just need to reach towards."

### Characterization

> "In characterizing me, I'm constantly yapping, talking, whistling, giving feedback. I think some of my statements are really profound, although I'm not sure they think it's profound. Sometimes they speak in different languages because I know how to say 'fast' in a bunch of different languages."

---

## No Metrics Tracking

### Why Not

> "I don't track anything. I've seen over the years too many coaches who get caught up in tracking whatever metrics they're looking at or looking at a particular set and determining what kind of success they're going to have based on that, and it's so unpredictable."

**Lactate Example:**
> "We went for a number of years where coaches were monitoring lactate, and all of a sudden the lactates weren't going the direction they wanted them to or expected them to, and they'd start freaking out and think their season's lost."

**His Approach:**
> "I don't do that. I just expect my athletes to come in every day with the intent to work really hard and swim fast every day, and not worry about the metrics per se."

**The Reality:**
> "If you're doing the work every day, you're working really hard, you're going to get in shape. It just comes with working hard. So I don't focus too much attention on that. I've used some metrics in the past but I've kind of gotten away from it because things will plateau out and then you're going 'oh no! Uh-oh!'"

### Figure It Out (FIO)

**Terry McKeever's Phrase:**
> "I borrow a phrase that probably Terry McKeever uses a lot and I use a lot as well with my athletes."

**When They Ask Questions:**
- "Watch my stroke rate or whatever"
- "FIO. FIO. You have to figure it out."

**Simple Metrics:**
> "You got to figure it out. You're trying to get from A to B and you're trying to get there fast. Count your strokes—it's real simple. Count your strokes. If you're going faster and you're getting less strokes, that's more efficiency."

**Result:**
> "A lot of times there's a lot of FIOs. I know Terry does that with her kids all the time too—figure it out."

---

## Detail-Oriented Yet Autonomous

### The Paradox

> "A lot of my athletes will tell you that I'm very detail-oriented and have high expectations. At the same time, sometimes they characterize me as 'he lets us do whatever we want.'"

**The Truth:**
> "It's not that simple. It's yeah, you can do whatever you want as long as you do what I want you to do."

### Discovery in Workouts

> "There's a lot of discovery that goes on in my workouts too, because sometimes I have an intent of what I think something needs to be, and they will misread my intent. I will learn from that misinformation—they kind of take it and run with it."

**The Learning:**
> "I kind of use that as a vehicle to: 'oh well, I didn't really think that, but I didn't communicate what I wanted. But they showed something to me that might be a little bit different.'"

---

## Player's Coach?

### Pete Carroll Comparison

**Interviewer:** "Would you consider yourself a player's coach? I don't want to go Pete Carroll with the USC connection..."

**Dave's Response:**
> "I'm not sure I would characterize myself. Pete Carroll is awesome, and I loved watching Pete Carroll. I wish I had his—there's a lot of characteristics that I like about Pete."

### John Urbanchek Parallel

> "He's a lot like Urbanchek. Urbanchek might be considered a 'player's coach' kind of guy. He gets away with so much because he's got that stupid Hungarian accent of his."

**The Accent:**
- Been here 60 years, still has an accent
- Playing it up
- "One of the most endearing qualities among the many about him"
- At home talking to wife Mel, speaks perfect English
- Around everybody else: Hungarian accent

**We Need a Recording:**
> "We've got to get a recording of John's American accent."

### "I Hate Them All"

> "I used to tell people 'do you like so-and-so better?' I said no, I don't like anybody, I hate everybody. It's just the same. But that's not true."

**In Club Coaching:**
> "You play favorites, I don't play favorites. I hate them all. If they're all hated, then they're fine."

**Evolved Thinking:**
> "I went through phases of that. I went through the coach phase where you were supposed to play favorites—that was like how you divvied out your currency, your attention. If you gave it, the only other option is that you just get angry at all the kids who are not doing it right and not there. Yeah, I do have favorites: the kids who are coming every day. Then I realized that's still not quite enough."

---

## Community College Unique Characteristics

### Broad Skill Range

> "The skill set of these kids is a really broad range. Water polo kids have just finished their season, now they're going into swim season. You just got to be a lot more aware that their skill sets are so different."

**Unique to Community College:**
> "Community college has its own kind of characteristics that are kind of unique. You got to be careful with these kids a lot because they do this purely out of the joy of the sport."

**No Scholarship:**
- Not on scholarship
- Can do it or not do it
- They choose to be there

### Flexibility Required

**One Thing to Learn:**
> "There's a lot more class conflict and they've got jobs that they've got to go to, so their attendance is sometimes scattered. I want people at practice, and I want them in on time—those kind of things that are really important to me. But you've got to be a lot more flexible with the community college kids."

**Can Still Ask the Same:**
> "But I think you can still ask of them the same thing."

### Hit the Gas

**Conversation with Anthony:**
> "I told him a couple weeks ago, because we have such an abbreviated season: 'Look, you're being a little too cautious. I think these kids can handle a lot more than you're giving them. I think you just said we don't have much time—so let's just kind of get after it.'"

**Anthony's Approach:**
> "He's a seasoned veteran, so he's been trying to be very progressive, and I'm like 'just do it, let's go. We don't have time to wait, we've only got five weeks left—hit the gas!'"

**Dave's Adjustment:**
> "I gotta kind of roll that back a little bit. Somebody's a little out of breath when they're sitting on the wall, I gotta kind of ignore that a little bit because I'm like 'come on!' That's my tendency, so I gotta make sure: 'are you okay? Is everything all right?'"

### Why They're There

> "It's a different group. But they're there because they want to—they want to swim. No other, there's nothing else driving them. It's a really interesting dynamic."

---

## Best of Both Worlds

### Freedom Plus Impact

**For Dave:**
- No recruiting pressure
- No compliance paperwork
- No scholarship decisions
- No transfer portal anxiety
- Can just coach
- Can focus on the art of training design
- Helps when Anthony needs family time
- Uses decades of experience as sounding board
- Genuine enjoyment of being on deck

**For Anthony:**
- Hall of Fame coach as assistant
- Olympic-level experience and insight
- Complete trust to run practice if needed
- No ego threat—Dave's not after his job
- Mentorship from someone who's done it all
- Sounding board for ideas
- Relief from some logistical burdens
- Model of veteran coach staying humble

### The Real Motivation

> "I'm lucky to be working with Anthony at Orange Coast College."

**Recruiting Pitch:**
> "If you've got any athletes that are looking for a really great environment to swim, college pirates!"

---

## Key Lessons for Coaches

### How to Be a Great Assistant
- **Check your ego:** Not about proving anything anymore
- **Listen actively:** Anthony's comments about equipment or lane lines become directives
- **Adapt fully:** Support head coach's vision, not impose your own
- **Add value quietly:** Fix problems before head coach has to think about them
- **Trust the process:** Young coach knows what he wants, just needs support
- **Family first:** Cover practice so head coach can be with family
- **Experience without ego:** Offer insights when asked, don't impose

### Freedom of Letting Go
- **Head coaching burden:** Recruiting, compliance, scholarships, transfer portal
- **Assistant freedom:** Just coach, just teach, just be on deck
- **Veteran perspective:** After decades at top, service without ego is fulfilling
- **The dream realized:** "Always been in the back of my mind"

### Detail vs. Autonomy Balance
- **Detail-oriented:** Expects precise execution of vision
- **Room for choice:** Athletes make decisions within structure
- **Moment-by-moment:** Responds to what's happening now, not yesterday
- **Discovery learning:** When athletes misread, learn from what they show you
- **FIO (Figure It Out):** Simple metrics (stroke count + speed) beat complex tracking

### Community College Differences
- **Broad skill range:** Water polo players to D1 transfers
- **Flexibility required:** Jobs, class conflicts, scattered attendance
- **Pure joy:** No scholarships, they're there because they want to be
- **Abbreviated seasons:** Hit the gas, don't be too cautious
- **Different dynamics:** Can still ask for high standards with flexible approach

### Painting the Workout
- **Don't plan ahead:** Respond to who shows up and how they look
- **Each workout standalone:** Today is different from yesterday
- **Multiple components:** Power, strength, flexibility, mechanics all in one session
- **Crescendo awareness:** Know when set is reaching peak, when to pull back
- **Constant communication:** Always coaching, always talking, never silent

### No Metrics Necessary
- **Work hard every day:** That's the metric that matters
- **Plateau fear:** Tracking numbers can create anxiety when they plateau
- **Body adapts:** If doing the work, getting in shape will follow
- **Simple over complex:** Count strokes, compare to speed
- **Trust the process:** 40+ years of experience says this works

---

## The Full Circle

### From Urbanchek to Eddie

**John Urbanchek:**
- Dave swam for John at Long Beach State
- Asked John to be his assistant at USC after John's Michigan retirement
- "Great to have John on the deck as a friend, as a mentor"

**Anthony Eddie:**
- Anthony swam for Dave at Nova
- Dave becomes Anthony's assistant at Orange Coast College
- "Great to be working with Anthony—if he hires me back next year"

### Mentorship Chain

1. **John Urbanchek** mentored → **Dave Salo**
2. **Dave Salo** coached → **Anthony Eddie**
3. **Dave Salo** assists → **Anthony Eddie**

The student becomes the head coach.  
The master becomes the assistant.  
The circle completes.

---

## Conclusion

> "I'm not trying to prove anything. I've had a great career. Now it's just being able to give back as much as I can."

Dave Salo's choice to become an assistant coach represents the ultimate confidence of a veteran coach—no ego to protect, no résumé to build, no need to be in charge. Just the pure love of coaching, the joy of being on deck, and the satisfaction of helping a former athlete succeed in his own coaching career.

**It's not a step down.**  
**It's a step into freedom.**

---

- [[dave-salo-coaching-philosophy]]
- [[dave-salo-innovation-creativity]]
- John Urbanchek mentorship
- Assistant coaching philosophy
- Community college swimming
- Veteran coach transitions
- Coaching without ego
- Detail vs. autonomy in coaching

# Dave Salo on Supplements and Doping in Swimming

**Category:** Sports Ethics, Nutrition, Coaching Philosophy, Anti-Doping  
**Coach:** Dave Salo  
**Context:** USC tenure, international athletes, high-profile cases

## Summary

Dave Salo is one of the most vocal anti-supplement coaches in swimming, despite being associated with several athletes who tested positive during his career. His stance is clear: hard work pays off, supplements are unnecessary, and coaches should actively discourage their use. This article examines his philosophy, experiences, and recommendations for managing supplement culture in competitive swimming.

---

## Core Philosophy

### Salo's Position: "I'm probably the most anti-supplement coach there ever was"

**What he discourages:**
- ANY kind of supplementation
- Creatine
- Glycogen repletion drinks
- Vitamins (even general multivitamins)
- Performance enhancers
- Recovery supplements

**What he promotes:**
- Hard work
- Good, balanced diet
- Time-sensitive eating
- Natural recovery

---

## The Paradox

### High-Profile Positive Tests

Despite being the most anti-supplement coach, Salo has been associated with more positive tests than perhaps any other coach:

**Cases include:**
- **Yulia Efimova** - Russian breaststroker (meldonium)
- **Jessica Hardy** - Tainted vitamin supplement
- **Kicker Vencill** - Proven tainted supplement

**The Irony:** The coach most against supplements has the most supplement-related issues

---

## Why Athletes Take Supplements Anyway

### The "Hot Tub Problem"

Athletes go to international championships:
1. Swim their race
2. Sit in the jacuzzi with other elite athletes
3. Have "the conversation" - "What are you taking?"
4. See successful athletes using supplements
5. Want the same "edge"
6. Start taking things without coach knowledge

### The Creatine Era (Pre-2008)

Salo recounts:
- Everyone was taking creatine
- Athletes convinced it would help phosphocreatine stores
- Didn't matter what Salo told them
- He provided research showing it didn't work
- Athletes took it anyway
- Eventually he got "exhausted trying to tell them"

---

## The Hiding Game

### What Happens When Coaches Take a Stance

**At USC:**
- Once athletes knew Salo's position
- They would hide supplements
- Walk into locker room → quick shuffle into lockers
- Didn't matter the supplement type
- Athletes knew his stance and concealed use

**The Problem:**
- Coaches can't monitor what they can't see
- Athletes take advice from peers over coaches
- International athletes from different regulatory environments
- Varying supplement quality standards across countries

---

## International Supplement Standards

### Quality Control Issues

**Salo's observations:**
- Different countries have different manufacturing standards
- U.S. has higher standards
- Brazil and other countries may have lower grading
- Athletes don't know where supplements are made
- Don't know what's actually in them
- Don't even know WHY they're taking them
- "Just taking it because the guy next to you is taking it"

---

## Specific Cases

### Jessica Hardy - Tainted Supplement
- Took a vitamin
- Tested positive
- PROVEN to be tainted
- Successfully sued the supplement company
- Made significant money from lawsuit
- BUT: Career impact was devastating

### Kicker Vencill - Career Ended
- PROVEN tainted supplement
- Career ended despite proof of innocence
- Shows the brutal reality of strict liability

### Yulia Efimova - Meldonium
- Russian breaststroker
- Second positive test
- Heart-protection concept drug
- All Eastern European athletes taking it
- WADA banned it after widespread use
- "Not a prevalence of legit scientific background"
- Banned because "everyone's doing it so there must be something wrong"

**University Response:**
- USC told Salo to cut ties with Efimova
- He had to end coaching relationship
- Great relationship otherwise
- She trained with him during 2016 Russian trials

---

## The Meldonium Controversy

### What Is It?

- Supposedly protects the heart from exhaustion
- Eastern European athletes widely using it
- Belief: saves the heart during intense training
- NO strong scientific evidence of performance enhancement
- More of a "concept" than proven PED

### WADA's Response

- Saw widespread use
- Assumed it must work
- Banned it
- Created retroactive problems for athletes

**Salo's Frustration:** Banning substances based on use patterns rather than proven performance enhancement

---

## The "Monsterizing" Problem

### Double Standard in Swimming

**When Americans swim fast:**
- "She works hard!"
- "Great technique!"
- "Deserved success!"

**Example: Katie Ledecky**
- Huge leap in performance for 800 free gold
- Nobody accused her of doping
- Response: "This girl works hard, we've seen it, great stroke"

**When internationals swim fast:**
- "Must be on drugs!"
- Immediate suspicion
- Guilty until proven innocent

### Katinka Hosszu Example

- Constantly criticized
- Never tested positive
- Tested frequently worldwide
- One of the hardest workers Salo ever coached
- Exceptional in weight room and pool
- "Driven and amazing athlete"
- "As clean as can be"

**Yet:** Constant accusations based only on exceptional performance

---

## Current Supplement Rules

### FINA/World Aquatics Policy

**Rule implemented:** Coaches cannot coach athletes who:
- Have tested positive
- Are under investigation for positive test
- Have doping-related suspensions

**Salo's View:** Uncertain if this is the best approach

**Alternative:** Keep athlete under coach's wing rather than cut them loose

---

## What Salo Learned

### Evolution of His Approach

1. **Initially:** Tried to educate athletes, provide research
2. **Mid-career:** Got exhausted fighting the culture
3. **Current:** Complete anti-supplement stance

### Post-Hardy/Vencill Changes

**After Jessica Hardy tainted vitamin:**
- Stopped encouraging even general vitamins
- "You just don't know what's happening with those things"
- Complete zero-tolerance for ANY supplements

---

## Salo's Recommendations for Coaches

### What You Can Control:

1. **Make your position clear**
   - Be explicit about anti-supplement stance
   - Explain why
   - Be consistent

2. **Educate on tainted supplements**
   - Show real cases (Hardy, Vencill)
   - Explain strict liability
   - Show career consequences

3. **Promote proper nutrition**
   - Good balanced diet
   - Time-sensitive eating
   - Natural recovery
   - Replenish stores through food

4. **Accept limitations**
   - Athletes will hide supplement use
   - International athletes have different influences
   - You can't monitor 80-100 people 24/7
   - Peer influence is powerful

5. **Stay informed**
   - Know what's being taken
   - Understand the culture
   - Check locker rooms occasionally
   - Ask questions about unknown substances

---

## The Glycogen Repletion Example

### Michael Phelps Story (2004 Olympics)

**Scene:** Phelps age 15, opening care package from home

**Contents:**
- Glycogen repletion drink
- Three big boxes of Mike & Ike candies

**Salo's observation:**  
*"I think it's the Mike and Ikes, not the glycogen repletion drink"*

**Point:** Kids are on every supplement imaginable, but performance comes from training, not pills

---

## The Systemic Problem

### Why It Persists:

1. **Transitory athletes** - Internationals not always at your pool
2. **Peer influence** - Hot tub conversations worldwide
3. **Marketing** - Supplement companies target athletes
4. **Desperation** - Athletes looking for any edge
5. **Coaches promoting it** - Some coaches DO push supplements
6. **Cultural differences** - International norms vary
7. **Lack of education** - Athletes don't understand risks

---

## Practical Takeaways

### For Coaches:

- **Be the anti-supplement voice** even if you're swimming against the tide
- **Show real consequences** - Hardy, Vencill, Efimova cases
- **Promote food-based nutrition** - It works
- **Inspect locker rooms** occasionally - Know what's happening
- **Accept you can't control everything** - But maintain your standards
- **Educate on tainted supplements** - Strict liability is real

### For Athletes:

- **Listen to your coach** - Not the guy in the hot tub
- **Understand strict liability** - Doesn't matter if it was an accident
- **Know what you're taking** - Where it's made, what's in it, why
- **Consider the risk** - Career-ending for a marginal benefit?
- **Trust the process** - Hard work and good food work

---

## Key Quotes

> *"I'm probably the most anti-supplement coach there ever was. I don't encourage my athletes to take any kind of supplement. I don't care what it is."* - Dave Salo

> *"Hard work is what pays off, it's not about supplementation."* - Dave Salo

> *"I'm amazed by the number of coaches who are promoting the use of any kind of supplementation... I've had more athletes test positive over something than anybody else and I'm the one most ardent about hard work."* - Dave Salo

> *"We kind of monsterize some of these kids who inadvertently have taken something and they've lost their careers which is unfortunate."* - Dave Salo

---

## The Bottom Line

**Salo's Message:** 
- Supplements are unnecessary
- They carry massive risk
- Natural nutrition works
- Hard work pays off
- The culture is toxic
- Coaches must fight it
- But accept you can't control everything

**The Irony:** The most anti-supplement coach has had the most supplement problems - proving how pervasive and insidious the culture really is.

---

**Related Topics:** Sports Ethics, Nutrition for Swimmers, Coaching Philosophy, Anti-Doping Education, International Swimming Culture

**Sources:** Brett Hawke Inside With interview series, Dave Dennison corroboration

# Open Water Swimming For Pool Coaches

**Source:** Open Water Swimming - Steven Munatones (270 pages, Human Kinetics, 2011)

## Why Pool Coaches Should Care About Open Water

**The growing market:**
- 3,600+ open water events in 84+ countries (as of 2011, likely 5,000+ by 2025)
- Olympic sport since 2008 (10K marathon swim)
- Triathlon swim legs (Olympic sport since 2000)
- Fastest-growing demographic: Women over 40

**Key insight for coaches:**  
Open water skills ≠ pool skills. Your fastest pool swimmer may struggle in open water. This is a SEPARATE skill set that requires specific training.

---

## Three Categories of Open Water Swimming

### 1. Short-Distance Swims (Under 5K / 3.1 miles)

**Examples:**
- Triathlon swim legs (750m - 1500m)
- 1 mile ocean swims
- 5K charity swims

**Key characteristics:**
- High-intensity, race-pace efforts
- Tactical positioning critical
- Minimal feeding (if any)
- Wetsuit legal in most cases

**Training focus:**
- Speed endurance
- Sighting skills
- Pack swimming tactics
- Starts and turns (water entry/exit)

---

### 2. Middle-Distance Swims (5K - 25K / 3.1 - 15.5 miles)

**Examples:**
- 10K Olympic marathon swim
- 10 mile charity events
- Multi-hour swims

**Key characteristics:**
- Sustained aerobic effort (1.5 - 6 hours)
- In-race feeding required
- Weather/conditions become major factor
- Wetsuit sometimes legal (varies by race)

**Training focus:**
- Aerobic base building
- Feeding practice
- Temperature acclimation
- Mental endurance

---

### 3. Marathon Swims (Over 25K / 15.5 miles)

**Examples:**
- English Channel (21 miles)
- Catalina Channel (20.2 miles)
- Manhattan Island Marathon Swim (28.5 miles)

**Key characteristics:**
- 6-24+ hour swims
- No wetsuit allowed (channel rules)
- Hypothermia risk
- Support crew required

**Training focus:**
- Extreme endurance
- Cold water acclimation
- Mental toughness
- Crew coordination

---

## Open Water vs. Pool: Key Differences

### Environmental Factors

**Water temperature:**
- Pool: 78-82°F (25.5-27.8°C) - controlled
- Open water: 35-85°F (1.7-29.4°C) - variable

**Visibility:**
- Pool: Crystal clear, see bottom
- Open water: 0-50 feet visibility, sometimes zero

**Conditions:**
- Pool: Calm, predictable
- Open water: Currents, waves, chop, tides

**Course:**
- Pool: Black line to follow, walls every 25-50m
- Open water: No guideline, sighting required

### Skill Differences

| Skill | Pool | Open Water |
|-------|------|------------|
| **Breathing** | Every 3-5 strokes, bilateral | Adaptive - may need every stroke to sight |
| **Pacing** | Pace clock, intervals | RPE (rate of perceived exertion), no clock |
| **Navigation** | Black line | Sighting buoys, landmarks, sun position |
| **Starts** | Blocks or wall push | Beach run, pontoon dive, water start |
| **Turns** | Wall flip turn | Buoy rounding (sharp turn, body position change) |
| **Pack dynamics** | Lane discipline | Physical contact, drafting, positioning |

---

## Building Open Water Skills in the Pool

### 1. Sighting Drills

**Why it matters:** Must locate buoys without stopping

**Pool drill: "Water Polo Head-Up Freestyle"**
- 10x100 @ 1:30
- Every 4th stroke, lift head (eyes above water) to see forward
- Minimize body drop (kick harder during sight)
- Goal: Maintain pace within 3-5s of normal 100 time

**Progression:**
- Week 1-2: Sight every 6 strokes
- Week 3-4: Sight every 4 strokes  
- Week 5+: Sight every 2-3 strokes (race simulation)

### 2. Pack Swimming

**Why it matters:** Open water is contact sport - arms, legs, bodies collide

**Pool drill: "Controlled Chaos"**
- 4-6 swimmers per lane
- 4x200 @ 3:00
- All start together (simulates mass start)
- Swim in tight pack (intentional contact OK)
- Rotate positions: front, back, side

**Safety rule:** No intentional hitting, but incidental contact expected

### 3. Bilateral Breathing Under Pressure

**Why it matters:** Waves may force breathing on "wrong" side

**Pool drill: "Forced Bilateral"**
- 8x100 @ 1:30
- Odd 100s: Breathe only RIGHT
- Even 100s: Breathe only LEFT
- Goal: Maintain same pace regardless of breathing side

**Advanced:** Alternate breathing side every 25m

### 4. Buoy Turns

**Why it matters:** Sharp turns around buoys without flip turn

**Pool drill: "Open Turn Sprint"**
- 8x50 @ 1:00
- Sprint to wall
- Open turn (touch wall with hand, pivot 180°, push off)
- Sprint back
- Goal: Minimize time lost on turn vs. flip turn

**Coaching cue:** Keep head down, rotate hips quickly, explosive push-off

### 5. Drafting Practice

**Why it matters:** Swimming in someone's wake saves 10-30% energy

**Pool drill: "Follow the Leader"**
- 3 swimmers per lane
- 10x100 @ 1:30
- Leader swims steady pace
- Swimmers 2 & 3 draft (swim at leader's feet, 1-2 feet back)
- Rotate positions every 2x100

**Coaching points:**
- Drafting swimmer: Stay close but don't touch leader's feet
- Feel the "pull" of the wake
- Adjust stroke rate to match leader without overworking

---

## Freestyle Technique Adaptations for Open Water

### Head Position: Higher Than Pool

**Pool freestyle:** Head neutral, eyes down, waterline at hairline  
**Open water:** Head slightly higher to sight, waterline at eyebrows

**Why:** Need to see forward for navigation, buoys, other swimmers

**Training adaptation:**
- 2-3x per week: Add 500-1000m of head-up freestyle
- Focus: Maintain core stability, avoid dropped hips

### Stroke Rate: Variable vs. Constant

**Pool freestyle:** Consistent stroke rate (e.g., 75-85 SPM)  
**Open water:** Adaptive rate based on conditions

**Examples:**
- **Calm water:** Lower rate, longer glide (efficiency)
- **Choppy water:** Higher rate, shorter strokes (stability)
- **Sprint to buoy:** Maximum rate (positioning)

**Training drill:**
- 12x100 @ 1:30
- Odd 100s: Low rate (60-70 SPM) - long, smooth strokes
- Even 100s: High rate (80-90 SPM) - quick, choppy strokes
- Goal: Maintain similar pace at both rates

### Kick: Power Reserve vs. Constant Propulsion

**Pool freestyle:** Steady 2-beat or 6-beat kick  
**Open water:** Save legs for positioning moves, buoy turns, finish sprint

**Training adaptation:**
- Build kick endurance: 3000m kick per week
- But practice minimal kick in distance sets (save energy)
- Sprint sets with full kick (simulate buoy positioning)

---

## Feeding & Hydration Training

### For Middle-Distance (5K-25K) Swimmers

**In-race feeding:**
- Every 20-30 minutes
- Carbohydrate gels, sports drinks, or both
- Must not stop swimming (treading water = DQ in some races)

**Pool training drill: "Feed While Swimming"**
- 10x200 @ 3:00
- Every 3rd 200: Grab cup from deck during swim (coach places at wall)
- Tread water 5-10s, drink, continue swimming
- Goal: Minimize time lost during feed

**What to practice:**
- Grabbing cup/bottle without stopping
- Drinking while treading (head up)
- Discarding container (designated feed zone)

### For Marathon Swimmers (25K+)

**Feeding strategy:**
- Every 30-45 minutes
- Mix of solid food (banana, energy bars) and liquids
- Handed from kayak or boat via feeding stick

**Pool drill: "Kayak Feed Simulation"**
- Coach stands at pool edge with water bottle on stick
- Swimmer approaches, treads, grabs bottle
- Drinks 4-8 oz, hands back, resumes swimming
- Practice: 5-10 feeds per 5000m swim

**Coaching tip:** Train stomach to tolerate food while swimming. Start with liquids only, progress to semi-solids.

---

## Temperature Acclimation Protocols

### Cold Water Exposure (For 10K+ Open Water)

**Physiological adaptation takes 2-4 weeks**

**Week 1:** 15 min cold showers daily  
**Week 2:** 20 min cold showers + 10 min open water swim  
**Week 3:** 30 min open water swim (60-65°F)  
**Week 4:** 45-60 min open water swim

**Safety rules:**
- Never swim alone in cold water
- Monitor for hypothermia symptoms (confusion, slurred speech)
- Exit water immediately if shivering uncontrollably

### Warm Water Adaptation (For Tropical Events)

**Challenge:** Overheating, not hypothermia

**Protocol:**
- Train indoors with minimal ventilation (if safe)
- Wear extra cap (simulate sun exposure)
- Reduce water intake slightly (train body to tolerate mild dehydration)

**Race day:** Apply sunscreen to shoulders, back of neck. Hydrate aggressively.

---

## Race Day Tactics (Pool Coach Perspective)

### Pre-Race Warm-Up

**Pool meet warm-up:** 1500-2000m, progressive pace  
**Open water warm-up:** 400-800m MAX (conserve energy, already in race environment)

**Warm-up goals:**
1. Test water temperature (adjust expectations)
2. Check visibility (how far can you see?)
3. Feel current/waves (plan pacing)
4. Sight landmarks (know where to aim)

### Start Strategy

**Beach start (shallow water):**
- Run until waist-deep
- Dolphin dive (push off bottom, dive forward) until water too deep
- Begin swimming

**Pontoon/deep water start:**
- Tread water before gun
- Explosive first 10 strokes (get clear of pack)
- Settle into rhythm by 100m

**Coaching cue:** First 200-400m is chaos. Stay calm, find clear water, THEN settle into race pace.

### Drafting Strategy

**Where to draft:**
- **Directly behind swimmer:** Maximum draft (20-30% energy savings) but can't see ahead
- **At swimmer's hip:** Moderate draft (10-15% savings), better visibility

**When to draft:**
- Middle of race (conserve energy)
- When pace feels comfortable but you want to save energy for finish

**When NOT to draft:**
- Last 500-1000m (position for sprint)
- If lead pack is pulling away (stay with them)

### Finish Sprint

**When to start:**
- Short-distance (under 5K): Last 300-500m
- Middle-distance (5K-10K): Last 500-800m
- Marathon (25K+): Last 1000-1500m

**Tactic:**
- Move to outside (clear water, avoid getting boxed in)
- Increase stroke rate by 10-15 SPM
- Full 6-beat kick
- Sight frequently (ensure you're on direct line to finish)

---

## Sample Weekly Training Plan (10K Open Water Prep)

**Base Phase (12 weeks out)**

| Day | Pool Workout | Open Water |
|-----|--------------|------------|
| Mon | 5000m aerobic base (mix IM, kick, pull) | - |
| Tue | 4000m: 20x200 @ 3:00 (build pace) | - |
| Wed | 3000m easy recovery | - |
| Thu | 6000m: 3000 pull, 3000 swim (negative split) | - |
| Fri | REST | - |
| Sat | 3000m technique focus | 3000m open water (easy pace) |
| Sun | - | 5000-8000m open water (race pace) |

**Total:** 21,000m pool + 8,000-11,000m open water = **29,000-32,000m/week**

**Peak Phase (4 weeks out)**

| Day | Pool Workout | Open Water |
|-----|--------------|------------|
| Mon | 6000m: 30x200 @ 2:45 (hold race pace) | - |
| Tue | 4000m: Speed work (50s, 100s, 200s) | - |
| Wed | REST or 2000m easy | - |
| Thu | 5000m: 2x2500 @ race effort | - |
| Fri | 3000m easy + sighting drills | - |
| Sat | 2000m technique | 6000-10,000m open water (race simulation with feeds) |
| Sun | REST | - |

**Total:** 20,000m pool + 6,000-10,000m open water = **26,000-30,000m/week**

**Taper (Race Week)**

| Day | Workout |
|-----|---------|
| Mon | 3000m easy (technique focus) |
| Tue | 2000m: 10x100 @ race pace (short rest) |
| Wed | REST |
| Thu | 1500m easy + open water 1000m (test conditions) |
| Fri | REST |
| Sat | **RACE DAY** - 400m warm-up only |

---

## Cross-References

- [[Open-Water-Race-Strategy]] - Tactical positioning and pack dynamics
- [[Cold-Water-Swimming-Science]] - Physiology of temperature adaptation
- [[Triathlon-Swim-Training]] - Specific prep for triathlon swim legs
- [[Channel-Swimming-Protocols]] - Marathon swim logistics

---

**Coach's Bottom Line:**

> Open water swimming is NOT just "pool swimming outdoors." It requires specific skills (sighting, pack tactics, variable pacing) and specific training (temperature acclimation, feeding practice). If your swimmers compete in open water, dedicate 20-30% of training to open-water-specific drills and swims.

**Key Coaching Principles:**

1. **Train the skills in the pool:** Sighting, bilateral breathing, pack swimming, buoy turns
2. **Train in the environment:** 1-2x per week minimum in actual open water
3. **Acclimate to temperature:** Start 4-6 weeks before race
4. **Practice feeding:** Middle-distance and marathon swimmers MUST train nutrition
5. **Race tactics ≠ pool tactics:** Drafting, positioning, navigating are learned skills, not instincts

**Your job as a coach:** Prepare your swimmers for the SPECIFIC challenges of open water, not just general swimming fitness. Pool speed helps, but open water intelligence wins races.

# World Championship Performance Standards

**Sources:** 2024 Short Course Worlds (Budapest), 2025 Long Course Worlds Results

## For Coaches: Why Study World Championship Data?

World Championships represent the highest level of competitive swimming (alongside Olympics). Analyzing these results reveals:

- **Championship qualifying standards** - What times earn lanes at Worlds?
- **Finals vs. semifinals gaps** - How much faster do swimmers go in finals?
- **Event-specific trends** - Stroke rates, split patterns, relay exchanges
- **Age demographics** - When do swimmers peak?
- **Tactical patterns** - How do world-class swimmers race?

**Bottom line:** These are the benchmarks. If you're coaching elite swimmers, this is what they're chasing.

---

## 2024 Short Course World Championships (Budapest)

**Date:** December 10-15, 2024  
**Venue:** Budapest, Hungary (25m pool)

### Women's 400m Freestyle Analysis

**Qualifying standard (Heat):** 3:57-4:00 range for top 8 finalists

#### Championship Final Qualifiers

| Place | Name | Country | Heat Time | Key Insight |
|-------|------|---------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | McIntosh Summer | CAN | 3:57.55 | Age 18 - teenage dominance |
| 2 | Pallister Lani | AUS | 3:57.97 | Consistent 14-15s splits |
| 3 | Weinstein Claire | USA | 3:58.58 | Age 17 - rising star |
| 4 | Madden Paige | USA | 3:58.83 | Veteran experience (age 26) |
| 5 | Gose Isabel | GER | 3:59.87 | |
| 6 | Harvey Mary-Sophie | CAN | 4:00.04 | |
| 7 | Neale Leah | AUS | 4:01.99 | |
| 8 | Diakova Sofia | NAB | 4:02.03 | Neutral Athlete |

**Performance benchmarks:**

**World-class splits (per 100m):**
- First 100: 56-58s
- 100-200: 1:56-1:58 (1:00-1:02 second 100)
- 200-300: 2:57-3:00 (1:01-1:02 third 100)
- 300-400: 3:57-4:00 (1:00-1:01 final 100)

**Key coaching insight:** Negative splitting is rare. Most swimmers slow 1-2s per 100 after the first 200.

#### World Record Comparison

**WR:** 3:51.30 - Li Bingjie (CHN), Beijing 2022

**Split analysis:**
- 50m: 56.72
- 100m: 1:55.88 (59.16)
- 150m: 2:54.52 (58.64)
- 200m: 3:51.30 (56.78) **INSANE closing speed**

**Coaching takeaway:** Li's WR featured nearly even splits - only 2.5s variance across 200m segments. This is ELITE pacing control.

---

### Women's 100m Backstroke Analysis

**Qualifying standard:** 55.86-57.55 range for top 16 (semifinals)

#### Championship Semifinal Qualifiers

| Place | Name | Country | Time | 50m Split | Second 50 |
|-------|------|---------|------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Smith Regan | USA | 55.86 | 26.78 | 29.08 |
| 2 | Anderson Iona | AUS | 56.25 | 26.95 | 29.30 |
| 3 | Berkoff Katharine | USA | 56.33 | 27.29 | 29.04 |
| 4 | Shkurdai Anastasiya | NAA | 56.50 | 27.70 | 28.80 |
| 5 | Masse Kylie | CAN | 56.58 | 27.23 | 29.35 |
| 6 | Wilm Ingrid | CAN | 56.66 | 27.27 | 29.39 |
| 7 | Weiler Sastre Carmen | ESP | 56.68 | 27.67 | 29.01 |
| 8 | Qian Xinan | CHN | 56.69 | 27.45 | 29.24 |

**Split patterns:**

**First 50m:** 26.5-27.5s (elite range)  
**Second 50m:** 28.8-29.4s (2-2.5s slower than first 50)

**Coaching insight:** Backstroke first 50 should be FAST. Even the best swimmers fade 2+ seconds on the back half. Train explosive underwater kicks and aggressive first-length pace.

#### World Record Comparison

**WR:** 54.27 - Regan Smith (USA), Singapore 2024

**Split:** 26.06 (50m), 28.21 (second 50)

**Key difference:** Smith's WR featured only 2.15s fade. Most championship swims fade 2.5-3s.

**Training application:** World-record backstroke requires sub-27 first 50 AND ability to hold within 2.5s on back half.

---

## World Championship vs. NCAA Standards

### How Do Times Compare?

**Women's 400m Free:**

| Level | Championship Standard |
|-------|----------------------|
| **World Championship Final** | 3:57-4:02 |
| **NCAA Championship Final** | 4:05-4:15 |
| **Gap:** | ~7-13 seconds |

**Women's 100m Back:**

| Level | Championship Standard |
|-------|----------------------|
| **World Championship Final** | 55.5-57.5 |
| **NCAA Championship Final** | 49.5-51.5 (SCY conversion ~57-59 LCM) |
| **Gap:** | Minimal (top NCAA = World-class) |

**Coaching insight:** NCAA swimming (especially women's) is VERY close to international standards. Top NCAA swimmers can compete at Worlds with minimal adjustment.

---

## Age Demographics at World Championships

### Women's 400m Freestyle Finalists (2024 SC Worlds)

| Age Group | Number of Finalists | Percentage |
|-----------|---------------------|------------|
| **16-19** | 3 (McIntosh 18, Weinstein 17, Diakova 18) | 37.5% |
| **20-23** | 3 (Pallister 22, Gose 22, Neale 29) | 37.5% |
| **24-27** | 2 (Madden 26, Harvey 25) | 25% |

**Key finding:** Women's distance freestyle peaks YOUNG (17-23 years old). Coaching implication: Don't wait until athletes are "mature" to push championship training. Elite 400 free swimmers are ready by age 17-18.

### Women's 100m Backstroke Finalists

| Age Group | Number of Finalists | Notes |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|
| **16-19** | 2 | Anderson (19), Hirai (17) |
| **20-24** | 8 | Majority - prime backstroke age |
| **25-29** | 4 | Veterans (Masse 28, Wilm 26, etc.) |
| **30+** | 2 | Gasson (30), Toussaint (30) |

**Key finding:** Backstroke peak is broader (20-26 years old). Longevity is common. Coaching implication: Backstrokers can improve well into their mid-20s.

---

## Reaction Time Analysis

### Starts: What's Fast?

**Women's 400m Free (Heats):**
- **Fastest RT:** 0.65 (Kong Yaqi, CHN)
- **Average RT (Top 8):** 0.73s
- **Slowest RT (Top 8):** 0.80 (Gose, GER)

**Women's 100m Back (Heats):**
- **Fastest RT:** 0.52 (Hirai, JPN)
- **Average RT (Top 8):** 0.58s
- **Slowest RT (Top 8):** 0.64 (de Waard, NED)

**Coaching standards:**

| Event Type | Elite RT | Acceptable RT | Slow RT |
|------------|----------|---------------|---------|
| **Sprint (50-100)** | 0.50-0.60 | 0.60-0.70 | >0.70 |
| **Middle-distance (200-400)** | 0.65-0.75 | 0.75-0.85 | >0.85 |
| **Distance (800-1500)** | 0.70-0.80 | 0.80-0.90 | >0.90 |

**Training application:** Dedicate 5-10 minutes per week to start practice with timing feedback. Reaction time is a trainable skill.

---

## Split Consistency Metrics

### What Separates Finalists from Heats Swimmers?

**400m Freestyle - Split Variance Analysis**

**Top 3 finalists (3:57-3:58):**
- Split variance: ±1.5-2.0s per 100m
- Negative split ability: Rare, but finals may show <1s fade on last 100

**Middle pack (3:59-4:02):**
- Split variance: ±2.0-3.0s per 100m
- Consistent fade: 2-3s slower per 100 after first 200

**Heat swimmers (4:05+):**
- Split variance: ±3.0-5.0s per 100m
- Significant fade: 4-6s slower on final 100

**Coaching drill to improve consistency:**

**"Even Split 400" Set**
- 4x400 @ 5:30
- Goal: Every 100m within ±2s of target pace
- Example: Target 1:00/100 → Every 100 must be 58-62s
- Track deviation; goal is <5s total variance across 400

---

## Tactical Observations (Heat Swimming)

### Strategic Heat Swims

**Not everyone goes full speed in heats:**

**Example: Leah Neale (AUS)**
- Heat time: 4:01.99 (7th place)
- Known capability: Sub-4:00 (based on previous performances)
- **Strategy:** Qualified comfortably for finals, saved energy

**Coaching lesson:** At elite levels, heats are about QUALIFYING, not winning. Teach swimmers to recognize when to push and when to cruise.

**Heat swim tactics:**
1. **Know the qualifying standard** - What place/time guarantees finals?
2. **Swim 97-98% effort** - Fast enough to qualify, not all-out
3. **Save 1-2 seconds** - Mental and physical edge for finals
4. **Conserve emotional energy** - Don't celebrate a heat swim; finals matter

---

## Cross-References

- [[2025-NCAA-Womens-Championship-Analysis]] - Compare NCAA vs. World standards
- [[Olympic-Trials-Qualification-Standards]] - US Olympic qualifying times
- [[Age-Group-Development-Pathways]] - When swimmers peak by event
- [[Relay-Standards-World-Level]] - Championship relay benchmarks

---

**Coach's Bottom Line:**

> World Championships show what's possible at the pinnacle of the sport. Use these standards to set realistic long-term goals for elite swimmers. A high school junior swimming 4:10 in the 400 free has a LONG way to go to reach Worlds (3:57-4:00 required). But an NCAA freshman at 4:02? That's 1-2 years of focused training away from championship finals.

**Key Coaching Principles:**

1. **Qualifying ≠ Medaling** - There's a 4-7 second gap between making finals and winning medals
2. **Consistency wins** - Split variance <2s per 100m separates finalists from heats
3. **Reaction time matters** - 0.1s RT difference = 0.1s final time difference
4. **Age curves vary by event** - Distance peaks young (17-23), backstroke peaks later (20-26)
5. **Heat strategy** - At championship level, qualifying efficiently > heat wins

**Your job:** Show your elite swimmers these standards. Make them tangible goals. Break down the gaps: "You're 4:08 now, finals is 4:00. That's 2 seconds per 100. We can find that in underwaters, turns, and pacing."

# Dave Salo X+Y+Z Set

**Category:** Training, Set Design, Individualization, Fine-Tuning  
**Coach:** Dave Salo  
**Created:** ~2000s (20+ years of use)

## Summary

The X+Y+Z set is a signature Dave Salo training methodology that allows individualized distance, stroke, and equipment choices within a structured interval framework. It's primarily used for fine-tuning during taper phases but is also effective as an in-season training tool. The set provides coaches with insight into what athletes believe they need while maintaining group cohesion on deck.

---

## Set Structure

**Base Intervals:**
- **X** = 1:00
- **Y** = 1:30  
- **Z** = 2:00

**Athlete Choices:**
- Distance (minimum typically 50 yards/meters)
- Stroke
- Equipment (sometimes)

**Effort:** Full speed throughout

**Rounds:** Variable
- Taper/fine-tuning phase: 4-5 rounds
- In-season: can be increased

---

## How It Works

Athletes choose how far they swim on each interval based on what they feel they need:

### Example Progressions:

**Jason Lezak** (sprinter, needs more rest):
- X: 25
- Y: 25
- Z: 25

**Ous Mellouli** (distance swimmer, less rest needed):
- X: 100
- Y: 150-200
- Z: 200

---

## Coaching Benefits

1. **Reveals athlete self-awareness** - Shows what each swimmer thinks they need for recovery vs. intensity
2. **Individualization within structure** - Different training needs accommodated while maintaining group cohesion
3. **Diagnostic tool** - Coaches can observe patterns and adjust training accordingly
4. **Energy on deck** - Athletes send off together, creating "rowdy" group energy even while doing different work
5. **Athlete ownership** - Swimmers take responsibility for their choices

---

## Variations

Dave mentions using this set with creativity throughout the season:
- Varying minimum distances
- Adding equipment requirements
- Adjusting number of rounds
- Modifying intervals based on training phase

---

## Philosophy

This set embodies Salo's belief in:
- **Individualized training** - Recognizing different athletes have different needs
- **Athlete autonomy** - Trusting swimmers to understand their bodies
- **Race pace focus** - Full speed effort, not threshold or aerobic work
- **Fine-tuning mastery** - Precise preparation in critical taper phases

---

## Practical Applications

**For Coaches:**
- Use during taper to let athletes dial in their race prep
- Track what athletes choose - it reveals their race mentality
- Adjust future training based on patterns observed
- Create variations for different points in the season

**For Swimmers:**
- Be honest about what you need, not what others are doing
- Full speed means full speed - don't sandbag
- Use this to learn about your recovery needs
- Experiment during training season, commit during taper

---

## Key Quote

*"Some kids think they need very little rest so they're going to maximize the distance that they go. Some think they need a lot of rest... As a coach you get to find out what they think they need in this critical phase of fine-tuning and then you can adjust your training throughout that period of time."* - Dave Salo

---

**Related Topics:** Race Pace Training, Individualization, Taper Strategies, Sprint Training  
**Source:** Brett Hawke Inside With interview series

# Timing Systems: Brand Comparison Guide

**Sources:** Gen7 Legacy/Serial Timer Guides, Superior Swim Timing Manual, System 6 Manual, Colorado System 5 Manual, Omega Quantum Manual

## For Coaches: Why Know Your Timing System?

Different timing systems have different:
- **Reliability profiles** - Some handle touchpad failures better than others
- **Data outputs** - Not all capture splits or relay exchanges
- **Operator complexity** - Affects meet delays and error rates
- **Integration** - Some play nicer with Meet Manager software

**Bottom line:** Know which system is running your meet, and you'll know what data to expect and what issues might arise.

---

## Major Timing System Manufacturers

### 1. Colorado Time Systems (CTS)

**Market position:** Dominant in USA Swimming age-group and high school markets

**Product lines:**
- **System 5** - Older LCD display model (still widely used)
- **System 6** - Current generation (color touchscreen)
- **System 7 (Gen7)** - Newest platform (modular, networked)

**Strengths:**
- Industry standard in USA Swimming
- Excellent Meet Manager integration (Hy-Tek)
- Extensive dealer/service network in US
- Relay exchange time measurement (built-in)

**Weaknesses:**
- Proprietary cables/connectors (expensive replacements)
- Older systems (5/6) have limited network capability
- Printer setup can be finicky (especially System 6)

**Best for:** Age-group meets, high school championships, college dual meets

---

### 2. Daktronics OmniSport

**Market position:** Strong in high school and multi-sport facilities

**Key models:**
- **OmniSport 2000** - Workhorse console (swimming + diving modes)
- **OmniSport 4000** - Advanced model (better networking)

**Strengths:**
- Built-in printer (no external printer needed)
- Dual swimming/diving modes (saves equipment costs)
- Excellent scoreboard integration (Daktronics makes scoreboards too)
- Battery backup system

**Weaknesses:**
- Less common in pure swimming facilities
- Operator training curve (more complex menu structure)
- Service network smaller than CTS in some regions

**Best for:** Multi-sport high schools, pools with diving programs, facilities with Daktronics scoreboards

---

### 3. Omega (Swatch/Omega)

**Market position:** Olympics, World Championships, elite international events

**Key models:**
- **Quantum Timer** - Flagship timing system
- **Ares 21** - Touchpad system (1/1000th second precision)

**Strengths:**
- Highest precision (0.001s vs. 0.01s for most others)
- FINA-certified for world records
- Ultra-reliable touchpads
- Comprehensive data logging

**Weaknesses:**
- Expensive (typically $50k-$200k+ for full system)
- Overkill for age-group meets
- Limited service network outside elite venues
- Requires specialized operator training

**Best for:** Olympic Trials, World Championships, elite college programs, international meets

---

### 4. Superior Timing Systems

**Market position:** Budget-friendly option for smaller programs

**Strengths:**
- Lower cost than CTS/Daktronics
- Simpler operation (fewer features = fewer errors)
- Good for dual meets and small invitationals

**Weaknesses:**
- Less robust feature set
- Limited Meet Manager integration
- Smaller service/support network
- May not capture relay exchange times

**Best for:** Small club teams, dual meets, training environments

---

## Feature Comparison Table

| Feature | CTS System 6/7 | Daktronics OmniSport | Omega Quantum | Superior |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|---------------|----------|
| **Precision** | 0.01s (hundredths) | 0.01s | 0.001s (thousandths) | 0.01s |
| **Relay Exchanges** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Limited |
| **Split Timing** | Yes (all lanes) | Yes (all lanes) | Yes (all lanes) | Basic |
| **Built-in Printer** | No (System 6), Yes (Gen7) | Yes | Yes | Optional |
| **Meet Manager Sync** | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Basic |
| **Diving Mode** | No | Yes | No | No |
| **Battery Backup** | Optional | Standard | Standard | No |
| **Touchpad Types** | CTS-proprietary | Daktronics-proprietary | Omega-proprietary | Generic |
| **Typical Cost** | $15k-$40k | $20k-$45k | $50k-$200k+ | $8k-$20k |

---

## Gen7 (Colorado System 7) - The Future

**What's new:**
- **Modular design** - Components can be replaced individually
- **Networked** - Full IP-based communication (no more proprietary cables for some components)
- **Touchscreen interface** - Easier operator navigation
- **Cloud integration** - Real-time results publishing
- **Wireless options** - Bluetooth/WiFi for some peripherals

**Coaching impact:**
- Faster results posting (live to web during meet)
- Better split data capture
- Fewer cable-related delays
- Easier operator training = fewer timing errors

**Adoption status (as of 2025):**
- Replacing System 6 at major meets
- Still expensive (slow rollout to smaller facilities)
- Backward compatible with older CTS touchpads/buttons

---

## What Coaches Should Ask Before a Meet

### Questions for Meet Director / Timing Operator

1. **What timing system are you running?**
   - Affects what data you'll get (splits, relay exchanges, etc.)

2. **Is automatic timing (touchpads) used for ALL events?**
   - Or just finals? Or button-only for prelims?

3. **Will split times be captured?**
   - Distance events (200+) should have splits
   - Not all systems/setups capture them

4. **How are results delivered?**
   - Paper printouts only?
   - Uploaded to Meet Manager?
   - Live web results?

5. **What's the backup plan if touchpads fail?**
   - Button times?
   - Manual watches?
   - Spare touchpads available?

6. **Will relay exchange times be provided?**
   - Essential for training relay teams
   - Not all systems capture this

---

## Interpreting Results from Different Systems

### CTS System 6/7 Printout

**Typical format:**
```
Lane 4  Smith, John        SR  TeamName  1:45.23  **
                                                   1:45.28
                                                   1:45.31
```

**What it means:**
- 1:45.23 = Touchpad time (primary)
- ** = Two backup buttons recorded
- 1:45.28, 1:45.31 = Button times

**Relay format:**
```
Lane 3  TeamName A              3:12.45  **
  1) Jones r:+0.65   48.23 (48.23)
  2) Smith r:+0.18   47.89 (1:36.12)
  3) Brown r:+0.22   48.11 (2:24.23)
  4) Davis r:+0.16   48.22 (3:12.45)
```

**Key data:**
- r:+0.XX = Relay exchange time (legal if 0.00 to +0.30)
- Split times in parentheses = Cumulative time

### Omega Quantum Printout

**Precision difference:**
```
Lane 5  Johnson, Sarah     1:45.234  (touchpad)
                           1:45.28   (button A)
                           1:45.31   (button B)
```

**Note the extra digit** - Omega records to 0.001s (but USA Swimming only recognizes 0.01s for records)

### Daktronics OmniSport Printout

**Similar to CTS, but may include:**
- Time-of-day stamp
- Water temperature (if sensor connected)
- Altitude compensation (for high-altitude pools)

---

## Troubleshooting by System Type

### CTS System 6 Common Issues

**Problem:** Printer not working  
**Likely cause:** Wrong printer driver selected in setup  
**Coach action:** Ask operator to check "Setup → Printer" menu

**Problem:** Touchpad not registering  
**Likely cause:** Loose connector at deck plate  
**Coach action:** Notify official; backup buttons still work

### Daktronics OmniSport Common Issues

**Problem:** Scoreboard not updating  
**Likely cause:** Radio channel mismatch  
**Coach action:** Results still valid; operator needs to reset radio settings

**Problem:** Battery warning displayed  
**Likely cause:** Console battery low (but still functional)  
**Coach action:** Results still accurate; backup power active

### Omega Quantum Common Issues

**Problem:** "Pad Not Ready" warning  
**Likely cause:** Arming delay still active (pad touched recently)  
**Coach action:** Wait 0.5s; pad will re-arm automatically

**Problem:** Split times not printing  
**Likely cause:** Split arm function not enabled  
**Coach action:** Ask operator to enable "Split Arm All"

---

## The Future: Fully Wireless Systems

**What's coming:**
- Wireless touchpads (battery-powered, no deck cables)
- Mobile app timing (backup/recreational use)
- AI-powered video timing (computer vision backup)
- Blockchain time verification (tamper-proof records)

**When:**
- Wireless pads: Already in limited use (Omega)
- Mobile apps: Available now (but not USA Swimming sanctioned)
- Video timing: Pilot programs at NCAA level
- Blockchain: 3-5 years out (experimental)

---

## Cross-References

- [[Timing-System-Basics-For-Coaches]] - How timing works
- [[Meet-Equipment-Reference-Guide]] - Daktronics specifics
- [[USA-Swimming-Timing-Rules]] - Official regulations
- [[Relay-Exchange-Measurement]] - How r:+0.XX is calculated

---

**Coach's Bottom Line:**

> Don't obsess over which timing system is "best." They all meet USA Swimming standards. What matters: (1) Is the operator trained? (2) Are touchpads working? (3) Do results match your expectations? If yes to all three, the brand doesn't matter.

**Key Coaching Insight:**
- **CTS** = Industry standard, you'll see it everywhere
- **Daktronics** = Great for dual swimming/diving facilities
- **Omega** = Olympics and elite international (expensive overkill for age-group)
- **Superior** = Budget option for small programs

**Your job:** Know what data your system CAN provide (splits, relay exchanges), and make sure you're getting it.
