## Cross-Cutting Themes

### Academic Foundation

**Present in ALL Areas:**
- PhD background influences everything
- Laboratory work (6 years) = formulaic thinking
- Chart-based training (all from best 100 time)
- Evidence-based methods
- Challenges traditional dogma with science
- Ronald Costill research application

**Application:**
- Race pace training methodology
- Progressive workout design
- Formula-based set construction
- No arbitrary efforts
- Everything purposeful and measurable

---

### Detail Orientation

**Consistent Focus Areas:**

**Posture:**
- Off every wall
- During every glide
- Throughout stroke cycle
- Back flat, streamlined

**Wall Speed:**
- Into walls (last 10m fast)
- Off walls (passive glide, then surge)
- Habit formation
- Race-specific

**The Surge:**
- Feel body move with each stroke
- Speed change sensation
- Not just moving fast, but feeling acceleration
- Proprioception development

**Technique Under Load:**
- Elbow position maintained
- Stroke integrity never compromised
- Race tempo even when tired
- Quality over survival

---

### Progressive Development

**Stroke-by-Stroke Philosophy:**

Appears in:
- Workout structure (1 stroke → 2 → 5 → 25 → 50+)
- Power development (maximize one stroke at a time)
- Resistance work (feel → tempo → speed)
- Set progression (easy → moderate → hard)
- Seasonal training (build to taper)

**Why It Works:**
- Athletes master fundamentals first
- Quality maintained as volume/intensity increases
- Technical integrity never sacrificed
- Confidence builds progressively

---

### Individualization Within Structure

**The Balance:**

**Structure (Non-Negotiable):**
- Race pace or faster
- Detail execution
- Technical components
- Work ethic standards

**Individualization (Variable):**
- X+Y+Z distance choices
- Stroke variations (8 different breaststroke styles)
- Gear selection
- Recovery needs
- Body type accommodations

**Result:**  
Athletes get what they need while maintaining team cohesion and coach's standards

---

### Race-Pace Training Across All Topics

**Universal Application:**

1. **Aerobic Development:** Through race pace, not slow volume
2. **Resistance Work:** Race tempo under load
3. **Stroke Development:** At race pace from early in set
4. **Breaststroke:** Drill work at race pace
5. **Fine-Tuning:** X+Y+Z full speed efforts
6. **Friday Practice:** Everything race pace or faster

**Philosophy:**  
If you're not training at race pace, you're not training specifically for racing

These paradoxes create an environment where Olympic champions are made while athletes discover their own path within a detailed structure.

# Dave Salo: Breaststroke Technique and Coaching Philosophy

**Category:** Breaststroke, Technique, Coaching Philosophy, Stroke Mechanics  
**Coach:** Dave Salo - "The Breaststroke Coach"  
**Athletes Coached:** Rebecca Soni, Jessica Hardy, Amanda Beard, Staciana Stitts, Eric Shanteau, Kosuke Kitajima, Yulia Efimova, and many others

## Summary

Dave Salo is widely considered the greatest breaststroke coach of all time. His success stems from being a former breaststroker himself, giving him an intimate feel for the stroke's timing and nuances. While he recognizes that elite breaststrokers all look different, Salo focuses on key technical components that apply across all swimmers while respecting individual variation based on flexibility, body type, and feel.

---

## Why Breaststroke?

### Personal Experience

> *"The reason I've been successful with breaststroke is because I was a breaststroke swimmer."* - Dave Salo

**Background:**
- Former breaststroker himself
- Made first Junior Nationals in 200 breaststroke
- Long Course Santa Clara International meet (1976-77)
- Entered illegally (didn't have qualifying time)
- Coach warned: "Better make this time or you're going to be fined $100"
- Was sick (cold, stressed)
- Made the time, avoided the fine

**Why It Matters:**
*"I still to this day remember what that feels like, and even though stroke has changed to the extent that it has over the last 30-40 years, I still have that sense of what it's like."*

---

## The Timing Problem

### The Feel Issue

Salo understands something unique to breaststroke:

> *"I tell breaststrokers or coaches - you've all had breaststrokers come into practice and they're complaining that stroke doesn't feel right. It doesn't feel good. 'I don't know what happened, it felt great yesterday.' I remember what that's like because you come in and all of a sudden your stroke doesn't feel right, it feels awkward. Because I experienced that, I know how to get the kids back into the stroke that's right for them because it's a TIMING issue."*

**Key Insight:** Breaststroke is the most timing-dependent stroke, and "feel" can disappear suddenly

---

## Key Technical Components

### 1. The Line at Extension

**Most Important Check:**  
What does the body look like at completion of extension?

**Goal:**  
A perfect line from fingertips through head down to feet

- Fingertips → Head → Feet alignment
- No breaks in the line
- Everything streamlined
- This is the starting point for analysis

---

### 2. Recovery Path

**Salo's Philosophy:**  
"I don't believe in recovering over the surface of the water"

**Correct Recovery:**
- Turn hands and elbows into extension
- Elbows rotate (don't point downward)
- Hands travel in line with elbows inside
- Recover underwater, not over

**What to Avoid:**
- Elbows pointed downward
- Recovering over water surface
- Hands coming out of water
- Creating drag during recovery

**Drag Check:**  
If you're creating drag between fingertips and shoulders during recovery, you must eliminate it

---

### 3. Elbow Position on Outsweep

**Critical Element:**  
Elbow position determines effectiveness

**Path of Hands:**
- Inside the path of elbows
- Elbows stay high/out
- Maintains pressure on water

**Common Error:**  
Elbows dropping, losing leverage

---

### 4. Head Position

**Alignment:**
- In line with back
- In line with shoulders
- NOT forced one way or another
- Shoulders drive movement forward, not head

**What to Avoid:**  
"Head thing" - head-driven stroke mechanics  
Salo doesn't like this, tries to tone it down even in athletes who naturally do it

---

### 5. The Kick

#### Evolution of Technique

**Old School (when Salo was young):**  
"Up, Out, and Together"

**Modern Technique:**  
Straighter path, more direct

#### Knee-Heel Relationship

**Key Points:**
- Relationship between knees and heels matters
- Heels should be outside knees (even if abbreviated)
- Ankle flexibility crucial
- Hip flexibility matters
- Knee position determines effectiveness

---

## The Kitajima Analysis: Mastering "The Line"

### Kosuke Kitajima - The Gold Standard

**Background:**  
Salo coached Kitajima into retirement, got to study him extensively

**Physical Profile:**
- No more than 5'7"
- Maybe 150 pounds
- "Tremendous speed" despite small stature

**What Made Him Special:**  
THE LINE

### Downward Deflection Hierarchy

Salo identified a spectrum from beginner to elite based on how swimmers achieve streamline:

**ELITE (Kitajima):**
- Slight downward deflection of kick as extending
- Almost seamless transition to line
- "Just kind of goes here and then it's lined up"
- Everyone talked about toes finishing together
- Reality: Perfect alignment through entire body

**ADVANCED (Eric Shanteau):**
- Good at achieving the line
- "Little bit of a jolt into that positioning"
- Still very effective, minor disruption

**JUNIOR NATIONAL:**
- Downward deflection
- Then very dramatic lift into line
- Noticeable break in flow

**BEGINNER:**
- Down, down, down
- Maybe they come up to line
- Constant deflection
- Never achieves smooth alignment

### The Kitajima Secret

**Heel Speed + Hand Speed:**  
Not just about moving fast

**REAL SECRET:**  
Getting alignment from feet through legs seamlessly

**Timing:**  
Shoulders through head into recovery - everything lined up perfectly

---

## Individual Variation

### The Rebecca Soni Story

**Early Workout Crisis:**  
Long course, first year together, Salo doing race-pace drill work

**Scene:**
- Rebecca stops 15 meters from wall
- In tears
- Best swimmer on team
- Looks at Salo: *"I hate your drills"*
- Salo panics: "Oh my god, my best swimmer's gonna quit"

**Salo's Response:**

> *"Rebecca, I understand your stroke and I'm not going to have you swim like Amanda or Jessica or Staciana. I'm going to swim you YOUR stroke and I understand YOUR stroke. I'll get better at understanding your stroke. There's some key things to your stroke that I think we can enhance versus take away from. I'm not going to change your stroke. I know breaststroke and you're just gonna have to trust me."*

**Result:** Calmed her down, got through first year, went on to Olympic gold and WR

---

### The "Hummingbird of Breaststroke"

**Rebecca Soni's Unique Style:**

**What It Looked Like:**
- Half scull on pull
- Really brief, abbreviated kick
- Seemed like it shouldn't work

**Someone at a clinic:**  
"The hummingbird of breaststroke swimming - it doesn't make any sense"

**Why It Worked:**
- Elbow position was correct
- Heels outside knees despite abbreviated kick
- Positioning was right even if abbreviated
- Timing was perfect for her body
- Worked with her flexibility/body type

---

### Proud Moment: Three Different Strokes, All Elite

**Short Course World Championships:**  
Salo's breaststrokers go 1-2-3 in 50m breast:

1. Yulia Efimova (Russia)
2. Jessica Hardy (USA)
3. Rebecca Soni (USA)

**Why It Mattered:**  
"Every one of them is very different. [They all had] different stroke mechanics [but] be that successful - was kind of a proud moment."

**Lesson:** Multiple paths to excellence in breaststroke

---

## Understanding Individual Differences

### Variables That Matter:

1. **Shoulder flexibility**
2. **Hip flexibility**
3. **Ankle flexibility**
4. **Knee structure**
5. **Body proportions**
6. **Natural timing sense**
7. **Feel for the water**

### Salo's Approach:

**Constant:**  
Look for key components (line, recovery, elbow position, etc.)

**Variable:**  
Take into account individual differences

**Balance:**  
Respect what makes each swimmer unique while enforcing fundamental principles

---

## Race Finals Observation

### Eight Different Strokes

> *"If you watch the finals of the Olympics, eight swimmers, you'll have eight different looks to what this stroke looks like."*

**Coaching Implication:**  
You must:
- Look at key components
- Accept variation
- Not force one "perfect" model
- Understand what each athlete can do with their body

---

## The Nuance Factor

**Why Breaststroke Is Different:**

1. **Most complex stroke** - Timing-dependent
2. **Most variable** - Eight finalists, eight different strokes
3. **Most feel-dependent** - Can lose it overnight
4. **Most flexibility-dependent** - Ankles, knees, hips, shoulders all matter
5. **Most individually specific** - Must respect body types

**Salo's Advantage:**  
Having been a breaststroker, he can feel what they're feeling and guide them back

---

## Drill Work Philosophy

### Race Pace Drills

**Salo's Method:**
- Lot of drill-type work at race pace
- Not slow, technical drilling
- Full intensity with technical focus

**Why It Works:**
- Develops power while refining technique
- Simulates race conditions
- Builds stroke-specific strength
- Maintains timing under pressure

**Caution:**  
This can be emotionally intense (see Rebecca Soni story)  
Requires trust between coach and athlete

---

## Coaching Different Athletes

### The Salo Roster:

**Different Styles, Same Excellence:**

- **Rebecca Soni** - Abbreviated, "hummingbird" style → WR holder, Olympic gold
- **Jessica Hardy** - Different pull/kick timing → Multiple world championships
- **Amanda Beard** - Classic technique → Olympic medals
- **Staciana Stitts** - Her own variation → National team
- **Kosuke Kitajima** - Perfect alignment master → Olympic gold (2x)
- **Eric Shanteau** - Advanced line achievement → World championship medalist
- **Yulia Efimova** - Powerful Russian style → World records

**Common Thread:**  
Salo adapted to each while maintaining key principles

---

## Key Principles Summary

### Non-Negotiables:
1. Line at extension (fingertips to feet)
2. Recovery underwater (elbows rotate, don't point down)
3. Elbow position on outsweep (high and wide)
4. Head alignment (in line with back/shoulders)
5. Heel-knee relationship (heels outside knees)

### Flexible:
1. Amplitude of pull
2. Amplitude of kick
3. Stroke rate
4. Hand/foot speed ratios
5. Surface vs. underwater emphasis
6. Recovery width/pattern
7. Breathing pattern

---

## Practical Applications

### For Coaches:

**DO:**
- Study key components across all elite breaststrokers
- Recognize individual variation is normal
- Feel what your swimmers are feeling (if possible)
- Understand timing issues destroy feel overnight
- Have fixes for when stroke "doesn't feel right"
- Respect athlete's natural stroke while improving it

**DON'T:**
- Force one "perfect" model on everyone
- Ignore flexibility limitations
- Change a stroke drastically if it's working
- Overlook the importance of "feel"
- Neglect the line at extension

### For Swimmers:

**Focus On:**
- Perfect line at extension every stroke
- Elbow position (high and wide on pull)
- Recovery path (underwater, elbows rotating)
- Heel-knee relationship on kick
- Timing (the most critical element)

**Trust:**
- Your coach when stroke feels "off"
- The process even when drills feel wrong
- Your individual style within technical framework

---

## The Other Strokes

**Salo's Perspective:**

> *"The other strokes I think are pretty simple compared to breaststroke."*

**Why Breaststroke Is Special:**
- Most complex timing
- Most variable technique
- Most dependent on feel
- Hardest to diagnose when wrong
- Requires intimate understanding

**Having been a breaststroker:**  
Gives Salo the experiential foundation other coaches lack

---

## Key Quotes

> *"I still to this day remember what that feels like."* - On swimming breaststroke himself

> *"I know breaststroke and you're just gonna have to trust me."* - To Rebecca Soni

> *"You've got to understand the nuance of the stroke that it can be so different."* - On individual variation

> *"It's a TIMING issue."* - On why breaststroke feel disappears

> *"Look how he gets this alignment."* - On Kitajima's mastery

---

## Bottom Line

**Dave Salo's Breaststroke Mastery = Experience + Analysis + Respect**

1. **Experience:** Former breaststroker, knows the feel
2. **Analysis:** Studied the best (Kitajima, Soni, Hardy, etc.)
3. **Respect:** Honors individual variation while teaching fundamentals

**Result:** The greatest breaststroke coach of all time, producing Olympic champions with eight different stroke styles

---

**Related Topics:** Stroke Technique, Individual Coaching, Timing and Feel, Olympic Training, Flexibility and Swimming

**Sources:** Brett Hawke Inside With interview series, decades of coaching Olympic/World champions in breaststroke

# Dave Salo: Friday Speed Practice Protocol

**Category:** Speed Training, Resistance Work, Practice Design, Weekly Programming  
**Coach:** Dave Salo  
**Venue:** Irvine Novaquatics  
**Day:** Friday (speed emphasis)

## Summary

Dave Salo's Friday practices at Irvine Novaquatics represent his race-pace training philosophy in action. The session progresses from stroke development with paddles and fins, through various speed drills emphasizing posture and surge, to resistance work with parachutes, culminating in race-pace application. Every element builds toward maximum effort strokes at race tempo, with detail-oriented focus on technique under load.

---

## Practice Philosophy

### Typical Friday Structure

**Total Time:** 90 minutes to 1:45  
**Warm-up:** Minimal (heart rate stays low - "bunch of pansies")  
**Main Work:** Progressive power development → resistance → race pace  
**Cool-down:** Active recovery

### Weekly Pattern

**Fridays:** Resistance work (parachutes)  
**Tuesdays/Thursdays:** Wet cords and other resistance  
**All Days:** Race pace or faster emphasis

---

## Phase 1: Stroke Development

### Goal: Maximize Power Per Stroke

**Progressive Build:**
1. One stroke at a time
2. Two strokes
3. Five strokes
4. Whole length (25m/y)
5. 50+ meters

---

## Set 1: Knuckle Paddles + Fins

### 75s - Knuckle Paddle Work

**Equipment:** Knuckle paddles + fins  
**Focus:** THREE FAST STROKES per 25

**Purpose:**
- Grab paddle with knuckles (not palm)
- Moves pressure point back into mid-arm
- Forces elbows to stay up/high
- Feel the water differently

**Salo's Coaching Cue:**  
"Surge" - feel your body move with speed

**Technical Elements:**
- Posture on side kick
- Side kick surging on pull
- Grab that water
- Feel body move with speed
- "If they find that, then they're finding their stroke"

---

## Set 2: Regular Paddles

### 50s - Full Paddles + Fins

**Focus:** FOUR STROKES coming off wall

**Contrast Work:**
- Just came from knuckle paddles
- Now open to full paddle
- Feel the difference
- More power available

**Technical Emphasis:**
- Posture coming off wall
- Speed coming off wall
- Passive speed maximized
- Then surge into four strokes

**The Surge:**  
"Body should surge with a stroke. When you're doing minimal stroke count like three strokes or four strokes, you should feel that change in speed. Then you settle down into slower speed or less effort."

---

## Set 3: Full Speed 25s

### Regular Paddles + Fins

**Focus:** FULL SPEED, one breath

**Breathing Control:**
- Pick a spot to breathe
- Only one breath
- Same spot every rep
- Not random breathing

**Why It Matters:**
*"I think it's important to teach sprinters to pick their spot. If they're not going to hold their breath for a whole 50, then they should learn to pick a spot and take that spot as their breathing point rather than just random breathing. There's some control in this element."*

---

## Phase 2: Speed Into/Off Walls

### Rotation Kick + Breakout Drill

**First Rounds (2×25s):**

**Part 1:**
- Push off on back
- Glide to line 4 on surface
- Scrunch scull to wall
- Reverse turn
- Push off

**Part 2:**
- Three underwater pulldowns (fast flutter kick)
- Break out into flip turn
- 360° rotation

**Part 3:**
- Superman kick at flags (stop, lower head, 3-count)
- Fast swim into wall

**Later Rounds (2×25s):**
- Push off back, glide to line 4
- Fast kick to line 5
- Fast swim to wall
- **5-second fast kick against wall**
- **Fast flip turn**

### Purpose: Wall Speed Habit

**Into Wall:**
- Fast kick last 10m
- Good posture
- Maintain velocity approaching

**Off Wall:**
- Passive glide maximized
- Reduce resistance/drag
- Posture perfect
- Then power into strokes

---

## Phase 3: Distance Per Stroke (DPS)

### One Stroke Per Black Line

**Setup:**
- Push and glide to line 3
- No stroke until head reaches line 3
- Then ONE powerful stroke per black line
- Head reaches next line = next stroke

**Equipment:**
- 75s: Knuckle paddles
- 50s: Regular paddles
- 25s: Full speed, one breath

**Gears (By Time):**
- 3rd gear
- 4th gear
- 5th gear
- Chart shows targets based on best 100 time

**Coaching Points:**
- Avoid catch-up drill
- Every stroke = full power
- Long, powerful strokes
- Strong kick
- Good posture
- Grab the water effectively

---

## Phase 4: Resistance Work (Parachutes)

### The Main Event: 4 Rounds

**Structure Per Round:**
- 100 (max resistance)
- 4×25 (max resistance)
- 75 (various intensities)
- 25 + fins (active recovery)

---

### 100s - Maximum Resistance

**Parachute:** Fully closed  
**Purpose:** Feel stroke, posture, mechanics

**NOT About:**
- Going fast
- Time
- Speed

**IS About:**
- Accentuating stroke feel
- Maximizing proprioception
- Posture awareness
- Using resistance to enhance feeling

**Coaching:**
*"I'm just using the resistance to really accentuate the feel for the stroke. So it's mostly posture and stroke feel."*

---

### 4×25 - Maximum Resistance, Race Tempo

**THE KEY SET:**  
Salo's "mantra the last few years"

**Protocol:**
- Parachute fully closed (max resistance)
- Only FOUR STROKES per 25
- Somewhere within the 25 (athlete chooses where)
- **RACE TEMPO** required
- Maximum effort

**The Mantra:**

> *"My mantra the last few years is trying to get race tempo when loaded. I think that's the key to that next development."*

### The Problem This Solves

**Common Issue:**
- Athletes put load on
- Slow things down
- Compromise stroke to manage resistance
- Elbows drop
- Stroke integrity lost

**Salo's Goal:**
*"The focus is to get the tempo up - race tempo - without compromising the stroke. That's more specific training than letting the elbows drop and stuff like that."*

**Coaching Cue (Mid-Set):**
*"When you turn it on, race tempo! Gotta dig into the corners. Gotta get here [high elbow], not here [low elbow]. And when you go to light load on that freestyle, you're still on that right side dropped in the elbow. You gotta still hold it even on the casual part. Hold that elbow position."*

---

### 4th × 25 - Minimum Resistance

**Parachute:** Fully open  
**Purpose:** Accommodating resistance

**Protocol:**
- Whole 25 at race tempo
- Get across pool as quickly as possible
- Feel the difference from max resistance
- Still maintaining stroke integrity

---

### 75s - Progressive Max Effort

**First 3 Rounds:**
- Max effort on ANY TWO of the 25s (athlete choice)
- Other 25 = controlled

**Last Round:**
- ALL THREE 25s max effort
- Whole 75 maxed out

**Purpose:**
- Build to full intensity
- Choice within structure
- Progressive overload

---

### Plus 25 - Active Recovery

**Equipment:** Fins  
**Effort:** Easy  
**Purpose:** Active recovery between rounds

**Salo:** *"Oh yeah, we don't have to work fast."*

---

## Parachute Equipment Details

### Why Singapore Parachutes?

**1. Customization**
- Can brand with Nova logo
- Team identity

**2. Durability**
*"We've had others in the past that just don't hold up very well. They use a lot of metal in the framing and then they rot out."*

**3. Adjustability**
- Can open or close
- Variable resistance
- Though "you can't control it that well"

### Two Settings Used

**Fully Closed:**
- Maximum resistance
- 100s (feel work)
- First 3×25 (race tempo under load)

**Fully Open:**
- Minimum resistance
- 4th×25 (race tempo with accommodation)
- 75s (speed application)

---

## Coaching Throughout Session

### Constant Communication

**Salo's Presence:**
- Always talking
- Giving feedback
- Reminding of goals
- Correcting technique
- Encouraging
- Challenging

### Key Phrases Heard:

**"Race tempo!"**  
**"Grab that water!"**  
**"Feel your stroke!"**  
**"Good posture!"**  
**"Maximum effort!"**  
**"Dig into the corners!"** (high elbow)  
**"Hold that elbow position!"**  
**"Fast! Fast! Fast!"** (in multiple languages)

---

## The "Nightmare" Philosophy Applied

### Creating Stress

**By This Point in Practice:**
1. **Physically fatigued** - Resistance work is cumulative
2. **Mentally challenged** - Constant variety, detail demands
3. **Technically stressed** - Must maintain integrity under load
4. **Psychologically pushed** - "Where dreams become nightmares"

### The Race Simulation

**What Late-Race Feels Like:**
- Fatigued from cords/parachutes
- Still must execute perfectly
- Tempo can't drop
- Stroke can't break down
- Must be your best when feeling worst

**Result:**  
When race gets hard (and it will), athletes have been there before under even worse conditions

---

## Integration with Overall Training

### How Friday Fits

**Not Random:**
- Part of weekly pattern
- Tuesday/Thursday = different resistance (wet cords)
- Friday = parachute emphasis
- All days = race pace or faster

**Progressive Through Week:**
- Different resistance types
- Different speeds
- Different technical focuses
- All building same capacities

---

## Athlete Responses

### What They Experience:

**Early Set:**
- Feeling powerful
- Building confidence
- Technical focus

**Mid Set:**
- Resistance increasing
- Fatigue building
- Must maintain quality

**Late Set:**
- Maximum stress
- Finding reserves
- Proving capability

**Post-Practice:**
- Accomplished
- Faster
- More confident under pressure

---

## Practical Applications

### For Coaches:

**DO:**
- Progress from stroke development to race pace
- Use resistance to enhance feel first, then build tempo
- Demand race tempo under load
- Don't let athletes slow down with resistance
- Use equipment progressively (knuckle → regular paddles)
- Emphasize walls (speed into/off)
- Build in packages (3-4 rounds, not 7-8)

**DON'T:**
- Let elbows drop under load
- Allow tempo to slow with resistance
- Skip the feeling/posture work
- Forget the surge emphasis
- Overload with too many rounds

### For Swimmers:

**Focus On:**
- Feeling the surge every stroke
- Maintaining posture under load
- Race tempo even when tired
- Elbow position (dig into corners)
- Speed into walls (last 10m)
- Speed off walls (passive, then surge)
- Four strokes max effort when loaded

---

## Sample Friday Practice

### Complete Breakdown

**WARM-UP:** (Minimal - not detailed)

**SET 1: STROKE DEVELOPMENT**
- 3×75 knuckle paddles + fins (3 fast strokes per 25)
- 3×50 regular paddles + fins (4 strokes off wall)
- 3×25 regular paddles + fins (full speed, one breath, pick spot)

**SET 2: WALL SPEED**
- 2×25 rotation kick + breakout
- 2×25 push/glide + fast kick + fast swim + 5s kick wall + fast turn

**SET 3: DPS + GEARS**
- 3 rounds of 3×25 (one stroke per line, 3rd/4th/5th gear by time)
- Using knuckle paddles → regular paddles → no equipment

**SET 4: PARACHUTE RESISTANCE**  
4 rounds of:
- 100 max resistance (feel stroke)
- 4×25 max resistance (4 strokes race tempo)
- 75 progressive max effort
- 25 + fins (active recovery)

**COOL-DOWN:** Active recovery

**Total Time:** ~90-105 minutes  
**Volume:** Not tracked ("I don't track volume")  
**Focus:** Maximum effort strokes at race pace

---

## Key Takeaways

**1. Progressive Build**
- Stroke by stroke
- One element to next
- Power → Speed → Resistance → Race Pace

**2. Race Tempo Under Load**
- The mantra
- The key to development
- Cannot compromise

**3. Detail Orientation**
- Posture
- Elbow position
- Surge feeling
- Wall speed
- Every element matters

**4. Resistance as Tool**
- Enhance feeling (max resistance, slow)
- Build power (max resistance, race tempo)
- Apply speed (min resistance, race tempo)

**5. Creating Stress**
- Physical (fatigue)
- Mental (complexity)
- Technical (integrity under load)
- Psychological (nightmare scenario)

---

## Bottom Line

**Friday at Nova = Race Pace Training in Action**

Every element serves the goal: Maximum effort strokes at race tempo with perfect technique, even under maximum fatigue and resistance. This is how Dave Salo produces Olympic champions - not through volume, but through quality execution under stress.

**The Result:** Athletes who can maintain stroke integrity, race tempo, and mental toughness when the race gets hard, because practice was harder.

---

**Related Topics:** Race Pace Training, Resistance Training, Speed Development, Stroke Mechanics, Progressive Training

**Sources:** Filmed Friday practice at Irvine Novaquatics with SwimSwam/Brett Hawke, Dave Salo interviews

# Relay Timing and Pad Delay: Critical Details for Coaches

**Source:** Superior Swim Timing Manual (2019)  
**Category:** Meet Operations / Relays  
**For:** Coaches training relay teams and managing relay exchanges

---

## Why Pad Delay Matters for Relays

The single most important timing setting for relays is **pad delay** - and most coaches don't know it exists.

**What it is:** The time period after a touchpad is hit when it's temporarily disabled and won't register new touches.

**Why it exists:** To prevent relay swimmers from accidentally triggering the pad as they exit the pool after their leg.

**Standard setting: 17 seconds**

---

## How Pad Delay Works

### The Sequence

1. **Incoming swimmer touches pad** → Timer records split/finish time
2. **Pad delay activates immediately** → Pad is "dead" for 17 seconds
3. **Swimmer exits pool** → Any touches during this window are ignored
4. **Pad delay expires** → Pad becomes "live" again for next swimmer
5. **Next swimmer touches** → Timer records their split/finish

### Visual Timeline

```
Touch #1 → [---- 17 second PAD DELAY ----] → Touch #2 can register
            ↑                                  ↑
         Swimmer 1 finishes              Swimmer 2 can touch
         Swimmer 1 exits pool            without false trigger
```

---

## Why 17 Seconds?

**The calculation:**
- Average swimmer takes 5-10 seconds to exit pool after touching
- Need buffer for slower swimmers or difficult exits
- Must allow enough time but not so much that next swimmer is delayed

**17 seconds is the sweet spot** used by most timing systems as default.

### When Pad Delay Needs Adjustment

**Increase to 20-25 seconds when:**
- Young age group swimmers (slower exits)
- Swimmers with disabilities
- Shallow warm-up pool (harder to exit)
- Bulkhead configuration makes exit difficult

**Decrease to 12-15 seconds when:**
- Elite swimmers with quick exits
- Easy exit configuration (stairs, wide gutters)
- Fast-moving relay where next swimmer arriving quickly

**Coach tip:** Timing operators can adjust pad delay DURING a race if needed. If your swimmer is struggling to exit, ask timing to extend the delay before the next leg starts.

---

## Common Relay Timing Problems

### Problem 1: "Early Touch" Before Pad Delay Expires

**What happens:**
- Incoming swimmer touches pad
- Outgoing swimmer HASN'T exited yet
- Pad delay still active (less than 17 seconds elapsed)
- Touch doesn't register

**Result:** System doesn't record the split time

**Prevention:**
- ✅ Train swimmers on quick, clean exits
- ✅ Practice relay exchanges with timing (use pace clock for 17-second drills)
- ✅ Position next swimmer farther back on blocks if necessary

### Problem 2: Swimmer Exits Too Slowly, Triggers Pad Multiple Times

**What happens:**
- Swimmer touches to finish their leg
- Takes too long to exit (>17 seconds)
- Pad delay expires while they're still touching wall
- Accidental touch registers as false finish

**Result:** Invalid split time recorded, needs manual correction

**Prevention:**
- ✅ Train FAST EXITS as part of relay practice
- ✅ Use "touch and GO" drills
- ✅ Ask timing operator to increase pad delay if needed

### Problem 3: Relay Exchange Happens During Pad Delay

**What happens:**
- Incoming swimmer touches pad
- Outgoing swimmer dives before pad delay expires
- Outgoing swimmer touches wall to turn or finish
- Touch doesn't register because pad still disabled

**Why this is bad:**
- If it's the final leg, finish time may not record
- If it's a turn, lap count will be wrong
- System may require manual correction

**Prevention:**
- ✅ Time relay exchanges so outgoing swimmer doesn't arrive until pad is live
- ✅ Practice exchanges with pace clock (17-second marker)
- ✅ Timing operator can use "ARM" button to force pad live if needed

---

## Training Relay Exits: The 17-Second Drill

### Setup

1. Swimmer 1 touches wall (simulates finish)
2. Coach starts stopwatch
3. Swimmer 1 exits pool as fast as possible
4. Swimmer 2 waits on blocks
5. At 17 seconds, coach signals
6. Swimmer 2 dives and swims

### Goal

- Swimmer 1 should be OUT of pool before 17-second mark
- Swimmer 2 should be READY to dive at 17-second mark
- Builds timing awareness for both swimmers

### Common Mistakes to Fix

❌ **Swimmer exits too slowly** → Train explosive exit, use arms to push up
❌ **Swimmer exits wrong direction** → Exit opposite side from next swimmer's lane
❌ **Swimmer hangs on wall** → "Touch and GO" mindset
❌ **Next swimmer dives too early** → Wait for clear visual that pad is ready

---

## Manual Override: ARM and F ARM Buttons

Timing operators have emergency controls to override pad delay:

### ARM Button
**What it does:** Forces pad to become "live" immediately, ignoring pad delay
**When used:**
- Incoming swimmer exited quickly and cleanly
- Next swimmer is ready to go
- Don't want to wait full 17 seconds
- Speeds up meet

**Coach scenario:** Your relay swimmer exits in 8 seconds. Timing operator can ARM the pad immediately so next swimmer can touch without waiting another 9 seconds.

### F ARM Button
**What it does:** Forces the NEXT touch to be the finish for that lane
**When used:**
- Lap count is wrong
- Pad delay caused system to miss expected touch
- Emergency correction needed

**Coach scenario:** Your swimmer's final touch didn't register because they touched during pad delay. Operator uses F ARM to make the next touch (even if lap count is off) finish the race.

---

## Relay Rules vs. Timing System Reality

### USA Swimming Rules

**Rule 101.7 - Relay Takeoffs:**
- Outgoing swimmer's feet must leave block BEFORE incoming swimmer touches wall
- Early takeoff = disqualification
- Timing pads do NOT enforce this rule automatically

**Important:** Pad delay has NOTHING to do with legal relay exchanges. Pad delay is about timing equipment, not rule enforcement.

### What Timing Systems CAN'T Do

❌ Detect early takeoffs (that's the referee's job)  
❌ Measure reaction time on relay exchanges  
❌ Automatically DQ illegal relays  

### What Timing Systems CAN Do

✅ Record accurate split times when pad is functional  
✅ Provide backup times via buttons if pad fails  
✅ Display splits on scoreboard in real-time  
✅ Send relay data to meet management software  

---

## Backup Button Timing for Relays

### When Backup Buttons Activate

**Key fact:** Backup buttons become active AFTER the initial pad delay expires (not just on final lap).

**Timeline:**
1. Race starts
2. Pad delay period elapses (~17 seconds)
3. Backup buttons now active for entire race
4. Operator can press button for ANY split, not just finish

**Why this matters for relays:**
- If pad fails during a relay leg, operator can hit backup button
- Backup time can be used as official split time
- Multiple backup operators can cover different legs

### Backup Button Strategy for Big Meets

**Best practice:**
- One backup button operator per 2-3 lanes
- Focus on watching incoming swimmers' touches
- Be ready to hit button if pad doesn't register
- Note any delayed exits that might cause timing issues

---

## Coaching the Perfect Relay Exit

### The 5-Second Exit

Elite relay swimmers should exit in **5 seconds or less** after touching.

**Technique breakdown:**
1. **Touch:** Full hand contact, don't pull away immediately (ensures pad registers)
2. **Pull:** Use both arms to pull body toward wall (builds momentum)
3. **Push:** Explosive push UP and OUT of water (core + arms)
4. **Rotate:** Turn body away from next swimmer's lane (clears space)
5. **Exit:** Fastest path to deck, don't linger in gutter

### Common Exit Mistakes

| Mistake | Fix |
|---------|-----|
| Gentle tap, quick pullaway | Full palm press, wait for light touch confirmation |
| Lazy climb out | Explosive push-up from wall |
| Exit on same side as next swimmer | Exit opposite side, clear the lane |
| Hang on gutter/lane line | Touch wall, immediately push away |
| No sense of urgency | Practice with timer, build exit speed awareness |

### Practice Drill: Exit + Exchange

**Setup:** Full relay, but focus on exit timing
1. Swimmer 1 finishes their leg, exits ASAP
2. Coach times exit (should be <8 seconds)
3. Swimmer 2 waits until timing confirms pad is live
4. Swimmer 2 dives
5. Repeat for all relay legs

**Goal:** Build muscle memory for fast exits + awareness of pad timing

---

## Meet Day: What to Tell Your Relay Teams

### Pre-Race Briefing

✅ "Touch the pad with full hand pressure - don't just tap it"  
✅ "Exit the pool in under 10 seconds - fast and explosive"  
✅ "Exit away from the next swimmer's lane"  
✅ "Next swimmer: don't dive until you see their head clear the water"  
✅ "If something feels wrong, keep swimming - timing will sort it out"  

### What NOT to Say

❌ "Just touch lightly and get out" → Touch must register on pad  
❌ "Dive as soon as they touch" → Pad delay makes this impossible  
❌ "If pad doesn't light up, do it again" → Keep swimming, don't re-touch  

---

## Troubleshooting Relay Timing Issues

### "My swimmer touched but time didn't show"

**Possible causes:**
1. Pad delay was still active (less than 17 sec since previous touch)
2. Touch was too light to register
3. Pad malfunction

**What to do:**
- Alert timing operator immediately
- Note the clock time when touch occurred
- Ask about backup button time
- Meet referee determines official time

### "Wrong lap count showing"

**Possible causes:**
1. False touch during pad delay
2. Swimmer touched twice (once during delay, once after)
3. Equipment malfunction

**What to do:**
- Timing operator can manually adjust lap count
- "Touch +/-" buttons can add/remove touches
- F ARM button can force next touch to be finish

### "Split times not showing on scoreboard"

**Possible causes:**
1. Scoreboard not configured to display splits
2. Network delay between timing system and scoreboard
3. Meet management software not processing splits in real-time

**What to do:**
- Usually not a timing error, just display delay
- Times are still being recorded
- Results will appear in official meet results

---

## Key Takeaways

1. **Pad delay = 17 seconds (default)** - swimmers must exit in less than this time
2. **Train fast exits** - 5-8 seconds is ideal, under 10 is minimum
3. **Touch with purpose** - full hand contact ensures pad registers
4. **Exit opposite side** - clear space for next swimmer
5. **Timing operators can adjust** - pad delay can be changed during race if needed
6. **Backup buttons activate early** - not just on final lap, but after first pad delay
7. **ARM button speeds things up** - operator can force pad live before 17 seconds
8. **Pad delay ≠ relay rules** - timing system doesn't enforce early takeoff rules

---

## Practice Integration

### Relay Practice Must Include Exit Training

**DON'T just practice exchanges. Practice:**
- ✅ Full-speed touch with immediate exit
- ✅ Timed exits (coach with stopwatch)
- ✅ Realistic exchange spacing (wait for pad delay to expire)
- ✅ Communication between swimmers
- ✅ Backup plan if pad doesn't register

**Sample set:**
```
4 x Relay Exchanges (each swimmer goes twice)
- Focus: FAST EXITS
- Coach times exit for each swimmer
- Goal: All exits under 10 seconds
- Next swimmer waits 17 seconds before diving
- Track improvement over multiple practices
```

---

## Related Wiki Pages

- [[Swim Meet Timing Systems Guide]] - Overall timing operations
- [[USA Swimming Relay Rules]] *(if available)*
- [[Relay Training Strategies]] *(if available)*

---

**Bottom Line:** Pad delay is the hidden factor in relay timing that most coaches ignore. Train your swimmers to exit fast, understand the 17-second window, and communicate with timing operators when issues arise.

**A perfectly executed relay exchange doesn't matter if your swimmer is still in the pool when the pad goes live.**

# Dave Salo: Workout Structure and Training Methodology

**Category:** Training, Workout Design, Coaching Philosophy, Progressive Training  
**Coach:** Dave Salo  
**Environment:** Irvine Novaquatics (Nova), USC (retired), post-grad athletes

## Summary

Dave Salo's workout methodology is formulaic, progressive, and detail-oriented, stemming from his academic background (PhD, laboratory work). Every practice follows a thematic structure designed to build power stroke-by-stroke, recruit maximum muscle fibers, and create race-specific stress. His approach prioritizes quality over volume, packages work into digestible rounds, and maintains athlete engagement through variety within structure.

---

## Core Philosophy

### "This is where your dreams become nightmares"

**Primary Job:**  
*"To be at their best when they feel their worst"*

**Practice Purpose:**  
Creating a nightmare scenario where athletes must perform under maximum stress - physical, mental, psychological

---

## Universal Workout Structure

### Thematic Consistency

> *"Thematically every practice is the same. I don't bury things up."* - Dave Salo

**Every Practice:**
1. Warm-up (minimal heart rate elevation)
2. Stroke development (power-building, detail-oriented)
3. Resistance work (cords, parachutes, wet cords)
4. Race pace sets
5. Cool-down

---

## Stroke Development Phase

### Philosophy: Power Per Stroke

**Goal:**  
Maximize power ONE stroke at a time

**Progression:**
1. One stroke
2. Two strokes
3. Five strokes
4. Whole length (25m/y)
5. 50+ meters

### Example Sequence:

**Knuckle Paddles + Fins (75s):**
- Grab paddle with knuckles
- Moves pressure point back into mid-arm
- Forces elbows to stay up
- Focus on THREE FAST STROKES per 25
- "Surge" - feel body move with speed

**Regular Paddles (50s):**
- Contrast with knuckle paddles
- Feel the difference
- Focus on FOUR STROKES coming off wall
- Emphasis on posture off wall
- Passive speed, then surge into strokes

**Full Speed (25s):**
- Pick a breathing spot
- Only one breath, same spot every rep
- Full speed execution
- Controlled breathing pattern

---

## Detail-Oriented Emphasis

### What Salo Watches:

**Posture:**
- Coming off walls
- During passive glide
- Throughout stroke cycle
- Back flat, streamlined

**Speed Into Walls:**
- Habit formation
- Last 10m of each 25 = fast
- Good kick
- Maintain velocity approaching wall

**Speed Off Walls:**
- Passive glide
- Minimize resistance/drag
- Maximize efficiency
- Then surge into strokes

**The Surge:**
- Body should feel speed change with each stroke
- Swimmers must FEEL their body move
- Not just moving fast, but feeling the acceleration

---

## The Formula Approach

### Academic Background Influence

> *"It kind of goes back to my academic background. I worked in the laboratory for like six years for my PhD where everything was formulaic."*

**Characteristics:**
- Every element has a formula
- All work based on best 100 time
- No arbitrary efforts
- Everything purposeful
- Mathematically structured

### Chart-Based Training

**Not Genres/Color Charts:**
- No test sets
- No 3000s for time
- No threshold-based work

**Salo's Method:**
- Base everything on best 100 time
- Real to race
- Real to goal times
- Not arbitrary effort levels

---

## Workout Packaging

### The 3-4 Round Philosophy

**Old Salo (Early Nova Days):**
- 7-8 rounds
- Just kept adding more
- "Not functioning"

**Modern Salo (Last 2+ years):**
- Restrict to **3-4 rounds max**
- 15-20 minutes of really good work per package
- Move to next thing
- "Fewer rounds, do it really well"

**Result:**  
"I don't think any of my athletes consider practices boring because there's no repeat. There's repeat things but the way I package them they're generally pretty different."

---

## Engagement Strategy

### Why Break Things Up?

**Question:** "Dave, why do you break things up so much? How come you like it broken up like this?"

**Answer:**  
*"Then they're engaged in the process, right? It's more interesting. It just keeps your mind thinking about what the next thing is to do. And all of it's purposeful. It's not just throw stuff out there, it's all purposeful."*

**Method:**
- Constant variety within structure
- Mind stays engaged
- Always something new coming
- No monotony
- Every element has purpose

---

## Resistance Training

### Friday Protocol (Example)

**Sequence:**
1. Stroke development work (paddles, drills)
2. Parachute resistance sets
3. Race pace application

### Parachute Work Philosophy

**Why Singapore Parachutes:**
- Can customize branding (Nova logo)
- Hold up better than metal-framed versions
- Variable resistance (open/closed)

**Two Modes:**

**1. Maximum Resistance (Closed):**
- 100s - feel for stroke
- Accentuate stroke feel
- Mostly posture and stroke awareness
- Using resistance to enhance proprioception

**2. Minimum Resistance (Open):**
- 25s - race tempo
- Speed work with slight resistance
- Accommodating resistance

### The Race Tempo Under Load Principle

> *"My mantra the last few years is trying to get race tempo when loaded. I think that's the key to that next development."*

**Problem:**
- Athletes put load on → slow things down
- Compromise stroke to manage resistance
- Elbows drop

**Salo's Goal:**
- Race tempo WITHOUT compromising stroke
- More specific training
- Don't let elbows drop under load
- Maintain stroke integrity at race pace

**Specific Protocol (Example):**
- 4 × (100 + 4×25 + 75 + 25)
- 100: max resistance, feel stroke
- First 3 × 25: max resistance, only FOUR STROKES max effort, race tempo
- 4th × 25: open parachute, minimum resistance, whole 25 race tempo
- First 3 × 75: max effort ANY TWO of the 25s
- Last 75: whole 75 maxed out
- Plus 25: fins, active recovery

---

## Mid-Pool Speed Development

### Inspired by Vlad Morozov

**Observation:**  
Morozov could move his body forward on the FIRST stroke

**Most Swimmers:**  
Takes 3-4 strokes to really get body velocity moving

**Salo's Challenge:**  
How to teach everyone that same capability?

**Method:**
- Dead stops mid-pool
- 4-5 strokes maximum effort
- Learn to grab water immediately
- Develop instant body movement

**Sets Include:**
- Push and glide to line 4
- One stroke per black line starting at line 3
- Avoid catch-up drill trap
- Every stroke = full power
- Distance per stroke emphasis

---

## Gear Systems (Tempo Control)

### Third Gear, Fourth Gear, Fifth Gear

**By Time:**
- Chart-based on 100 best time
- Not by effort/feel
- Specific target times
- Objective measurement

**Purpose:**
- "Sneak up on speed a little bit"
- Don't want loss of efficiency in stroke
- Build into speed progressively
- Maintain stroke integrity

**Background:**

**Early Nova (1990s):**
- Aaron Peirsol, Amanda Beard, Jason Lezak
- Young kids who didn't know better
- "Just go fast all the time"

**Post-Grad Group:**
- Older, experienced athletes
- More fragile mentally/physically
- Must build to speed more carefully
- But still hit specific target times

---

## Volume Philosophy

### Not Volume-Centric

**Question:** "How do you keep track of volume in the workout?"

**Answer:**  
*"I don't keep track of any of that stuff. I'm not volume-centric and so I don't worry about how far we're going. It's really the content. I'm trying to take as many maximum effort strokes as possible in the course of a workout."*

**Focus:**
- Maximum effort strokes
- Quality over quantity
- Content matters, not distance
- Can't tell you total volume without adding it up

---

## Time Management

### Two-Hour Block, 90-105 Minute Reality

**Framework:**  
Build 2-hour block into schedule

**Actual Practice:**
- 90 minutes to 1:45 typical
- "Haven't gone two hours in a long time"
- Get done what needs to get done
- Not trying to fill time

---

## Coaching Adjustments

### Reading the Room

> *"A lot of good coaches like you, Brett, would kind of ascertain what your crew is like on a given day and then you alter work based on your sense and feel of where their heads are at."*

**Salo's Environment:**
- Post-grads
- Not distracted by school stress
- But have other stresses

**Approach:**
- Gauge readiness
- Adjust based on feel
- Still maintain structure
- Flexibility within framework

### On-the-Fly Adjustments

**Example:**
- Writes "3 × 25 + ..." on board
- Athletes question the "+"
- Salo: "Should be dot dot, not plus. Coaches' adjustments on the fly."
- "Dot dot" = rounds of
- "Plus" = additional work

**Nomenclature:**
- Athletes have him figured out
- He makes mistakes
- Adjusts in real-time
- Maintains sense of humor

---

## Friday Sample Workout

### Complete Breakdown

**WARM-UP:**
- Not detailed (minimal)
- "Bunch of pansies" on heart rate monitor
- Low intensity

**STROKE DEVELOPMENT:**

**Set 1: Knuckle Paddles + Regular Paddles**
- 75s knuckle paddles (3 fast strokes per 25)
- 50s regular paddles (4 strokes off wall, surge)
- 25s full speed (one breath, pick spot)
- Focus: Power development, feel, surge

**Set 2: Distance Per Stroke (DPS)**
- Push and glide to line 3 (no stroke before)
- One stroke per black line minimum
- Head reaches line = pull
- Avoid catch-up drill
- Long, powerful strokes
- Strong kick + posture

**3rd/4th/5th Gear by Time:**
- Chart-based targets
- Progressive speeds
- Maintain efficiency

**POWER WORK:**

**Rotation Kicks + Breakouts:**
- 360° rotation kick × 3
- Break out into 4 strokes freestyle full speed
- Casual kick to finish (first rounds)
- Later rounds: full speed to wall

**Push + Glide + Fast Kick + Fast Swim:**
- Push/glide to line 4
- Fast kick to line 5
- Fast swim to wall
- 5-second fast kick against wall
- Fast flip turn

**RESISTANCE WORK:**

**Parachute Set (4 rounds):**
- 100 max resistance (feel stroke)
- 4 × 25 max resistance (4 strokes race tempo each)
- 75 max effort (two 25s of choice, then all three)
- 25 + fins (active recovery)

**COOL-DOWN:**
- Active recovery
- Minimal

---

## Key Coaching Mantras

### Repeated Throughout Practice:

**"Race Tempo"**
- Everything at race speed or faster
- Not sprint training, RACE PACE training
- Tempo must be maintained under load

**"Grab That Water"**
- Immediate engagement
- First stroke matters
- Catch effectiveness

**"Feel Your Stroke"**
- Proprioception emphasis
- Body awareness
- Surge sensation

**"Posture"**
- Constant reminder
- Head position
- Back flat
- Streamline

**"Maximum Effort"**
- Full recruitment
- No halfway
- Best possible execution

---

## Practical Applications

### For Coaches:

**DO:**
- Package work in 3-4 round sets
- Make every element purposeful
- Build power stroke by stroke
- Use resistance to enhance feel
- Base work on best times, not arbitrary effort
- Break things up to maintain engagement
- Sneak up on speed with older athletes

**DON'T:**
- Add rounds just to add volume
- Lose detail focus
- Let athletes slow down under load
- Use test sets
- Ignore stroke integrity for speed
- Bore athletes with monotony

### For Swimmers:

**Focus On:**
- Every stroke with maximum effort
- Feel the surge
- Posture off every wall
- Speed into every wall
- Race tempo under load
- Specific targets (not just effort)

---

## The Nightmare Philosophy

### Why Create Stress?

**Goal:**  
Prepare athletes for the worst moments of racing

**Method:**
- Physical fatigue (cords → parachutes → race pace)
- Mental challenge (constant variety)
- Psychological stress ("when you feel your worst")
- Technical demand (perfect execution under duress)

**Result:**  
When race gets hard, athletes have been there before

---

## Evolution of Training

### Early Salo (1990s Nova):

- Young athletes
- Just go fast all the time
- High volume of speed
- Less sophisticated

### USC/Modern Nova:

- Post-grad athletes
- More sophisticated
- Build to speed
- Maintain stroke integrity
- Formulaic approach
- Detail-oriented

**Constant:**  
Race pace emphasis, detail focus, power development

---

## Bottom Line

**Salo's Workout DNA:**

1. **Academically rooted** - Formulaic, purposeful
2. **Progressively built** - Stroke by stroke to race pace
3. **Detail-obsessed** - Every element matters
4. **Engagement-focused** - 3-4 rounds, constant variety
5. **Quality over volume** - Maximum effort strokes, not distance
6. **Race-specific** - Everything tied to race times and tempo
7. **Stress-inducing** - "Where dreams become nightmares"

**Result:** Olympic champions across all distances and strokes

---

**Related Topics:** Race Pace Training, Resistance Training, Progressive Workout Design, Power Development, Stroke Mechanics

**Sources:** Brett Hawke Inside With interview series, filmed practice sessions at Nova

# Meet Equipment Reference Guide

**Sources:** Dak_1, Dak_2 (Daktronics OmniSport 2000 Timing Console Operation Manual)

## For Coaches: Why This Matters

Understanding meet equipment helps you:
- **Troubleshoot delays** - Know whether issues are operator error or equipment failure
- **Advocate for swimmers** - Understand when times might be inaccurate
- **Plan logistics** - Work better with meet management on timing setup
- **Train better** - Know what data the system captures (splits, relay exchanges, etc.)

---

## Daktronics OmniSport 2000 Overview

**Common at:** High school championships, college dual meets, large invitational meets

**Key differences from Colorado (CTS) system:**
- Built-in printer (CTS requires external printer)
- Can support both swimming AND diving modes
- Radio channel system for wireless components
- Network-enabled for Meet Manager integration

---

## Equipment Components

### 1. Console
- Central timing unit with built-in printer
- Sport-specific inserts (Swimming vs. Diving)
- Battery backup (continue timing during power loss)
- LCD display shows lane status in real-time

### 2. Touchpads
- Pressure-sensitive panels mounted on wall
- **Standard size:** Full lane width
- **Arming delay:** User-adjustable (prevents accidental touches)
- **Typical setting:** 0.3-0.5s delay after touch before re-arming

### 3. Deck Cabling Systems

**On-Deck System:**
- Lane modules sit on pool deck
- Easy to set up/remove
- Best for temporary installations

**In-Deck System:**
- Deck plates embedded in pool deck
- Permanent installation
- Cleaner appearance, fewer trip hazards

### 4. Horn Start HS-200
- Electronic starting system
- Microphone triggers timer start
- Ensures all lanes start simultaneously
- **Backup:** Manual "START" button on console

---

## Swimming Mode Features

### Key Functions (Coach Perspective)

**Lane On/Off**
- Turns lanes on/off for empty lanes
- Important: Operator must disable empty lanes to avoid false finishes

**Finish Arm / Finish Arm All**
- Prepares system to record finish
- "Finish Armed" should flash when swimmer approaches wall
- If it doesn't flash → manual "Finish Arm" button press needed

**Split Arm / Split Arm All**
- Arms intermediate splits (for distance events)
- Captures 50m/100m/200m splits in longer races

**Touch Commands**
- **+ Touch:** Add a missed lap
- **- Touch:** Remove an accidental/extra touch
- Used when touchpad fails to register turn

### Relay Functionality

**Relay Take-Off Settings:**
- Measures reaction time of incoming swimmer
- Calculated from outgoing swimmer's touch to incoming swimmer's departure
- **Legal range:** 0.00 to +0.30s (per USA Swimming rules)
- **DQ threshold:** Negative time (early departure)

**Printout includes:**
- Individual leg times
- Cumulative times
- Relay exchange times (r:+0.XX format)

---

## Scoreboard Display Modes

### Single-Line Scoreboards

**Display sequence:**
1. **Start of race:** Running time (shared across all lanes)
2. **Split times:** Brief hold (2-5s), then return to running time
3. **Finish times:** Each lane shows final time
4. **After reset:** Time-of-day or "READY"

### Multiple-Line Scoreboards

**Display shows:**
- Lane number
- Running time / final time
- Place (1st, 2nd, 3rd...)
- Split times (configurable hold duration)

**Results Order Options:**
- Lane order (1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8)
- Place order (fastest → slowest)

---

## Common Operator Errors (and What Coaches Should Watch For)

### 1. Missed Start
**Symptom:** Console shows "RESET" instead of running clock  
**Cause:** Horn start signal didn't reach console (loose cable, dead battery)  
**Fix:** Operator presses "START" button manually  
**Impact:** Slight timing delay (usually negligible)

### 2. Lane Not Disabled
**Symptom:** Empty lane shows a "finish" time  
**Cause:** Operator forgot to turn lane off  
**Fix:** Operator disables lane, reprints results  
**Impact:** Results printout shows extra lane (no effect on actual swimmers)

### 3. Finish Not Armed
**Symptom:** Swimmer finishes, but no time displays  
**Cause:** System didn't detect final lap approaching  
**Fix:** Operator manually presses "Finish Arm" for that lane  
**Impact:** Time may be slightly delayed (backup button times used)

### 4. Touchpad Malfunction
**Symptom:** "Touch" registered but swimmer didn't touch, OR swimmer touched but no registration  
**Cause:** Pad sensitivity issue, loose connection, or pad damage  
**Fix:** Operator uses +/- Touch commands; may need to replace pad  
**Impact:** Manual intervention required; backup button times become primary

---

## Diving Mode (Bonus Feature)

**Why coaches care:** Many pools use same timing system for diving

**Key features:**
- Judges enter scores via wireless consoles
- System calculates degree of difficulty automatically
- Displays running totals for each diver
- Prints individual dive sheets

**Judge Console Options:**
- Wireless (radio-linked)
- Wired (for permanent installations)

---

## Meet Manager Integration

### How It Works

**Daktronics OmniSport connects to Meet Manager via:**
1. **Serial connection** (RS-232) - older systems
2. **Network connection** (Ethernet) - modern systems

**Data flow:**
- Meet Manager → Console: Event/heat assignments, swimmer names, seed times
- Console → Meet Manager: Finish times, splits, relay exchanges

**Benefits:**
- Automatic results import (no manual entry)
- Real-time scoreboard updates
- Heat sheet generation with times

### Troubleshooting Meet Manager Connection

**If results aren't importing:**
1. Check network cable connection
2. Verify IP address matches Meet Manager settings
3. Confirm "Pro Software Enable" is ON in console settings
4. Restart Meet Manager and re-establish connection

---

## Battery Backup System

**Purpose:** Keep timing accurate during brief power outages

**How it works:**
- Internal battery maintains console memory
- Race in progress continues timing
- Printouts still work
- Scoreboard may go dark (depends on separate power)

**Battery test:** Menu → Test → Battery Test  
**Replacement:** Contact Daktronics service (not user-serviceable)

---

## Maintenance & Troubleshooting

### Basic Maintenance (Meet Host Responsibility)

**Before each meet:**
- [ ] Test all touchpads (press each one, verify console registers)
- [ ] Test buttons (2-3 per lane, verify asterisks appear on console)
- [ ] Test horn start (fire horn, confirm console starts clock)
- [ ] Load printer paper (thermal paper, correct orientation)
- [ ] Check battery status (Test Menu → Battery Test)

**During meet:**
- [ ] Monitor touchpad status for malfunctions
- [ ] Keep spare pads/buttons available
- [ ] Keep console out of direct sunlight (LCD screen visibility)

**After meet:**
- [ ] Print backup of all results (archive)
- [ ] Coil cables properly (avoid kinks/damage)
- [ ] Store touchpads flat (avoid bending connectors)

### When to Call for Service

**Immediate (stop meet if necessary):**
- Console won't power on
- Multiple touchpads fail simultaneously
- Start signal not working AND manual start fails
- Printer won't print AND computer connection down

**Can finish meet, but schedule service:**
- Single touchpad consistently failing
- Scoreboard not updating (if results still printing)
- Battery test shows low charge
- Paper jams frequently

---

## Cross-References

- [[Timing-System-Basics-For-Coaches]] - CTS system comparison
- [[Meet-Day-Operations-Guide]] - Running smooth meets
- [[USA-Swimming-Timing-Rules]] - Official regulations
- [[Relay-Exchange-Technology]] - Reaction time measurement

---

**Coach's Bottom Line:**

> Most timing issues don't affect your swimmers' official times. Touchpads can fail, operators can make mistakes, but backup systems (buttons, manual watches) protect results. Your job: Know when to question a time (2+ seconds off expectation) and when to let the officials handle it (minor delays, operator corrections).

**Key Coaching Insight:**
- **Touchpad failure ≠ lost time.** Backup buttons are official.
- **Relay exchanges are measured electronically.** Trust the printout, not your eye.
- **Splits are gold.** If system captures splits, use them for post-race analysis.

# Dave Salo: Innovation and Creativity in Coaching

**Category:** Coaching Philosophy, Career Development, Training Innovation, Methodology  
**Coach:** Dave Salo  
**Source:** Inside With Brett Hawke Podcast - Coaches Corner Interview

## Summary

Dave Salo's career represents a unique blend of academic rigor and practical coaching innovation. With a PhD in Exercise Science and over 40 years of coaching experience, Salo built his reputation on challenging conventional training wisdom—particularly around volume-based training—and developing a race-pace focused methodology that has produced Olympic champions and world record holders.

---

## Career Origins

### Early Influences

**Family Background:**
- Father was a high school teacher and hockey player from Minnesota
- Father coached football and basketball
- DNA of coaching ran in the family

**Swimming Career:**
- Started swimming age 8-9, tagging along with older brother
- Modest swimming career—made Junior Nationals but nothing beyond
- Swam breaststroke, which later informed his expertise

**Early Coaching Aspiration:**
- Doodled Olympic rings on school binders in high school
- Always envisioned becoming an Olympic swimming coach
- Applied for first head coaching job straight out of high school (unsuccessful)

### First Coaching Positions

**Downey Dolphins (Age 19-20):**
- Found job out of necessity to pay for college at Long Beach State
- Became head coach by age 20
- Started as traditional coach doing 9,000 yards/practice
- Hated it—kids weren't buying in
- Began questioning conventional methods

**Academic Foundation:**
- Studied under Professor Joseph Mastropolo at Long Beach State (1978-1983)
- Mastropolo opened his eyes to sprint training effects on performance
- This planted the seed for race-pace training philosophy

---

## Academic Journey

### Educational Path

**Why Academia:**
- Didn't want to be a high school teacher (traditional coach path)
- Wanted to separate himself from "average" coaches
- Friends were taking advanced classes (algebra, trigonometry, chemistry, physics)
- Followed their academic trajectory

**Degrees:**
- Bachelor's at Long Beach State
- Master's degree (exercise physiology focus)
- PhD in Exercise Science at USC

**Philosophy:**
- "Most coaches have a bachelor's—master's would separate me"
- "Friend pursuing PhD—that would separate me even more"
- Swimming taught him how to deal with demanding work
- Hard science degree requiring 10-12 hours/day in laboratory

### Mentorship Under Peter Daland

**USC Assistant Coach (1984-1990):**
- Graduate assistant while pursuing PhD
- Peter Daland hired him on recommendation from wife Ingrid
- Very different coaching philosophies
- Peter respected Dave despite philosophical differences

**Key Conversation:**
> Peter told him: "I'm coming under a lot of criticism for hiring you because of your philosophy... but that doesn't matter. I want you on my staff and the guys like you."

**What Dave Learned:**
- Not X's and O's of training
- How Peter had great rapport with 18-20 year olds despite being in his 70s
- Alumni relationships and support systems
- How to interact with athletes to advance your philosophy
- Recognized coaching philosophies could differ while relationships succeeded

---

## Development of Training Philosophy

### Philosophical Evolution

**Experiential vs. Academic:**
- Most coaches have experiential philosophies (based on what they experienced)
- Salo's philosophy rooted in academics and research
- Professor Mastropolo taught him about sprint training effects
- Studied physiology to understand performance mechanisms

**Early Innovation (Mid-1980s):**
- Started implementing race-pace concepts 10+ years before they became mainstream
- Used 20-yard pool as much as 25-yard pool
- Never went over 100 yards per repetition
- All race pace work

**Famous Example:**
- 12-year-old boy breaking 5:00 in 500 freestyle
- Never touched 1000 straight in practice (except once, stopped him halfway)
- Qualified for Junior Nationals in mile/1000 without traditional distance work

### The "Physiology Corner"

**Swimming World Magazine Column:**
- Co-editor Chris (last name forgotten) encouraged him to write
- Explored concepts contrary to current training vogue
- One article received severe criticism

**Infamous Letter to Editor:**
> "Who is this coach Salo? He doesn't know what he's talking about. Who has he ever coached? This is ridiculous. I'm not changing the way I coach my athletes because this upstart is telling us that's what the research shows in non-specific studies."

**Salo's Response:**
- Kept the letter framed on his desk as motivation
- "You're right, I've never coached anybody. But these are the concepts I believe in, and when I get the chance to coach that level of athlete, that's what I'm going to employ."

### "Sprint Salo" Book

**Origin Story (1983-84):**
- Wrote article in Swimming World
- At end said: "I've got a book coming out—send $10 if interested"
- **Received 200 requests for a book he hadn't written**
- Had to quickly write it with self-publishing friend

**Book Content:**
- Part 1: Physiology background and understanding
- Part 2: Actual workouts used with athletes
- Focus on race pace, not just reducing volume
- Efficiency of mechanics
- Training philosophy that continues 40 years later

---

## Core Training Philosophy

### Race Pace Focus

**Why Speed in Practice:**

> "Swimming slow is boring to watch."

**Mechanical Reasons:**
- Drag forces increase exponentially with speed
- Must train at drag force encountered at racing speed
- Body positioning changes dramatically at race speeds

**Physiological Reasons:**
- Buffering system only enhanced by going fast
- Can't improve buffering capacity at slow speeds
- Must recruit all muscle fiber types at maximum intensity

### Volume Philosophy

**The 20-Minute Concept:**
> "I package my work in 20-minute segments of really intense work. You've got to equate what you're doing in that 20 minutes to going 1:48 speed or 1:45 speed—whatever your race time is."

**To Athlete Worried About 200 Fly:**
> "We train once a day for an hour and a half to 1:45, and you're asking me if that's enough work for an event you want to go 1:48 in? Come on. An hour and 45 minutes to get you ready for 1 minute 45 seconds should be enough."

**Walking Analogy:**
> "I could literally walk a marathon. I couldn't go very fast because I'm not doing anything very fast. Same with swimming—if you want to go 46 seconds in 100 fly, you have to train at that speed."

### XYZ Set Innovation

**Created for 2004 Olympic Trials:**
- Had 25 kids going to trials
- Range from Jason Lezak (sprinter) to Haley Pearsall (distance)
- Only coach on deck during fine-tuning phase

**Set Structure:**
- X = 1:00 interval
- Y = 1:15 interval  
- Z = 1:30 interval
- Athletes choose distance, stroke, equipment
- **Requirement: FAST**

**Purpose:**
- Learn what athletes think they need
- Jason going 25-25-25 = thinks he needs more rest
- Haley going 100-150-200 = thinks she needs more volume
- Use information to guide championship preparation
- Athletes get what they want while coach gets data

**Modern Variations:**
Example: 100 + X, 75 + X, 50 + X
- 100/75/50 on set intervals (1:30, 1:15, 1:00)
- X always on 1:20
- "Decide what you want on X, but I just want it fast"
- Minimum 25 or 50, varies by workout
- Constant evolution based on feedback

---

## Breaststroke Mastery

### Why Breaststroke Success

> "The reason I've been successful with breaststroke is because I was a breaststroke swimmer."

**Personal Experience:**
- Made first Junior Nationals in 100 breast (illegally entered without qualifying time)
- Had a cold, stressed about $100 fine
- Came off last turn, looked 50m down thinking "I'm going to be fined $100"
- Touched wall, made Junior Nationals
- Still remembers what that stroke feels like

**Understanding the Feel:**
- Knows what it's like when stroke "doesn't feel right"
- Common breaststroker complaint: "It felt great yesterday, today it feels awkward"
- Knows how to get kids back to correct timing
- Timing issue, not strength or technique issue

### Key Breaststroke Components

**1. The Line:**
- Completion of extension = line from fingertips through head to feet
- Starting point for stroke analysis

**2. Recovery:**
- Turn hands and elbows into extension
- **Does not believe in recovering over surface**
- Rotation of elbows critical
- Elbows pointed downward create drag

**3. Head Position:**
- In line with back and shoulders
- Not forced one way or the other
- Shoulders drive movement forward, not head
- Dislikes excessive "head thing"
- Dislikes hands coming out of water (tries to tone down)

**4. The Kick:**
- Relationship between knees and heels
- Old way: "Up, out, together"
- Modern: Straighter path
- Heels outside the knees even if abbreviated (like Rebecca Soni)

**5. The Pull:**
- Elbow position critical
- More of a scull than a pull
- Teaches sculling action vs. pulling action

**6. Lower Back:**
- Is it flattening out or caving in?
- Water caving in on top = problem

### Individualization

**Eight Different Looks:**
> "Watch the finals of the Olympics—eight swimmers, eight different looks to what this stroke looks like."

**Athletes Coached:**
- Jessica Hardy
- Amanda Beard
- Stacey Anna Stitts
- Rebecca Soni
- Julie Efimova (Russia)
- All very different stroke mechanics
- All successful at highest level

**Proud Moment:**
Short Course World Championships—his three breaststrokers went 1-2-3 in 50m breast:
1. Julie Efimova (Russia)
2. Jessica Hardy
3. Rebecca Soni

**Stroke Comparisons:**
- **Rebecca Soni:** "The hummingbird of breaststroke—doesn't make any sense"
- Half scull, brief abbreviated kick, but devastatingly fast
- Similar to Adam Peaty's stroke
- **Adam Peaty:** Very abbreviated kick, straight path, strong scull, maintains tempo entire 100
- **Lydia Jacoby:** Completely different from Lilly King
- **Tatjana Schoenmaker (South Africa):** Completely different with extremely late breath

### Coaching Rebecca Soni

**First Year Challenges:**
- Third coach in one year (club coach Speedling, Mark Schubert, then Salo)
- Went from distance-oriented 400 IM work to Salo's philosophy
- **During workout, stopped 15 meters from wall, crying:**

> "I hate your drills!"

**Dave's Response:**
> "Rebecca, I understand your stroke and I'm not gonna have you swim like Amanda or Jessica or Stacyanna. I'm gonna swim you YOUR stroke. I understand your stroke and I'll get better at understanding your stroke. There are some key things to your stroke that I think we can enhance versus take away from. I'm not going to change your stroke because she knew her stroke was different. Look, I know breaststroke, and you're just going to have to trust me."

**Evolution:**
- Won NCAAs sophomore year
- Went to meet in Australia right after NCAAs
- Swam 2nd in 200 breast long course with no LCM training
- Came back: "Maybe what we're doing is adequate, maybe it's going to be okay"
- Relationship flourished for 8-9 years

**Rebecca's Philosophy (taught by Dave):**
> When asked about tapering by recruits: "You don't worry about that stuff. Whether taper was good or bad or wasn't perfect, you're always going to get up and race fast because that's what you're supposed to do."

## Managing Elite Athletes

### Training Relativity

**Rebecca Soni as Trainer:**
> "She was one of my best trainers, but it was relative to Rebecca Soni. Rebecca worked hard, was very diligent, did everything we asked her to do. But if you watched her from the stands, you wouldn't go 'Oh that's Rebecca Soni, look!' She trained really hard FOR HER."

**Key Principle:**
> "Our athletes—is everybody meeting the expectation around themselves, not some other standard that you might have?"

**Examples:**
- Rebecca would trail in as Dave announced next set
- Got mad: "I haven't even finished this yet, you're telling the next set!"
- "Don't worry, I'm going to explain it 10 times"

### Champion Characteristics

**Athletes You Could Count On:**
- **Rebecca Soni:** Great racer, always best game every time
- **Aaron Peirsol:** "You never expected anything less"
- **Haley Anderson:** Hard worker, tenacious racer, gave it her all

**Carson Vissering:**
- So much talent and skill
- "In the clouds all the time"
- Had to bark at him: "Wake up Carson! Focus!"
- Always solid when time came to race
- "One of the easiest athletes to coach—count on them when they race"

### Multiple Athletes Same Event

**2012 Olympic Trials Breaststrokers:**
- Carrie Hein  
- Jessica Hardy
- Rebecca Soni
- Lindsey Payne
- Amanda Beard (briefly)
- **Five legit women for four Olympic spots**

**Management Strategy:**
> "I've always been very cognizant of never pitting women against each other. That's a disaster. I never pit the women together, never put them up against each other."

**Separation:**
- Rebecca and Jessica didn't really train together
- Trained differently
- Purposely kept apart
- "Massage not their ego but the way they believed they needed to train"

**Accidental Heat:**
- Doing repeat 50s LCM
- ~10 heats of swimmers
- Last heat ended up: Jessica Hardy, Rebecca Soni, Carrie(?)
- "Oh sh*t, you never want to do that"
- "Sent them off, they had a great 50, nobody got upset, raced really well"

**Amanda Beard:**
- Locker room dynamics she couldn't handle
- Salo: "Why don't you go swim with Cindy Gallagher?"
- Set her up for success elsewhere
- Okay with decision

## Pride and Psychology

### Empowerment Philosophy

**Running Away From Something:**
> "A lot of these athletes are running away from something. They think their life will change if they just win that gold medal or break a world record. Then they find out—oh my gosh, this didn't really change my life. That's kind of a dysfunctional thing."

**Empowering Women:**
> "Stop swimming for me or stop swimming for your parents. Do it because this is what YOU want to accomplish. Don't look at it as life-changing—it can impact your life positively, but don't look at it so disruptively that it makes you happy. That's not what's going to make you happy."

### The Pride Message

**Constant Theme:**
> "Be prideful of your work, what you do—not looking for somebody else's pride in you. Don't look for your parents' pride. They have to be proud of you because they were cheering you on when you learned to go potty. That's what they do, that's what they're supposed to do."

**Personal Pride:**
> "Learn to be prideful in your daily work, your performance, your results—not look for pride from somebody else: your coach, your parents, your grandparents. This is YOU. It's YOURS. You've got to take it, embrace it, and your life will be a lot better off."

**On His Own Career:**
> "Do I worry about criticisms of me? No. I'm proud of my career. I'm really proud of what I've done. I know my parents would be proud of me even though they're deceased, but I didn't seek their pride. I want to be proud of ME and what I've done and how I've had a positive impact on athletes—not just the gold medals."

---

## Supplement Philosophy & Doping Issues

### Anti-Supplement Stance

**Core Position:**
> "I'm probably the most anti-supplement coach there ever was. I don't encourage my athletes to take any kind of supplement, I don't care what it is."

**Dietary Philosophy:**
- Good balanced diet
- Time-sensitive eating
- Will replenish glycogen stores without supplements
- Stopped encouraging even general vitamins after Kicken Vencil incident

**Athlete Response:**
- Once kids knew his position, they would hide supplements
- Would shuffle items into lockers when he walked in
- "I don't care what it is—glycogen repletion, vitamins—you don't need it, just eat properly"

### Systemic Problem

**International Athletes:**
> "The systemic problem, especially when coaching international athletes—they're transitory. They go to international championships, do their race, sit in the jacuzzi with other great athletes talking about 'What are you taking?'"

**Creatine Era:**
- Before 2008, whole group taking creatine
- "Everyone said they had to take creatine for phosphocreatine stores"
- Gave them research consensus on creatine
- "Got exhausted trying to tell them you don't need this stuff"

### High-Profile Cases

**Jessica Hardy:**
- Tested positive for tainted vitamin
- Proved supplement was contaminated
- Sued supplement company, made a lot of money
- Still ended her career trajectory

**Kicken Vencil:**
- Proven tainted vitamin supplement
- Career ended
- "Both proved they had a supplement that was contaminated"

**Julie Efimova:**
- USC told him to curtail relationship
- She trained with him through Russian trials when allowed
- Second test was for "concept versus real performance enhancing drug"
- Supplement all Eastern European athletes taking for "heart protection"
- WADA banned it despite limited scientific evidence it enhanced performance
- "Unfortunate—everyone doing it so there must be something wrong with it"

**Katinka Hosszú:**
- Criticized constantly
- Never tested positive, tested all the time racing worldwide
- "One of the hardest working individuals in weight room and pool"
- "Amazing athlete, clean as can be"
- "We come under criticism for being exceptional"

### The Real Standard

> "When American athletes do amazing things, nobody accuses Katie Ledecky when she won her first 800 free gold. Her leap in performance was huge. This girl works hard, we've seen it, she's got a great work ethic—those results are possible."

**Position:**
> "I wish swimmers would just come to practice, train really hard, and have a good healthy diet. I think that takes care of itself."

He said 'Oh okay' (surfer dude). I said 'We're not going to talk about it again. I just think you can do that, I just want you to know that's what I think you can be.' ### Distance Success Without Distance Training

**Ous Mellouli:**
- Gold medal 10K open water
- Gold medal 1500m freestyle (four years apart)
- "We didn't do 10,000 yards in practice"
- "We didn't do a 1500 in practice in the eight years I coached him—never did a straight 1500"

**Larsen Jensen Story:**
- Called Dave: "Coach, Sunday's tomorrow, would you mind coming in? I want to go straight 1500 for time, just want to put in a really good effort."
- Dave called Ous: "Come on in, Larsen's going to do a 1500 for time"
- About 400m in, Ous got out
- Dave: "What's up?"
- Ous: "I haven't done a 1500 with you in two years. Why am I going to start doing them now?"
- Dave: "You're right."
- Larsen finished alone
