## Professional Swimming & ISL

### ISL Experience

**Abu Dhabi World Championships (Short Course):**
- Head coach for USA Swimming
- Did 2018 World Championships in China
- Called by Lindsay Mintenko
- Coaching staff: Ozzie Quevedo, Wyatt Collins, Blair Beckmann (Virginia), Bruce Marchionne
- "Really excited—young coaches I get to guide through this"

**Tokyo Frog Kings:**
- Didn't make semi-finals in Eindhoven
- "Amazing experience, really enjoyed it"
- Kitajima asked him to take on role
- Retired from USC, could commit 100%

**40 Days in Italy:**
> "Had a great time. I could have lived there forever probably. Enjoyed it with the kids, got to train them the way we wanted to train them, got to compete."

### Advice to Professional Athletes

**On Payment Issues:**
> "Athletes could best serve themselves and development of ISL by quitting complaining about not getting paid on time. They've been afforded an opportunity to train and compete, all expenses paid. Whether they've gotten bonuses or salaries is beside the point."

**The Sacrifice:**
> "God, look at the opportunity that's been presented. Give this league the option and footing it needs to be successful in the long run. Everybody has to sacrifice for change to happen. Sacrifice that payment on time to make this happen so down the road kids who come up are going to have an environment where they can be successful, train, compete, and get paid—maybe on time."

**Professionalization:**
- American swimmers are complimentary—give up time for age groupers
- Make themselves available for promotions
- Hope they never lose that, don't become entitled
- "Calm down—it's swimming, not major league baseball or football"
- Have balance, promote yourself, make yourself available
- Swimming lives in "cloud of imperceptible beings—nobody knows we're there"
- Problem: "You're a blob bouncing around the water, can't see face or personality"

### NCAA Name, Image, Likeness

> "They're gonna learn as NCAA rules change about name, image, and likeness. They're gonna be exposed to that, then match it up with performance. Make themselves available, get out of their comfort zone, don't have this sense of entitlement because you're great."

**Ditch Digger Comment:**
> "I kind of laugh at some of our athletes periodically—they're no better than a ditch digger. They take offense to that. I said no, ditch digger is better because he digs a ditch, you just swim. As coaches, we're entertained by it, and we get to coach you towards these performances. But it's just swimming. It's not that important in life. It's not curing cancer, not solving the problem with inflation, none of those things."

**But Still:**
> "I believe: if you're going to do it, do it well."

## Philosophy on Goals

### Anti-Goal Setting

**Recent Conversation:**
- International swimmer after one month: "Are we gonna have that goal meeting?"
- Dave: "I don't talk to my athletes about goals"

**His Actual Goal:**
> "My goal is to train you to be the best athlete that you can be. It doesn't matter what your goal is. I'm trying to make you the best you can be—not pigeonhole it based on a particular time."

**USC Philosophy:**
> "I don't know if it was good or bad, just my philosophy. I would never talk to kids about specific goals. If they had them, they could relate them to me—that's fine. But I told them my goal and my responsibility is to drive everybody towards NCAAs podium."

**The Charge:**
> "I'm looking at that. If your goal is something less than that, that's fine. But my goal is to coach you towards making NCAAs and scoring at NCAAs. So your goals are really irrelevant to what my charge is—that's what my job is. I don't have a lot of goal meetings with athletes. I'm just trying to make them better than they think they are."

---

## Evolution and Innovation

### Constant Evolution

**Feedback Loop:**
> "I keep evolving the way I do things. I evolve in part by the feedback I get from other coaches who try some of these things."

**Example: Balancing Distance & Race Pace**

Coach complaint: "I want to do what you do, but I took over a team and they think they're not doing enough yardage, not enough distance. How do I balance that?"

**Dave's Solution:**
"Here's a way to do it—let's say you want to go 2x200s but implement my philosophy (race pace):

- Each 200 is broken
- 3x25 + X
- X is the remaining 125 yards
- First three 25s: FAST, 5 seconds rest after each
- Then choose what the 125 looks like
- Break it how you want, 20 seconds total rest time
- Go straight, I don't care
- **You get fast work I want + distance work they think they need**
- Must be under your best 200 total swim time"

**Result:**
> "They'll find out in short order, 'Well okay, let me do that.' Gives balance between fast work and volume perception."

### Drill Philosophy

**High-Intensity Drills:**
- Does fast work in drill sets
- Drill at high intensity vs. slow intensity
- Warm down and recovery at slower intensities

**Rebecca Soni Reaction:**
> Long course workout, mostly drill at race pace. About 400m in she stops 15m from wall, in tears crying: "I HATE YOUR DRILLS!"

**Philosophy:**
> "A lot of drill type work at race pace. Recovery work between sets. Not focused on how far we go—that's not very important. Challenging athletes to see if they can't work faster."

## Criticism and Confidence

### Handling Criticism

**On Head Coach Appointment (No swimmers on team):**
> "I didn't read any criticism. I think [Lindsay] was looking for somebody who's got experience and can help guide coaches that don't have as much experience. I give my assistant coaches a lot of autonomy to really be partners in this—not superior/inferior."

**On Why He Got Invited:**
> "Generally speaking, I always thought she invites me to do this job because I'm available and I'll say yes. But that's true—top coaches just aren't available."

**Career-Long Perspective:**
> "I don't bother with criticism. I've been criticized my entire career. People used to criticize me and say I was 'off the rock' by what I do and how I do things. I don't care. I've been successful and proud of my career, and I'd rather have my career than a lot of other people's careers. If I'm criticized for that, I can deal with that."

### On Peter Daland's Warning

**What Peter Told Him:**
> "I'm coming under a lot of criticism for hiring you as an assistant coach because of your philosophy. But that doesn't matter—I want you on my staff and the guys like you. I'm telling you this because it may prevent you from moving on in our sport because your ideas and theories are very different."

**Dave's Response:**
- Knew he was there to be Peter's assistant
- That's what he was—assisting Peter's program, not creating his own
- Great relationship, mutual respect
- Peter always encouraged him to become university professor
- Dave kept going the swimming coach line

### Letter to the Editor (1980s)

**The Criticism:**
> "Who is this coach Salo? He doesn't know what he's talking about. Who has he ever coached? This is ridiculous. I'm not changing the way I coach my athletes because this upstart is telling us that's what the research shows in non-specific studies."

**How He Used It:**
- Kept letter framed prominently on desk
- Motivation: "Well okay, you're right, I've never coached anybody. But these are the concepts I believe in, and when I get the chance to coach that level of athlete, that's what I'm going to employ and see what happens with it."
- Famous coach who wrote it had very successful distance swimmer
- Used it as fuel to prove his methods

## Key Takeaways for Coaches

### Be Creative and Innovative
- Don't just follow what everyone else does
- Ground your philosophy in science and research
- Be willing to challenge conventional wisdom
- Constantly evolve based on feedback and results

### Individualize Training
- "Is everybody meeting the expectation around themselves, not some other standard?"
- Rebecca Soni was a great trainer "relative to Rebecca"
- Different strokes for different folks (literally in breaststroke)
- XYZ sets allow athletes to self-select while maintaining quality

### Fast Swimming Requires Fast Training
- Can't walk your way to a marathon PR
- Drag forces change exponentially with speed
- Buffering systems only improve with high intensity
- Volume is less important than velocity

### Psychology Matters
- Keep athletes happy and engaged
- Make training fun
- Empower athletes to swim for themselves, not others
- Teach pride in work, not seeking others' pride
- Manage egos and relationships carefully (especially women in same events)

### Trust the Process
- Taper doesn't matter if you're not ready to race
- Rebecca Soni: "You're always going to get up and race fast because that's what you're supposed to do"
- Consistency over magic
- Believe athletes can exceed what they think possible

### Academic Foundation
- PhD gave him credibility and deeper understanding
- But also learned from experience and observation
- Watched other coaches when recruiting
- "How do they interact with their team to advance their philosophy?"

### Anti-Supplement
- "Most anti-supplement coach there ever was"
- Good diet and time-sensitive eating is enough
- Supplements are athletes talking to each other, not coach-driven
- Protect athletes from tainted products and bad decisions

### Professional Development
- Read research
- Write and share (he wrote Swimming World column)
- Learn from mentors even if philosophies differ
- Separate yourself through education and innovation
- Don't be afraid to be different

### Personal Philosophy

> "I'm proud of my career. I'm really proud of what I've done. I know my parents would be proud of me even though they're deceased, but I didn't seek their pride. I want to be proud of ME and what I've done and how I've had a positive impact on athletes—not just the gold medals."

**Not Just Champions:**
> "I'm proud of forcing Anthony Ervin to swim when every time you swam you wanted to get out. I'd say 'Look, you just have to do it' and he did. We had a falling out, he went on to swim with Coach Marsh and won a gold medal. I'm really proud of him for that. But it was more important that he accomplished what he wanted to accomplish versus trying to make me proud."

# Swim Meet Timing Systems: A Coach's Guide

**Source:** Superior Swim Timing Manual (2019)  
**Category:** Meet Operations  
**For:** Coaches running meets or attending competitions

---

## Why This Matters for Coaches

Understanding timing systems helps you:
- **Run smoother meets** - Know what to expect from timing operators
- **Troubleshoot issues** - Understand why times might be delayed or adjusted
- **Advocate for swimmers** - Know when backup times are used vs. pad times
- **Prepare for meets** - Understand what information timing systems need

---

## Core Timing Concepts

### Pad Delay (Critical for Relays)

**What it is:** The time period after a touch when the touchpad is temporarily disabled.

**Why it matters:**
- Allows relay swimmers to exit the pool without triggering a false time
- **Standard setting: 17 seconds**
- Prevents accidental touches from being recorded as race finishes

**Coach tip:** If a relay swimmer is struggling to exit quickly, timing operators can adjust pad delay during the race.

### Backup Times vs. Pad Times

**Pad Times (Primary):**
- Recorded when swimmer touches the electronic touchpad
- Most accurate method (hundredths of a second)
- The "official" time in most cases

**Backup Times (Secondary):**
- Manual button press or stopwatch
- Used when pad fails or doesn't register a touch
- Can be selected as official time if pad malfunctions

**When backup times are used:**
- Pad doesn't register (swimmer touches too lightly, pad malfunction)
- Timing operator observes finish and presses button
- Purple lane color = backup time exists but no pad time

### Time Adjustments

**Important:** Not all swim organizations allow time adjustments during meets.

**If allowed, operators can:**
- Switch from pad time to backup button time
- Use wireless watch times
- Manually enter corrected times

**Coach responsibility:** Know your organization's rules. USA Swimming has specific protocols about when times can/cannot be adjusted.

---

## Lane States & What They Mean

### Lane Status Indicators

**Enabled (Active):**
- Lane is ready to record times
- Will register pad touches and button presses

**Disabled (Inactive):**
- Lane will not record times
- Used for empty lanes or mechanical issues

**Auto Lane Disable:**
- System automatically disables a lane if it has ZERO pad hits while other lanes have TWO touches
- Prevents timing errors from unused lanes
- Can be manually re-enabled by clicking lane number

**When it doesn't apply:** 50s (in 25Y pool) or 100s (in 50m pool) - no double touches expected

### DQ (Disqualification) Flag

- Timing operators can mark lanes as DQ
- Sends DQ status to meet management software
- Lane shows "place = 0" on scoreboard
- **Note:** Timing operator DQ is different from official referee DQ

---

## Meet Operations: What Coaches Should Know

### Auto-Finish vs. Manual Finish

**Auto-Finish (Standard):**
- Race automatically closes 8 seconds after last swimmer finishes
- System advances to next heat/event automatically
- Requires start list files loaded into timing system

**Manual Finish:**
- Operator must manually close each race
- Used when greater control is needed
- May slow meet progression

### False Starts

Timing systems have a false start function that:
- Alerts operators and meet management software
- May trigger audible/visual alarms
- Requires manual reset before next start

**Coach tip:** If you observe a false start, timing operator must be notified before the race proceeds.

### One-Lap Races (25s/50s)

**"Don't Run 25s" Feature:**
- For races run from near end with stopwatch timing at far end
- System advances to next race at start signal
- Timer does NOT start, no electronic output file created
- Common for age-group meets using manual timing

---

## Troubleshooting Common Issues

### Lane Won't Accept Touch

**Possible causes:**
1. **Pad delay still active** - Wait for delay period to expire (typically 17 sec)
2. **Lane disabled** - Operator can re-enable by clicking lane number
3. **Pad needs manual ARM** - Operator can force-arm the pad immediately

**ARM button:** Overrides pad delay, allows immediate touch registration
**F ARM button:** Forces next touch to FINISH the race for that lane (emergency use)

### Missing or Wrong Times

**Purple lane color = backup time exists but no pad time**
- Likely cause: Pad didn't register swimmer's touch
- Operator can select backup time using "USE" button
- Check if time adjustment is allowed by meet rules

**Backup buttons activate:**
- After initial pad delay expires (NOT just on final lap)
- Operators can record backup times throughout race

### Race Won't Close

**Common causes:**
1. Pad not registering final touch - Operator must hit "Force Finish"
2. Lane counting wrong number of laps - Operator can adjust lap count
3. Auto-finish disabled - Must manually finish

---

## Coach Checklist for Meet Day

### Before the Meet

- [ ] Confirm timing system is receiving your roster/entries
- [ ] Verify swimmers are listed in correct lanes on timing console
- [ ] Check if relay exhibition swimmers are properly noted
- [ ] Understand venue's policy on time adjustments

### During the Meet

- [ ] Watch for lane status indicators on scoreboard
- [ ] Note if backup times are being used frequently (may indicate equipment issues)
- [ ] Alert timing if you observe pad malfunctions
- [ ] Know the protocol for questioning times at your meet

### After Each Race

- [ ] Verify times posted match what you observed
- [ ] Check for DQ flags that may need referee confirmation
- [ ] Note any timing irregularities for meet referee

---

## Integration with Meet Management Software

Timing systems connect to:
- **Hy-Tek Meet Manager** - Most common system
- **TeamUnify TouchPad** - Cloud-based option
- Other platforms via serial interface or file sharing

**What coaches should know:**
- Start lists must be generated BEFORE timing system can use them
- Timing results flow back to meet management automatically
- Network issues can cause delays in result posting
- Manual entry may be needed if connection fails

**Files generated:**
- Results files (for meet software import)
- HTML printouts (for immediate reference)
- Each race: `Event XXX-HH (Race NNNN).html`

---

## Advanced Features Coaches May Encounter

### Team Scores Display

Modern systems can:
- Accept team score entries during meet
- Display running team scores on scoreboard
- Update automatically as races finish

### Statistics Tracking

Some timing consoles provide:
- Session-wide statistics
- Individual lane performance data
- Timeline views of race progression

### Wireless Watch Integration

- Backup timing via wireless watches
- Syncs with timing console
- Provides additional redundancy beyond button backups

---

## Key Takeaways

1. **Pad delay (17 sec) is essential for relays** - prevents false touches as swimmers exit
2. **Backup times are always recorded** - system has redundancy built in
3. **Lane colors tell a story** - purple = backup exists without pad time
4. **Not all meets allow time adjustments** - know your organization's rules
5. **Auto lane disable prevents errors** - but can be overridden if needed
6. **8-second auto-finish is standard** - but can be disabled for manual control

---

## Related Wiki Pages

- [[Relay Exchanges and Timing]] *(if available)*
- [[Meet Management Best Practices]] *(if available)*
- [[USA Swimming Timing Rules]] *(if available)*

---

**Bottom Line:** Understanding timing systems makes you a better meet host and a more informed coach. When something goes wrong with timing, you'll know what questions to ask and what solutions are possible.

**Next time your swimmer's touch doesn't register, you'll know exactly what the timing operator should do.**

# Dave Salo: Technical Coaching and Sports Psychology

**Category:** Sports Psychology, Coaching Philosophy, Technical Coaching, Communication  
**Coaches:** Dave Salo (Tokyo Frog Kings), Tom Rushton (Energy Standard)  
**Source:** ISL Podcast - Discussion about technical future of swimming

## Summary

Wide-ranging discussion with Dave Salo and Tom Rushton covering sports psychology in swimming, communication skills for coaches, visualization techniques, the role of trust in coach-athlete relationships, managing elite athletes, and technical innovations including the controversial freestyle with dolphin kick concept.

---

## Sports Psychology in Coaching

### Value of Sports Psychologists

**Dave Salo's Position:**
> "The use of sports-specific psychologists or counselors can be a really valuable asset to our athletes and coaches. It's not clinical—that's completely different. A really good sports psychologist works on communication skills."

**For Coaches, Not Just Athletes:**
- Worked with Kathy Wickstrand (not sports psychologist, but communication expert)
- Helped understand skill set and ability to listen
- Better communicate with athletes
- Recognize differences between people

**For Athletes:**
- Relaxation skills
- Visualization skills  
- Go-to person who's not the coach
- Removes barrier between coach and athlete
- Athletes sometimes think coaches only want great performance, don't care about them personally
- Third party can intervene helpfully

### Tom Rushton's Experience

**Past Use:**
- Common part of support staff with national sport federations
- Bring in psychologists before big problems arise
- Help athletes develop "toolkit" of mental skills
- Visualization, coping mechanisms under stress
- Also valuable for coaches

**Relationship Building:**
- Not necessarily brought into ISL team (athletes may have own at home)
- Big bonus: Can work over Zoom remotely
- Relationship already built is positive
- Helpful working with psychologists during practice/training scenarios
- Learn different ways of teaching skills to athletes

**Example:** Worked in Montreal with Veronique Richard on developing teaching methods for athletes

---

## Visualization Techniques

### Types of Visualization

**Tom Rushton's Toolkit Approach:**
- First person perspective
- Third person perspective
- Future tense (imagining positive outcome)
- Analyzing past error or good performance
- Seasonal plan for addressing different scenarios

**Teaching Moments:**
> "If we were coming into a competition where athletes would be likely challenged and beaten, we might in advance rehearse some visualization around how that might go both during the swim and afterwards, just for an example."

**End of Season:**
> "Same with imagining a positive outcome, especially towards the end of the season when you want and expect them to succeed."

### Dave Salo's Daily Practice Use

**Relaxation First:**
> "I started to do it every day my last couple years at USC. These kids are rushing around—busy, going to class, rushing from dining hall to classroom to workout. I use it as a time to bring them down—take a breath, relax, we're not going to rush into this training session yet."

**Re-energize Past Performance:**
- Focus on past performance to re-energize enthusiasm
- Process of complete relaxation
- Dive through that experience
- Bring back euphoric feel of what that felt like
- First person in that stroke, in that finish
- Carry that energy (great swims come with out-of-body type experience)
- Engineer that performance as they go into workout or competition

**Forward-Looking Visualization:**
> "Sometimes very specific to a race they've had in the past, or might be visualizing racing Cal in February where it's raining and the wind sucks and it's cold and we're back in that little corner. Get them to embrace the discomfort that's coming their way so it's not going to be a big surprise."

**Purpose:**
> "So when you arrive at the facility and it is exactly as I've just described, they're comforted with: okay, we've been through this, we know what to encounter."

### Visualization for Coaches

**Dave Salo's Personal Use:**
> "I use visualization for myself. About an hour before I get up out of bed, I visualize what my day is going to be like. I've got to think these things through a little bit (I'm 62 years old now). I visualize what my day is going to look like, what I'm going to do, so I'm more efficient with my time."

**For Practice Planning:**
> "I visualize my training sessions. Kids will tell you the reason I wear sunglasses at practice more often is to close my eyes and visualize what a set is going to look like. I put a lot of details in my sets—I've got to visually think it through to make sure it's going to work and what I'm after."

**Benefits:**
> "So that as I see it being done by the athletes, I can go 'that's what I want' or 'that's not what I want, I want it to look more like this.' It's really helped me be a more efficient coach."

**Coach Recommendation:**
> "Coaches, if you can utilize these skills a lot, it helps them present their methodology a lot more clearly."

---

## Facility Walk-Through Importance

### Tom Rushton's Standard Practice

**ISL Protocol:**
- Do walk-through of area when arriving at facility
- See ready room locations
- See all different places they'll move through
- Production team walks through: first ready room, second ready room, marshaling, camera positions
- Important from production POV but also helps athletes
- Athletes go through visualization process
- Knowing what's going to happen gives sense of comfort

### Dave Salo's Rebecca Soni Lesson

**2008 Olympic Trials - 100 Breast Final:**
> "I didn't give her awareness of what it was going to be like walking out on that deck with 15-20,000 people in the stands, all eyes front and center. As I watched her go out there for that final, you could tell she was a deer in headlights. She was so distracted by this crowd. It was my failure."

**Result:**
- Finished 4th instead of winning
- Apologized to her for not really preparing her
- Not sure how he should have done it, but took it for granted she was prepared
- She was ready—until the bright lights, then it was just scary

**Redemption:**
- Went on to win silver in 100 breast at Olympics
- Won gold in 200 breast at Olympics

**Lesson Learned:**
> "That experience I had to own up to—I let her down. It became part of the process that I would use. You kind of go through that production walk-through so they get comfortable: here's the first ready room, here's the second ready room, you're gonna walk up some stairs into the stadium—be prepared for that."

**Key Point:**
> "Probably one of the most important pieces to preparation for an athlete that sometimes we forget and take it for granted the athletes are all ready to go and can handle that."

---

## Doc Counsilman's Three Pillars of Coaching

### Tom Rushton's Framework

**The Three Essential Pieces:**

**1. Biomechanics:**
- Must be well-schooled in biomechanics
- Know the technique of how to swim well

**2. Physiology:**
- Know the physiology of training
- Know the physiology of the event
- Be able to train athlete to perform under those demands and stress

**3. Psychology:**
> "Coaching is all about psychology—getting people to do things they either don't want to do, or they're not willing to do, or they know they need to do but don't know how to do."

**Bottom Line:**
> "The biggest thing about coaching is the psychology piece."

---

## Communication Skills

### Dave Salo's Evolution

**Late Career Breakthrough:**
> "I've only in the last four or five years really better understood my delivery of information and how to deliver that information such that the athletes will appreciate it, understand it, and be able to utilize that to really test their boundaries."

**Took A While:**
> "It took me a while to understand that. All of us are very demanding coaches—that's why we've been able to get kids to perform as well as they have. But some kids we might lose because they just don't respond to the same kind of skill set that we have in terms of communication."

**Understanding Differences:**
> "Knowing that all of us are very different in how we receive and how we deliver information is really important. I think that's helped me be a better coach. My athletes will tell you that the last three or four years of my career at USC I really got better at it, and the kids appreciated that."

**More Than Just Showing Up:**
> "It's not that you're just a coach showing up on the deck every day giving them great workouts. It's they gotta know that you care about them, and they'll give more effort as they go along."

### Central Governor Theory

**Dave Salo's Explanation:**
> "I'm a big believer in the central government theory of training and fatigue. It's basically the balance you have to overcome: the brain trying to tell your body to quit doing all these extraordinary physical things because it wants to conserve all energy for the brain. You've got to have that constant battle of talking your brain out of talking the body out of doing the work."

---

## Coach-Athlete Relationships

### Professional Boundaries

**Dave Salo's Position:**
> "I used to tell my kids I hate them all equally so that they never felt like they were special."

**Friend vs. Coach:**
> "You've got to be really careful with the friend thing. We're friendly, we care about each other, but you gotta be very careful with that relationship."

**Gender Considerations:**
> "Especially when you're talking about women in particular. You've got to understand—in our sport, women and men are very different. Research and psychologists will tell you that what's important with men is very different from what's important with women."

**Risk of Manipulation:**
> "You gotta be careful that you don't take this relationship and use them to your advantage and disadvantage the athletes."

**Possessiveness Warning:**
> "Gotta be really careful as coach that we don't become so possessive of athletes, possessive of their performance. Our goal is really to be partners with our athletes to help them realize what they're capable of, push them towards that within realistic boundaries."

**More Parental Than Buddies:**
> "We need to be more parental than buddies. You gotta maintain those boundaries and be very very careful with them."

### Athlete Manipulation

**Athletes Can Manipulate:**
> "If you've got an athlete who thinks they're really friendly and tight with the coach, they can manipulate the coach in terms of changing that relationship."

**Standing Firm:**
> "You got to be careful not to let an athlete manipulate you to back off that track. I've had some really really outstanding athletes that I've had to stand firm about what they're going to do and how it's going to be done."

### Trust as Cornerstone

**Especially with Older Athletes:**
> "When you're talking about postgraduate athletes, ISL athletes, you have to sometimes just tell them: 'Look, just don't question, just trust me.'"

**Ous Mellouli Example (2006):**
- Would come in couple days here and there, then disappear
- After about a month, Dave stopped him before he got in

**The Conversation:**
> "I told him: Ous, you can't swim with me anymore. He looked at me like 'what do you mean I can't swim with you?' I said look, I'll coach you and I'll coach you to be really good, but you're going to do it my way. My way is: you come every single day, you do what I ask you to do, and most importantly you just have to trust me."

**Ous's Reaction:**
> "He looked at me like 'are you kidding me? I'm really good.' I said 'we just agreed you have to trust in my ability to coach you, and if you do, I think we're going to go really far.'"

**Result:**
- Went on to win a few gold medals

**Friendship vs. Coaching:**
> "We're friendly and we're friends, but there are times I had to ask him to do things that are very difficult. It's tough to have a friend do something that's hard. But if you're really a coach and you can separate yourself out from that, I think it works."

**Going Toe to Toe:**
> "We've gone to battle with athletes sometimes, and we have to be as unemotional about it as we can be. Otherwise it just doesn't work very well. I've had enough stories—sometimes you win, sometimes you lose, but you got to stand your ground about what you believe as a coach based on all your experiences."

---

## Coaching Talented Athletes

### Will They Be Great Anyway?

**The Myth:**
> "There exists a myth in swimming that there are some people with exceptional talent like Michael Phelps, Caleb Dressel, and the myth goes that no matter which coach these people would come to, they would have been superstars regardless. They're so talented, all they need to do is just swim."

**Dave Salo's Response:**

**Taking Ownership Without Possessing:**
> "What's really important is coaches need to take ownership of responsibility for igniting the flame in our athletes to do what they're capable of doing. Being realistic about what you see and envision and really believe in what it is they can do. But at the same time NOT being possessive over that performance."

**The Problem:**
> "A lot of times coaches get caught up in possessing that performance or possessing that athlete—that only by the grace of God that I'm your coach will that actually happen. I just don't believe that. I think those athletes are going to be great athletes no matter what."

**The Right Link:**
> "But there's a really strong link to the right kind of coach—the coach who doesn't manipulate, doesn't take ownership of all performance."

**Pride in Not Being a One-Hit Wonder:**
> "I'm really proud of the fact that I was not a one-hit wonder. I did not coach just one athlete to gold medal but several athletes to gold medals and world record performances. I'm really proud that I was able to separate myself from their performance to the extent that: boy, I'm really fortunate to coach Ous Mellouli, Rebecca Soni, Jessica Hardy, or all these other athletes. But also knowing that I was an important link to that performance, but NOT that it couldn't have happened without me. That's just silly."

### Biggest Coaching Challenge

**Not the First Olympics:**
> "I've had experiences coaching kids to Olympic performance and gold medal performance. My biggest challenge and fear was not coaching that kid their first Olympics, or coaching that same kid their second Olympics, but coaching that kid who'd been to Olympics and come to you subsequently from another coach, and coach that kid to that performance as well."

**When Coaching Young Athletes:**
> "When I was coaching Aaron Peirsol, Amanda Beard, Michael Cavic, I was always in my mind trying to get them to swim really really well under my coaching so that when they went off to college and swam for Frank Busch, Teri McKeever, Dave Durden, or Eddie Reese, they were receiving from me a really good, challenged athlete who accomplished great performances."

**The Handoff:**
> "Now don't screw it up. My hands are clean. But I just really think a good coach is really important with those athletes that are capable."

**The Truth:**
> "Caleb Dressel would have swum well with anybody. Michael Phelps would have swum well with anybody—as long as that coach had the skills to give them that direction."

### Staying One Chapter Ahead

**Teaching Assistant Analogy:**
> "When I was working on my PhD at USC, I was a teaching assistant in biology lab for a semester. I always told people I just stayed one chapter ahead of the students. If I was a chapter ahead of the students, I'd be okay. If I was a chapter behind and they knew it, I'm screwed. I think that's the way coaching is as well."

**Aaron Peirsol Example:**
> "I always tried to when I had young kids developing, let them know I felt they could go here. Whenever they're ready for that, I'm already there—but not to push them there, but to let them know."

**The Prediction:**
> "Aaron, I think in four years' time you can be at the Olympic Games. I told him that at 13. At that time, thinking a 17-year-old could make the Olympic team was just inconceivable back then. But in fact I did tell Aaron Peirsol I thought he could make the Olympic team four years from when he was 13 because I thought he was that good."

**The Approach:**
> "It wasn't to push him there. It was to let him know I'm a chapter ahead. But he would have been great. He was great with Eddie Reese as well."

---

## Managing Different Athlete Types

### Michael Cavic vs. Aaron Peirsol

**Michael Cavic:**
- Probably one of the most naturally talented athletes Dave ever coached
- Didn't always like to do the work
- Kind of a battle sometimes to get him to work
- Extremely talented

**Aaron Peirsol (same age as Cavic):**
- Would thrive on work
- Do all the work, all the challenges

**Coaching Adjustment:**
> "You had to kind of adjust. That's the thing that's really important to coaches: don't throw away some kid who lacks that desire to train great. You got to find out how to manipulate your training to get them to work hard and do it well."

**Jason Lezak:**
- Also pretty talented
- Didn't really like to work the way Dave wanted him to work
- Had to find ways to get him to work

**Understanding Physiology & Mechanics:**
> "Understanding the physiology, understanding the mechanics, and then influencing how they train consistently enough to drive performance."

### Racing Styles

**Cavic:**
> "When he was up and ready to go, he was really good. When he felt good and ready to go. More like: I'm just gonna try to stay up with me."

**Peirsol:**
> "Aaron would be good when he felt like crap. I don't care where you are, I'm gonna finish off and beat you."

**From Coaching Standpoint:**
> "It was kind of fun, difficult at times to work with such dichotomous, both ends of the spectrum in terms of athletes who could be really good."

---

## Breaststroke Coaching

### Individual Approach

**Core Philosophy:**
> "It really is dependent upon each individual athlete. You work on the strengths that an athlete presents you, and then you try to improve upon the weaknesses."

**What Impacts the Stroke:**
- Timing of the stroke
- Flexibility of ankles, hips, knees, shoulders
- All those components add to a stroke

**Reverse Engineering Problem:**
> "We look at the successful athletes generally, then we take that information, reverse engineer it, and say 'oh that's the key.' With breaststroke, each one of these athletes I think is separate, and you work within the framework of that stroke."

**Not Coaching Everyone the Same:**
> "To think we're all going to coach athletes like [narrow-kicking sprinters] is not really the best route to go for breaststroke."

### Dave's Breaststroke Advantage

**Personal Experience:**
> "I'm fortunate with breaststroke swimmers. I've had a lot of really successful breaststroke swimmers over my career, and I always attributed to the fact that when I was a kid swimming, I was a breaststroker. So I know what it feels like. I can still feel it again when I think through it and visualize it and remember what that's like. I think that's helped me coach breaststroke swimmers—having that kinesthetic sense of 'I get your stroke, I understand it.'"

### Rebecca Soni vs. Yulia Efimova

**Differentiation:**
> "I understand the differentiation between a Rebecca Soni stroke and a Yulia Efimova stroke."

**Efimova's Three Strokes:**
> "When she joined my group and I watched her race, she basically had three very distinct methodologies for swimming breaststroke—very different. She would incorporate these three different skill sets through the course of a 200 breaststroke. We tried to tone her down into more two different strokes, which I think she's mastered pretty well. But yet again, it's her ability to kind of change gears significantly different."

**Rebecca Soni:**
> "Rebecca Soni swam very much like the breaststrokers you see on the men's side now. She does not have a great kick. Her stroke—you'd watch it and it was just like this little thing out in front and this little complimentary kick."

**The Hummingbird:**
> "A coach once referred to speaking about Rebecca Soni: she was kind of the hummingbird of breaststroke swimmers—what she does doesn't make any sense."

**Coaching Approach:**
> "It's not what I would teach. I would never coach advanced breaststroke like Rebecca Soni swam. I would coach Rebecca Soni to really emphasize what worked for her. Same thing with Efimova—I would make a couple little changes but really not change up her stroke too dramatically because it works well for her."

### Adam Peaty Comparison

> "I think Peaty probably swims breaststroke a lot like Rebecca Soni did, and obviously it works really well for Peaty."

### Three Key Components

**Universal Elements (look different for each swimmer):**

**1. The Kick:**
> "I teach my athletes regardless of how they kick to really drive that kick straighter back. Compared to when I was a kid it was 'up, out, together.' Now it's very quick and very rapid, straighter motion backwards. I look for that, but it may look different from Efimova and might look different from Rebecca Soni."

**2. (Implied: The Pull/Scull)**

**3. (Implied: Timing/Coordination)**

---

## Freestyle Technical Discussion

### Leah Smith's Stroke Evolution

**High School:**
- Stroke was a lot more choppy
- Would recover more from the elbow versus the wrist

**Change Over Time:**
> "She has lengthened it out. There are a few different components I look for in each stroke that are the same, but at the same time within the framework of each athlete, they're incorporated just a little bit differently."

**Key Component - Acceleration Past Pockets:**
> "One of them is accelerating that stroke—I call it past the pockets. If you had pockets in your suit, you'd be accelerating past your pockets into the recovery. That's where Leah has changed her stroke dramatically."

**Recovery Change:**
> "She really has exited into the recovery from the wrist a little bit more than what it was when she was in high school, which was more elbow driven."

---

## Freestyle with Dolphin Kick

### The Concept

**Historical Attempts:**
- Michael Klim used it in last 15m before finish
- Alex Popov worked on it but didn't have core flexibility
- Every time he did a stroke to do fly kick, had to readjust body
- Didn't work out for him

**Dave's Experience:**
> "We've done drills like that in practice to kind of engage the core a little bit more. Coordination becomes the key. It's tougher to keep that dolphin kick going as fast as you probably have to to go along with a freestyle pull."

**Not New:**
> "It's not something new, it's something that has been out there before in the past."

### Will It Work?

**Dave's Opinion:**
> "It's like everything. There's going to be a coach out there that incorporates some of these ideas and thinks that's the direction to go, and they'll incorporate it with their athlete and they'll stumble upon an athlete that does it really really well."

**Mechanics Consideration:**
> "It's kind of like that backstroke breakout that some athletes are doing where they're breaking out their arm in front of their body into the recovery. It kind of works. You have to weigh the increase in drag force that accompanies greater force when you pull in front of your body."

**Awkwardness:**
> "I get that, I understand the mechanics of it, but it just seems awkward to roll into the first stroke when you break out."

**Patient Coach Required:**
> "It's really a coach who's patient enough to really push through—keep pushing through—and you're going to find an athlete that will be able to do that."

### Breaststroke Over-Water Recovery Analogy

**Similar Pattern:**
> "Go back years and years ago—it drives me crazy, it still does—when athletes would recover over the water in breaststroke and coaches started to develop drills for recovery over the water."

**The Requirement:**
> "The only way a breaststroke pull recovery over the water works is you've got a really effective kick. If you don't have a very effective kick, your hips drop and your kick drops, and it doesn't make a lot of sense."

**Drill Problem:**
> "Coaches for a while were incorporating these different drills because somebody was successful doing that."

**Rūta Meilutytė Example:**
> "Rūta Meilutytė who's got her elbow recovery—I get it, I understand you reduce the resistance going over the top versus going through the water. But what happens if you don't have a good kick? Hips start to sink, you put a lot of pressure on the lower body, and she gets disqualified a lot of times because she does it."

**Selective Application:**
> "The dolphin kick freestyle thing has probably more chance. Maybe it's selective into a finish or selective into a turn or something like that where you can kind of endure that."

---

## Age-Appropriate Coaching

### Don't Downsize Elite Technique

**The Problem:**
> "A lot of times the developmental coaches who are really motivated to get their kids to learn well—we take an adult elite athlete stroke and try to downsize it to an introductory level of swimming. I think that's the wrong approach. You got to do age-appropriate and skill-appropriate work."

### Butterfly Teaching Example

**Observing Elite Swimmers:**
> "If you watch butterfly swimmers underwater—Michael Phelps or a really good athlete—you'll see there's two strokes per kicks, or two kicks per stroke cycle."

**The Mistake:**
> "You introduce that really early, and what kids do is they STOP their stroke to accommodate these two kicks."

**The Better Way:**
> "I make my age group coaches coach from the hips up instead of from the legs up. What I mean is: don't teach them two kicks per stroke. Teach them to pop the hips on the catch in front and just feel the flow of the stroke. Don't even teach the kick with the stroke."

**Result:**
> "When you do that, kids won't stop in front to kick-pull-stop in back to kick-stop the kick-pull-stop the kick. If you teach them early on that process, it takes them for years to get over that."

**Natural Flow:**
> "If you just teach the hips connected to the upper body, the stroke will flow through. The second kick is natural—it's a very natural flow to the stroke. You don't even teach that."

**Key Lesson:**
> "We try to teach how Michael Phelps swims versus what an eight-year-old should—how the eight-year-old should swim. You gotta be really careful with that kind of stuff with developmental kids."

### The Michael Phelps Two-Kick Problem

**Another Example:**
> "When I was 19 years old, the coach telling me: 'Look at Michael Phelps' video—his two kicks are equal. This is what you have to achieve because this doubles your kick effort. If your first kick is that powerful, your second kick is that powerful, it's twice as fast.'"

**Doesn't Always Work:**
> "But it doesn't always work that way. They can learn that when they're 14, 15, 16—but not when they're seven, eight, nine."

---

## Key Takeaways for Coaches

### Sports Psychology Integration
- Use sports psychologists for communication skills, not just athletes' mental health
- Develop toolbox of mental skills (visualization, coping mechanisms, relaxation)
- Can work remotely via Zoom if relationship established
- Complements coach-athlete relationship rather than replacing it

### Visualization Mastery
- Use daily before practice to calm athletes
- Re-energize past great performances
- Prepare for future discomfort (weather, venue, pressure)
- Coaches should visualize their own practice sessions
- Visualize your day for better time efficiency

### Communication Skills
- Understand you're always different in how you receive and deliver information
- Better communication = better performances
- Athletes need to know you care, not just that you want results
- Last years of USC: Dave got better at this and athletes appreciated it

### Facility Preparation
- Always do walk-through before competition
- Show ready rooms, camera positions, procedure
- Reduces anxiety through familiarity
- Rebecca Soni Olympic Trials lesson: never assume they're ready for bright lights

### Trust Over Friendship
- Be friendly, not friends
- Maintain professional boundaries
- Be more parental than buddy
- Don't let athletes manipulate you
- Don't be possessive of their performance
- Stand firm on what they need to do
- Sometimes: "Just trust me" is enough

### Talent Recognition
- Great athletes will be great regardless
- Right coach is important link, not only possible coach
- Don't be possessive: "only I could coach this athlete"
- Stay one chapter ahead (teaching assistant analogy)
- Let young athletes know their potential without pushing them there
- Biggest challenge: coaching athlete who came from another successful coach

### Individualization in Technique
- Breaststroke especially: each athlete is separate
- Work within framework of their stroke
- Don't reverse engineer success and apply universally
- Know what it feels like (Dave's breaststroke advantage)
- Three key components look different for each swimmer
- Freestyle: accelerate past the pockets into recovery

### Age-Appropriate Development
- Don't downsize elite technique to beginner level
- Do age-appropriate and skill-appropriate work
- Butterfly: teach from hips up, not legs up
- Don't teach two kicks—teach hip pop and flow
- What works for Michael Phelps doesn't work for 8-year-olds
- They can learn advanced concepts at 14-16, not 7-9

### Managing Different Athletes
- Michael Cavic vs Aaron Peirsol: both talented, totally different work ethic
- Don't throw away kid who doesn't like to train
- Find ways to manipulate training to get them to work
- Understand their racing style (front-runner vs closer)
- Adjust coaching to athlete, not athlete to your one method

### Innovation with Patience
- Freestyle with dolphin kick: requires patient coach
- Will work for someone eventually
- Selective application (finish, turn) more likely
- Compare to backstroke breakout or breaststroke recovery variations
- Understand mechanics but don't dismiss because it seems awkward

# Meet Manager Essentials For Coaches

**Sources:** Meet_Manager_1, Meet_Manager_2, HelpManual (Hy-Tek Meet Manager 8.0 Handbook)

## Why Coaches Need to Understand Meet Manager

Meet Manager (by Hy-Tek) is the industry-standard software for running swim meets. As a coach, understanding the basics helps you:

- **Troubleshoot delays** - Know when issues are software vs. timing system
- **Submit accurate entries** - Understand what data is required
- **Work with meet staff** - Speak their language when problems arise
- **Train your team manager** - Help parents/volunteers who run software
- **Get better results** - Know what reports are available (splits, relay exchanges, etc.)

---

## What Is Meet Manager?

**Meet Manager** = Microsoft Access database + custom interface for swim meet operations

**Key functions:**
1. **Pre-meet:** Process entries, seed events, print heat sheets
2. **During meet:** Capture times from timing system, score events, print results
3. **Post-meet:** Generate final results, export data, create records reports

**Current version:** Meet Manager 8.0 (as of 2025, though MM 9.0 in beta)

---

## The Meet Manager Workflow (From Coach's Perspective)

### Phase 1: Pre-Meet (Days/Weeks Before)

**What's happening:**
1. Meet host sets up database (events, teams, rules)
2. Coaches/clubs submit entries (via SWIMS, Team Manager, or manual entry)
3. Meet Manager imports entries, assigns swimmers to heats/lanes
4. Psych sheets generated and published

**Coach's role:**
- Submit accurate entry times (seed times)
- Verify entries in psych sheet (check for errors)
- Report discrepancies to meet director ASAP

### Phase 2: Meet Day Setup (Morning of Session)

**What's happening:**
1. Database loaded onto meet computer
2. Timing system connected (CTS, Daktronics, etc.)
3. Events downloaded to timing system
4. Printers configured
5. Positive check-in processed (if required)

**Coach's role:**
- Check swimmers in (if meet uses positive check-in)
- Submit scratches before deadline
- Verify heat sheets after scratches/seeds finalized

### Phase 3: During Competition

**What's happening:**
1. Timing system sends times to Meet Manager
2. Operator reviews/adjusts times (if needed)
3. Results scored and printed
4. Times posted to scoreboard/live results

**Coach's role:**
- Review heat sheets for lane assignments
- Watch for DQs on scoreboard/results
- Question suspicious times immediately (before next heat)
- Collect results printouts for post-meet analysis

### Phase 4: Post-Meet

**What's happening:**
1. Final results compiled
2. Records checked/verified
3. Data exported for USA Swimming (SWIMS upload)
4. Results posted online

**Coach's role:**
- Download official results from meet website
- Import times into Team Manager (for your club's database)
- Review relay splits, DQs, and improvement metrics

---

## Key Meet Manager Screens (Operator View)

### Main Menu

**Top bar shows:** Current database file name and location

**Key buttons:**
- **Run** - Opens the "Run Screen" for meet operations
- **Teams** - Add/edit teams in meet
- **Athletes** - Add/edit/scratch swimmers
- **Events** - Create/edit event structure
- **Relays** - Create/edit relay entries
- **Reports** - Print heat sheets, results, etc.

### Run Screen (The Heart of Meet Manager)

**What it does:** Real-time meet operations

**Key elements:**
- **Session/Event/Heat selector** - Navigate through meet
- **Get Times button** - Pull times from timing system
- **Lane grid** - Shows swimmer names, seed times, results
- **Timing adjustment tools** - Fix touchpad failures, button errors
- **Score/List button** - Calculate places, print results

**Color coding (helpful for operators):**
- **Green** - Event ready to run
- **Yellow** - Times received, needs review
- **Red** - Timing issue flagged

---

## Common Operations (What You Might See Operators Doing)

### 1. Downloading Events to Timing System

**When:** Before each session starts  
**Why:** Timing system needs to know event distances, relay vs. individual, etc.  
**What it looks like:** Operator clicks "Download Events" → Timing system beeps confirmation

**If this doesn't happen:** Timing system may record wrong distance, or not accept relay exchanges

### 2. Getting Times from Timing System

**When:** After each heat finishes  
**How:** Two methods:
- **Get Times** - Manually click button to import
- **Race Number** - Timing system auto-sends when heat is "stored"

**What can go wrong:**
- Timing system not connected (times don't import)
- Wrong heat selected (times import to wrong swimmers)
- Network lag (times delayed)

### 3. Adjusting Times (Lane/Heat Malfunctions)

**When a touchpad fails:**
- Operator selects "Lane Malfunction"
- Chooses which time source to use (Button A, B, or C)
- System automatically recalculates official time per FINA rules

**When entire heat has issues:**
- "Heat Malfunction" mode
- Operator can manually enter times from backup watches
- Requires referee approval

**Coach's concern:** If you see operator doing lots of adjustments, watch your swimmers' times carefully. Verify they match what you saw on your stopwatch.

### 4. Handling DQs and No-Shows

**DQ (Disqualification):**
- Referee submits DQ slip
- Operator enters DQ code (e.g., S4 = false start, B1 = breaststroke kick)
- Swimmer's time shown as "DQ" with code on results

**NS (No Show):**
- Swimmer didn't compete
- Operator marks "NS" in heat
- Seed time NOT replaced with "NS" (stays as seed for future meets)

**NT (No Time):**
- Swimmer competed but time not recorded (rare)
- Usually due to total timing system failure
- Manual watches used if available

### 5. Scoring and Printing Results

**Scoring:**
- Meet Manager auto-calculates places (1st, 2nd, 3rd...)
- Applies scoring rules (e.g., top 8, dual meet scoring)
- Updates team scores in real-time

**Printing:**
- **Heat results** - Individual heat printout
- **Event results** - All heats combined, sorted by time
- **Cumulative results** - Running team scores
- **Award labels** - For medals/ribbons

---

## What Coaches Should Request from Meet Manager

### Essential Reports

**1. Heat Sheets (Pre-race)**
- Who's in your swimmers' heats
- Lane assignments
- Seed times

**2. Event Results (Post-race)**
- Official times
- Places
- Point values (if scored meet)

**3. Splits (for 200+ events)**
- 50m/100m/150m/200m splits
- Essential for post-race analysis
- **Request specifically** - not always printed by default

**4. Relay Exchange Times**
- Reaction times for each swimmer (r:+0.XX)
- Critical for training relay team
- **Request specifically** - often only printed for finals

### Optional (But Useful) Reports

**5. Team Finish Summary**
- All your swimmers' results in one document
- Great for parent communication

**6. Personal Best Report**
- Highlights swimmers who achieved PRs
- Morale booster for team meetings

**7. Records Report**
- Pool records, team records, age group records
- Automatically generated if records broken

---

## Troubleshooting Common Issues

### "Times Aren't Showing Up"

**Possible causes:**
1. Timing system not connected to Meet Manager
2. Operator forgot to click "Get Times"
3. Network cable unplugged
4. Wrong COM port selected

**Coach action:** Alert operator calmly. Don't panic - times are still on timing system, just need to be imported.

### "My Swimmer's Time Is Wrong"

**Possible causes:**
1. Touchpad failure (button time used instead)
2. Timing adjustment error (operator selected wrong button)
3. Swimmer was in wrong lane (operator imported to wrong name)
4. Your stopwatch was off

**Coach action:**
1. Check timing system printout (ask operator for copy)
2. Compare all three times (touchpad, button A, button B)
3. If discrepancy >0.5s, file inquiry with referee

### "Splits Aren't Printed"

**Cause:** Timing system didn't capture splits, OR operator didn't enable split printing

**Coach action:** Ask operator:
- "Did the timing system record splits for this event?"
- "Can you reprint results with splits included?"

**If splits weren't captured:** Can't be added later. Only option is to review video (if available).

### "DQ Code Is Wrong"

**Example:** Your swimmer got DQ'd for "S4 - False Start" but you saw them turn improperly (should be "T2 - Turn")

**Coach action:**
1. Speak with referee IMMEDIATELY (before swimmer leaves pool deck)
2. Referee can correct DQ code in Meet Manager
3. If corrected AFTER results are printed, request reprint

---

## Meet Manager Database Structure (For Team Managers)

### Key Tables (What's Stored)

**ATHLETE table:**
- Name, age, gender, team
- USA Swimming ID number
- Best times (for seeding)

**EVENT table:**
- Event number, distance, stroke
- Gender, age group
- Scoring rules

**ENTRY table:**
- Links athletes to events
- Seed times
- Relay assignments

**RESULT table:**
- Official times
- Places
- DQ codes

### Why This Matters

**For Team Managers:**
- Understanding structure helps with imports/exports
- Easier to troubleshoot data issues
- Can write custom queries (if SQL-savvy)

**For Coaches:**
- Know that Meet Manager is just a database
- Data can be exported to Excel, Team Manager, etc.
- Results are portable (not locked in proprietary format)

---

## Meet Manager vs. Other Software

### Team Manager (Hy-Tek)

**Purpose:** Club-level database for rosters, times, entries  
**Relationship:** Exports entries TO Meet Manager, imports results FROM Meet Manager  
**Who uses it:** Team administrators, coaches

### SWIMS (USA Swimming)

**Purpose:** National athlete database  
**Relationship:** Meet Manager can upload results directly to SWIMS (for time standards, rankings)  
**Who uses it:** Meet hosts, LSC administrators

### Real-Time Results Software

**Examples:** Meet Mobile, SwimTopia Live Results  
**Purpose:** Publish results to web/mobile during meet  
**Relationship:** Reads Meet Manager database in real-time  
**Who uses it:** Parents, fans, remote coaches

---

## Tips for Coaches Working with Meet Manager Operators

### Do:
✅ Ask politely for specific reports ("Can I get splits for Event 12?")  
✅ Point out discrepancies calmly ("Lane 4's time seems off - can you check?")  
✅ Respect their workflow (wait for lulls between events)  
✅ Offer to help if you're MM-trained

### Don't:
❌ Demand immediate reprints during a session  
❌ Hover over operator's shoulder  
❌ Blame operator for timing system failures  
❌ Ask for changes to seeding after heats are printed

---

## Cross-References

- [[Timing-System-Basics-For-Coaches]] - How times get TO Meet Manager
- [[Meet-Day-Operations-Guide]] - Running smooth meets
- [[SWIMS-Database-Integration]] - USA Swimming systems
- [[Team-Manager-Workflow]] - Club-level software

---

**Coach's Bottom Line:**

> Meet Manager is a tool, not magic. It's only as good as the data it receives (from entries, timing system, operator). Your job: Verify entries before the meet, watch for errors during the meet, and request the data you need (splits, relay exchanges) after the meet.

**Key Coaching Insight:**
- **Meet Manager doesn't CREATE times** - It imports them from the timing system
- **Garbage in, garbage out** - Bad entry times = bad seeding
- **Splits and relay exchanges** - Ask for these specifically; they're not always printed by default
- **Timing adjustments happen** - Touchpad failures are normal; backup systems work

# Building Engaging and Effective Practice

**Source:** *Every Movement Matters* by John O'Sullivan  
**Core Principle:** "Great on paper, s#!% on grass" — Practice design must create transfer, not just repetition  
**Application:** How to design swim practices that develop skill, engagement, and love of the sport

---

## The Central Problem

### The Magic Practice Myth
**O'Sullivan's realization after 30 years of coaching:**

> "I spent my coaching career looking for the magic practice session. I filled binders with Xs and Os. But there was no magic session. The sport-specific knowledge was critical, but NOT SUFFICIENT. The human side came first."

**The truth:**
- There's no "secret workout" that creates champions
- Technique knowledge matters, but it's only 15-20% of what athletes need
- **Coaches are architects of engaging learning environments**, not just transmitters of information

---

## What Athletes Actually Want from Coaches

### Research: Top 5 Qualities Kids Want (Ages 8-18)
**Amanda Visek, George Washington University:**

1. **Respect and encouragement** — 25%
2. **Positive role model** — 20%
3. **Clear, consistent communication** — 20%
4. **Knowledge of the sport** — 15%
5. **Good listener** — 20%

**The gap:** 6.5 million youth coaches in the US—fewer than 1 in 5 get training in motivation and communication (the other 80% of what kids want).

**Result:** 26% dropout rate for athletes coached by untrained volunteers vs. 5% for trained coaches.

---

## The "Architects of Learning" Mindset

### Old Model: Transmitter of Information
❌ Coach knows → Coach tells → Athlete executes → Repeat

**Problems:**
- Passive learning (athletes don't think)
- No transfer to competition (can't adapt)
- Boring (athletes check out mentally)

### New Model: Architect of Environment
✅ Coach designs → Athlete explores → Athlete discovers → Coach guides

**Benefits:**
- Active learning (athletes solve problems)
- Transfer to racing (decision-making under pressure)
- Engaging (athletes are invested)

---

## Principles of Effective Practice Design

### 1. Start Small, Add Gradually
**O'Sullivan's advice:**

> "Imagine if I said, 'Everything you're doing is wrong.' You'd say 'Get out!' But if I said, 'Replace 10 minutes of this with 10 minutes of that,' you'd try it."

**Application for swim coaches:**
- Don't overhaul your entire program at once
- Pick ONE principle from this page
- Try it for 2-4 weeks
- If it works, add another

**Example:**
- Week 1-2: Add music to dryland (energy/joy)
- Week 3-4: Add competitive element to one main set per week
- Week 5-6: Ask athletes "What did you feel?" instead of just telling them what they did wrong

### 2. Create "Safe-to-Fail" Opportunities
**The problem with fear-based coaching:**
- Athletes play it safe
- No experimentation
- No growth

**The solution:**
- Designate some sets as "experiment time"
- "Today, try a different pacing strategy—no consequences if it flops"
- "This is a learning set, not a test set"

**Example: Race Strategy Practice**
- Time trial: 200 IM with 3 different pacing strategies
  - Strategy A: Blast the fly, survive the back/breast
  - Strategy B: Controlled fly, attack the back
  - Strategy C: Even split all 4 strokes
- Debrief: "What did you learn? Which felt best? Why?"
- **No judgment—just discovery**

### 3. Build Engagement Through Variety
**The 3 Killers of Engagement:**
1. **Monotony** — Same sets every week (50 × 100 on 1:20, every Tuesday)
2. **Boredom** — Long, slow, mindless yardage
3. **Disconnection** — No input from athletes, no autonomy

**The Antidotes:**

| Engagement Killer | Solution | Example |
|-------------------|----------|---------|
| **Monotony** | Surprise elements | "Today we're doing something totally different" |
| **Boredom** | Competitive games | Relay races, stroke count challenges, team battles |
| **Disconnection** | Athlete input | "You design the next round—here are the parameters" |

### 4. Make Practice Feel Like Competition
**Pete Carroll's Seahawks principle:** Every rep is a competition

**Swimming applications:**
- **Relay format everything** — Even warm-up can be "first lane to finish wins"
- **Leaderboards** — Track weekly totals (distance, stroke counts, times)
- **Team vs. Team** — Split practice group in half, battle it out
- **Individual challenges** — "Beat your time from 2 weeks ago"

**Why it works:**
- Mimics meet environment (adrenaline, focus)
- Develops competitive instinct
- More engaging than just "grinding yards"

### 5. The 3:1 Positive-to-Corrective Ratio
**Research shows top coaches give 3 positive comments for every 1 corrective.**

**Why:**
- Athletes hear criticism 5x louder than praise
- Constant correction = fear, shutdown, disengagement
- Positive feedback = confidence, openness to coaching

**How to implement:**
- **Catch them doing it RIGHT** — "Great breakout speed on that one!"
- **Praise effort/process, not just results** — "Love the focus on your turns today"
- **Use "yes, and" not "no, but"** — "Yes, your tempo is good, AND let's add more power on the catch"

**Important:** This doesn't mean no correction—just balanced feedback.

---

## Practical Practice Design Strategies

### Strategy 1: The "Why Before What"
**Before every main set, explain the PURPOSE.**

❌ **Old way:**
> "Alright, 20 × 100 on 1:20. Go."

✅ **New way:**
> "Alright, 20 × 100 on 1:20. The purpose is to train your ability to hold speed under fatigue—race pace for the middle 10. This mimics what you'll feel in the 3rd and 4th 100 of your 500. Let's go."

**Why it works:**
- Athletes understand the "why" (not just following orders)
- They can self-regulate better ("Is this race pace? Should I adjust?")
- Builds buy-in and ownership

### Strategy 2: The "Discovery Set"
**Instead of telling, let them discover.**

**Example: Teaching breakout timing**

❌ **Old way:**
> "You're breaking out too early. Break out at the flags."

✅ **New way:**
> "Do 4 × 50 fly. First one: break out at 10m. Second: 12m. Third: 15m. Fourth: 18m. Which one felt fastest? Which one gave you the best momentum into your stroke?"

**Why it works:**
- Athletes FEEL the difference (proprioception)
- They own the discovery ("I found this!")
- More likely to apply it in races (internal vs. external cue)

### Strategy 3: The "Rotate the Challenge"
**Don't just train the same energy system every day.**

**Weekly rotation example:**

| Day | Focus | Set Example |
|-----|-------|-------------|
| **Monday** | Speed/Power | 20 × 25 on :40 — dive 15m FAST, glide to wall |
| **Tuesday** | Threshold | 5 × 400 on 5:00 — hold 200 race pace |
| **Wednesday** | Lactate Tolerance | 8 × 100 on 3:00 — descend 1-4, 5-8 (long rest) |
| **Thursday** | Technique/Efficiency | Stroke count challenge — 12 × 50, can't exceed X strokes |
| **Friday** | Competition Prep | Time trial or relay race day |

**Why it works:**
- Variety = engagement
- Targets different physiological adaptations
- Athletes stay mentally fresh

### Strategy 4: The "Athlete Input" Set
**Once per week, let athletes design part of practice.**

**How it works:**
1. Give parameters: "8-10 × 100, rest interval 1:15-1:30, focus on IM"
2. Athletes decide: Stroke order? Descending? All-out? Negative split?
3. Debrief: "Why did you choose that? How did it feel?"

**Why it works:**
- Ownership → motivation
- Teaches race strategy and self-awareness
- Reveals how athletes think (coaches learn too!)

### Strategy 5: The "Music + Energy" Boost
**Use music strategically to shift energy.**

**When to use music:**
- Dryland (always — pump-up energy)
- Warm-up (upbeat, get them moving)
- Easy recovery sets (chill vibes, mental reset)
- Sprint sets (hype music between reps)

**When NOT to use music:**
- Technical instruction (they need to hear you)
- Race simulation (meets don't have music)

**Pro tip:** Let athletes curate a team playlist (rotates weekly). Builds connection + ownership.

---

## The "Win the Day" Mindset

### Don't Show Up to Win. Show Up to Compete.
**The difference:**

| Mindset | Focus | Result |
|---------|-------|--------|
| **"Show up to win"** | Outcome (times, places) | Fear of failure, playing it safe |
| **"Show up to compete"** | Process (effort, execution) | Freedom to attack, resilience |

**How to train this:**
- Redefine "winning" in practice
  - ✅ "Did you execute your race plan?"
  - ✅ "Did you give max effort?"
  - ✅ "Did you support your teammates?"
  - ❌ "Did you get the fastest time?"

**Example: Time Trial Day**
- Before: "Your goal is to win."
- **Better:** "Your goal is to execute your plan with total focus and courage. The time will follow."

**Why it works:**
- Shifts focus to controllables (effort, strategy)
- Reduces anxiety (not obsessing over outcome)
- Builds resilience (can "win" even with a bad time if process was solid)

---

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

### Mistake 1: Too Much Volume, Not Enough Purpose
**The trap:** "More yardage = better"

**The fix:** Quality > quantity
- Every set should have a clear purpose
- If you can't explain why you're doing it, cut it

**Example:**
- ❌ 5000m straight swim for time (what's the point?)
- ✅ 10 × 400 with specific paces and rest intervals (training threshold)

### Mistake 2: Treating Practice Like Punishment
**The trap:** "You didn't perform, so we're doing extra sets"

**The fix:** Practice is a privilege, not punishment
- If athletes associate training with punishment, they'll burn out
- Consequences for poor effort should be different (conversation, not extra laps)

### Mistake 3: No Athlete Voice
**The trap:** "I'm the coach. I decide everything."

**The fix:** Co-create culture and strategy
- Ask: "What did you feel on that set?"
- Ask: "What do you need from me this week?"
- Ask: "How should we approach this meet?"

**Why it works:**
- Athletes who have voice = athletes who have ownership
- Ownership = motivation = performance

### Mistake 4: Ignoring the Social/Connection Side
**The trap:** "We're here to train, not socialize"

**The fix:** Connection IS training
- High-trust lanes train harder
- Friendships = retention (kids stay in sport)
- Build connection through team activities, partner sets, relay challenges

---

## Measuring Effective Practice

### Ask These 3 Questions After Every Practice:

1. **Did athletes leave better than they arrived?**
   - Physically? (Yes—they trained)
   - Mentally? (Did they learn something?)
   - Emotionally? (Do they feel valued and motivated?)

2. **Would athletes describe today as engaging?**
   - Were they mentally present? Or checking out?
   - Did they have moments of fun/joy?
   - Did they feel challenged (not crushed)?

3. **Can I explain the "why" of every set?**
   - If a swimmer asks "Why are we doing this?", can I answer in 1-2 sentences?
   - Does the set align with our season goals?

---

## Key Takeaways

✅ **No magic practice exists**—focus on the human side first  
✅ **Coaches are architects**, not just instructors  
✅ **Athletes want respect, communication, and role models** (80% of what matters)  
✅ **Start small, add gradually**—don't overhaul everything at once  
✅ **Create safe-to-fail opportunities**—learning > perfection  
✅ **Variety + competition = engagement**  
✅ **3:1 positive-to-corrective ratio**—catch them doing it right  
✅ **Explain the "why"**—athletes who understand train smarter  
✅ **Let athletes discover**—internal learning > external instruction  
✅ **"Show up to compete," not "show up to win"**—process > outcome

---

## Implementation Checklist

**This week, try ONE of these:**

□ Add music to one part of practice  
□ Turn one set into a competition (relay, team battle, stroke count challenge)  
□ Ask "What did you feel?" instead of immediately telling them what they did  
□ Explain the "why" before a main set  
□ Let athletes design one round of a set  
□ Give 3 positive comments for every 1 corrective  
□ Create a "safe-to-fail" experiment set  

**Next month, add 2-3 more.**

---

2. Do your athletes know the "why" behind your main sets?
3. On a scale of 1-10, how ENGAGING is your typical practice?
4. How often do you give athletes a voice in practice design?
5. What's ONE thing you could change this week to make practice more effective?

# Athlete-Centered Coaching for Swim Coaches

**Source:** *Every Movement Matters* by John O'Sullivan  
**Key Insight:** "You coach a person, not a sport"  
**Application:** How elite swim coaches can shift from instruction-focused to athlete-development-focused coaching

---

## Core Principle

**Quality coaching** isn't just about wins—it's about the consistent application of knowledge to improve athletes':
- **Competence** (technical/tactical skill)
- **Confidence** (self-belief, resilience)
- **Connection** (social bonds)
- **Character** (empathy, integrity)

### The Steve Kerr Lesson

When Steve Kerr became Warriors head coach, Pete Carroll asked: *"What will practice FEEL like? What will players feel walking into your building?"*

**Carroll's assignment:** Identify your 4 core values—then build EVERY practice around them.

**Kerr's 4 values:**
1. **Joy** — Sports should be fun
2. **Competitiveness** — The goal is to win
3. **Compassion** — Everyone is struggling with something
4. **Mindfulness** — Clarity = performance

**Result:** 3 NBA championships in 5 years, built on culture first

---

## The Quality Coaching Framework

### 1. Contextual Fit
**Don't copy-paste pro workouts for age-groupers.**

✅ **DO:** Adapt elite concepts to your swimmers' ages, stages, and needs  
❌ **DON'T:** Run Bowman's Phelps sets with 12-year-olds verbatim

### 2. Athlete-Centered Outcomes (The 4 Cs)

| Outcome | What It Means | Swimming Application |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Competence** | Technical/tactical mastery | Stroke refinement, race strategy, turns |
| **Confidence** | Self-belief + resilience | Handling bad swims, pre-race mindset |
| **Connection** | Social bonds | Lane culture, relay teams, mentorship |
| **Character** | Integrity, respect | Team values, sportsmanship, leadership |

**Key question:** Are you developing ALL 4, or just competence?

### 3. Essential Coaching Knowledge

**Three layers:**
1. **Professional** — Know your sport (technique, physiology, tactics)
2. **Interpersonal** — Understand how people interact (communication, team dynamics)
3. **Intrapersonal** — Know YOURSELF (your triggers, biases, purpose)

**Most coaches master #1. Elite coaches master #2 and #3.**

---

## React vs. Respond

### The Mirror Neuron Problem
When watching "your" swimmers race, your brain's neutral decision-making shuts down. You react as if YOU are swimming.

**This is why coaches (and parents) lose it at meets.**

### E + R = O (Urban Meyer)
**Event + Response = Outcome**

- We don't control events (bad calls, DQs, slow swims)
- We DO control our response
- Response > Event in determining outcomes

### The Iceberg Model

```
PERSONALITY (above water) → How we REACT
↓
CHARACTER (below water) → How we RESPOND
```

**Character = culture + experience + training + values**

**Goal:** Train yourself to respond, not react

---

## Know Your Why: Defining Your Coaching Purpose

### Why It Matters
**Simon Sinek:** "People don't buy what you do—they buy WHY you do it."

**Without a clear why:**
- Coaching becomes transactional
- Winning at all costs tramples athletes
- Burnout is inevitable

**With a clear why:**
- You have a compass (even with a bad map)
- Decisions align with purpose
- Athletes feel it and buy in

### Creating Your Coaching Purpose Statement

**Joe Ehrmann's 4 Questions:**
1. Why do I coach?
2. How do I coach?
3. How does it FEEL to be coached by me?
4. How do I define success?

**Example purpose statements:**

> "I coach to help young athletes discover their potential through discipline, connection, and joy—preparing them not just to swim fast, but to thrive in life."

> "My purpose is to create an environment where swimmers feel valued, challenged, and supported—where every moment matters and every athlete belongs."

**Action:** Write your 1-2 sentence purpose statement. Post it where you'll see it daily.

---

## Practical Applications for Swim Coaches

### 1. Start Practice with Purpose
**Before warmup:** Remind swimmers (and yourself) of the day's focus—not just sets, but the VALUES being trained.

Example:
- "Today we're training competitiveness AND connection. Push each other, celebrate each other."

### 2. The "Rule of One"
**Create ONE extraordinary moment per swimmer, per season.**

- A handwritten note after a breakthrough
- A surprise acknowledgment in front of the team
- A 1-on-1 conversation about their dreams

**Why:** Athletes remember how you made them FEEL, not the sets you wrote.

### 3. Shift from "Coaching at" to "Coaching with"
**Old model:** Coach talks → athletes execute  
**Athlete-centered model:** Coach asks → athletes discover → coach guides

**In practice:**
- "What did you feel on that 50?" (not "You broke out too early")
- "What adjustments can we make for the next round?" (not "Here's what you'll do")

### 4. The 10-Minute Check-In
**Weekly:** Spend 10 minutes with each swimmer outside practice.

Ask:
- How are you feeling?
- What's going well?
- What's challenging?
- What do you need from me?

**Result:** Connection, trust, and early problem-solving

### 5. Culture Audit
**Monthly reflection:**
1. What do my swimmers FEEL when they walk on deck?
2. Am I developing all 4 Cs (not just competence)?
3. Are my actions aligned with my stated values?
4. Am I reacting or responding?

---

## The John O'Sullivan Story: Steve's Last Game

**The wake-up call:**

O'Sullivan was verbally destroying "Steve" (not his real name) in a meaningless regional game. A teammate, Evan, tapped his shoulder:

> "Coach, Steve isn't playing next year—mostly because of how you treat him. This is his last game with us since he was 9. You should put him back in."

**The realization:**
- Every word matters
- Every moment matters
- Athletes remember how we made them feel

**The change:** O'Sullivan apologized, put Steve back in, let him finish with his friends.

**The lesson:** "That's when I realized coaching isn't a job. It's a calling. And if I was going to be a better coach, I had to be a better me."

---

## Key Takeaways

✅ **Coaching is a relationship business**, not just an Xs & Os business  
✅ **Athletes won't care what you know until they know you care**  
✅ **Quality = Competence + Confidence + Connection + Character**  
✅ **Your values must show up in EVERY practice and interaction**  
✅ **Train yourself to respond (character) vs. react (personality)**  
✅ **Define your WHY—it's your compass**  
✅ **Create extraordinary moments—they last a lifetime**

---

- [Championship Characteristics](../coaches/bob-bowman/championship-characteristics.md) — Daily habits

## Discussion Questions for Coaches

1. What are YOUR 4 core values? Can your swimmers name them?
2. When did you last REACT in a way you regret? What triggered it?
3. What does practice FEEL like for your athletes right now?
4. Are you developing all 4 Cs, or just competence?
5. What's your coaching purpose statement?

*"To be a better coach, you have got to be a better you."* — Joe Ehrmann
