# Starts, Turns, and Finishes: Montgomery's Competition Essentials
**Source:** *Mastering Swimming* by Jim Montgomery & Mo Chambers, Chapter 8 (2009)  
**Context:** Comprehensive guide to maximizing pool performance at walls  
**Audience:** Competitive masters swimmers, workout swimmers improving turns, coaches

---

## The 30% Rule

> "In a 25-yard (or 25m) pool, approximately **one-third of a competitive swimmer's time** is spent moving from the flags to the wall and back to the flags."

**Therefore:** Swimmers who polish starts, turns, finishes gain significant advantage.

**Benefit for non-competitors:** More efficient turns = improved workout quality, faster swimming, better momentum maintenance.

---

## Core Philosophy: Maintaining Momentum

### Why Swimmers Are Different

**Unlike running, cycling, speed skating:**
- Other athletes strive for maximum speed in first moments
- Swimmers reach top speed in **hundredths of second** diving from starting block

**Main objectives of start:**
1. Transition dive speed into water
2. Carry momentum into swimming speed

**Elements affecting start quality:**
- Body position in air
- Entry into water
- Quality of underwater streamline
- Timing of initial kicks and first strokes

### The Push-Off Principle

**After turn:** Explosive leg drive + tight torpedolike streamline = **Faster than swimming on surface**

**Seamless transition requires:**
- Maintain momentum of push-off
- Hold underwater streamline
- Break out smoothly into stroke rhythm

---

## STARTING PROTOCOL (Universal)

**Consistency = Mental Freedom**

Same procedure age-group → masters → Olympics  
Knowing what to expect → Focus on race strategy

### The Sequence

**1. Short Whistles**
Deck referee signals swimmers to ready:
- Remove T-shirts, sweats
- Final adjustments to caps, goggles
- Give attention to referee
- May announce event and heat

**2. Long Whistle**
Signals step onto starting block:
- Place one or both feet at front of block
- **Option:** Position adjacent to block with feet at pool edge
- **Or:** Enter water feet-first (with referee approval), place one hand on edge

**Backstroke specific:**
- All swimmers enter water at first long whistle
- Second long whistle signals positioning at block

**3. "Take Your Mark"**
Starter takes control:
- Swimmers move into starting position
- Await starting signal

**4. Starting Signal**
- Whistle
- Gun
- Electronic beep (most common)

**Race begins.**

---

## FORWARD START (Freestyle, Breaststroke, Butterfly)

### Safety First!

**Requirements:**
- Pool specifically designed for competitive springboard diving
- Depth approved for competitive diving
- Minimum 15 meters unobstructed water
- Dive away from sidewalls and shallow areas
- Starting blocks secured with nonskid surface
- Direct supervision by coach or lifeguard
- 6 feet (2m) depth MINIMUM

**Warning:** "Diving into shallow water is dangerous and may result in permanent spinal cord injury or possible death."

### Learning Progression (Step-by-Step)

#### Step 1: Perfect the Streamline

**On pool deck or shallow water:**
1. Place one hand over other
2. **Two squeezes:**
   - Thumb of top hand around pinkie side of bottom hand
   - Ears squeezed between biceps
3. Align spine:
   - Move chin away from chest
   - Contract abdomen and buttocks
   - Feet together
4. Reach fingertips to sky
5. Have partner help with alignment
6. Raise shoulders toward ears (further streamline)

**Lie down on deck** with toes pointed to perfect position.

#### Step 2: Dive From Pool Deck

**Practice in deep water:**
- Maintain streamlined body position throughout dive
- Imagine whole body entering through **single hole** created by fingertips
- Visualize diving through hula hoop on water surface without touching sides

**Progression:**
- Move imaginary hula hoop farther as confidence grows
- Resist temptation to look up (increases frontal resistance, hinders momentum)
- If goggles leak/slip: Tighten them

**Focus:**
- Hands together
- Body streamlined
- Head and neck aligned with spine

#### Step 3: Standing Broad Jump (From Block)

**Position:**
- Feet almost shoulder-width apart
- Toes just over front edge

**Action:**
- Jump from blocks into water feet first
- Maximize distance using hip and leg muscles
- Swing arms to increase momentum
- Explode off blocks

#### Step 4: Swinging Dive

**Position:** Front of starting block (same as Step 3)

**Action:**
1. Imagine hula hoop on water surface in front
2. Swing arms up, forward, around (create momentum)
3. Dive from block
4. Pierce center of hoop with fingertips
5. Keep body tight and streamlined
6. Body follows fingertips through center of hoop

#### Step 5: Transition to Breakout

**Build on swinging dive:**
- Maintain underwater streamlined position until breakout
- **Breakout** = Transition from underwater phase to full-stroke swimming
- When done well: Carries momentum seamlessly into stroke rhythm

See specific breakout sections for stroke details.

#### Step 6: Take Your Mark (Track Start)

**Why track start?**
- Fast
- Suitable for limited flexibility
- More stability
- **Recommended for new swimmers**

**Alternative:** Grab start (both feet front, grab block inside or outside feet) - Experienced swimmers may prefer traditional method.

**Track Start Position (Preferred):**

**At long whistle:**
1. Step onto block
2. Position at front edge:
   - Feet almost shoulder-width apart
   - Toes over edge
   - Eyes focused down pool

**At "Take Your Mark":**
1. Move one foot back on block
2. Bend down
3. Grab underside of block with both hands
4. Keep elbows slightly bent, arms tensed
5. Pull back from block for balance
6. **Back leg:** Bend to at least 90°, lift heel slightly
7. **Front leg:** Bend slightly
8. Draw weight forward (shoulders and arms)
9. **Center of gravity:** Held over ball of front foot
10. Head down (don't rest chest on legs)

**Variations (Flexibility/Preference):**
- Back foot farther back, hips lower (limited flexibility)
- Hands free for relay-style start (some prefer)
- Weight neutral or leaning back over back foot (preference)

**Practice:** Hold position for at least 2 seconds. Experiment with foot and hand positioning.

#### Step 7: GO!

**At starting beep:**
1. Pull body forward slightly (using underside of block)
2. Shifts center of gravity over water
3. Feel yourself falling forward
4. Push with legs
5. Body unfolds over water
6. Assume streamlined position
7. Imagine whole body shooting through narrow tunnel of air
8. Into hole in center of hula hoop

**Key:** Become comfortable with ENTIRE sequence (commands → breakout)

**Practice approach:**
1. Review steps slowly
2. Become comfortable
3. Add power to leg drive
4. Visualize exploding from starting position

---

## BACKSTROKE START

### Similarities to Forward Start

**Same hallmarks:**
- Explosive leg drive
- Streamlined body position
- Movement through single hole in water
- Streamlined underwater kicking
- Breakout strokes

### Skills Progression

**Perform in 6+ feet (2m) depth**

**Skills 1-5:** Hold pool edge or gutter  
**Skills 6-7:** Use starting block handles

#### Skill 1: Ready Position

**Setup:**
1. In pool
2. Hold pool edge/gutter with both hands
3. Position feet on wall:
   - Shoulder-width apart
   - Just under water surface
   - Most prefer staggered feet (choose comfortable position)
4. Head and neck aligned with spine
5. Knees bent to 90° (sitting position)

#### Skill 2: L-Seats

**From ready position:**
1. Drive with legs
2. Keep arms at sides
3. Shoot up and over water
4. Form L shape with body in air
5. Land buttocks first in pool

**Focus:** Leg thrust that gets you up, out, over water

#### Skill 3: L-Seats With Leg Kick

**Repeat Skill 2 + Add:**
- Upward kick with both feet as you enter water

#### Skill 4: Streamline the Entry

**Repeat Skill 3 + Add arm motion:**

**As you drive legs and lift hips:**
- Throw arms back to sides OR over head
- Into streamlined position
- Hands enter water first (create single hole)
- Maintain streamlined position
- Whole body follows fingertips through hole

**If landing on back or dragging:** Focus on:
- Driving legs
- Arching back
- Thrusting legs up before entry

**Underwater phase:**
- Maintain streamline
- Feel yourself losing momentum
- Begin underwater dolphin kick OR flutter kick
- Surface for breakout
- **High-amplitude underwater dolphin** = "Double-leg kick"

**Breathing tip:** Blow water out of nose (keep nose clear on back underwater)

#### Skill 5: Take Your Mark

**Move to starting block when comfortable**

**Position:**
1. Hands on handle of starting block
2. Feet shoulder-width apart on pool wall
3. **USS and USMS rules:** Feet completely submerged
4. Stay relaxed and focused

**At "Take Your Mark":**
- Pull head closer to block
- Bring knees into 90° position
- Buttocks held away from wall

#### Skill 6: GO!

**At starting signal:**
1. Drive with legs
2. Lift hips and body up, out, over water
3. Throw arms back into streamline
4. **Ideal:** Body arched over water
5. Poised for clean entry through single hole created by fingertips

**Practice drill:** Place soft kickboard or pool noodle behind you, practice getting buttocks over board.

---

### Finding What Works

**Experiment with:**
- Foot positioning
- Hand positioning

**Some swimmers:** More success using pool edge/gutter than starting block

**Challenge:** Learning to arch body over water (most swimmers master with time invested in progression)

---

## RELAY START

### Why It's Exciting

**Most exciting portion of meet:**
- Competitors AND spectators
- Critical timing of relay exchanges
- Close races become more exciting
- Minimize exchange time

### Roles in Exchange

**Two swimmers, different responsibilities:**

**Outgoing swimmer:**
- Time movements to leave block exact moment incoming swimmer touches
- Gain momentum from arm swing
- Begin movement BEFORE incoming touch (unlike forward start)

**Incoming swimmer:**
- Maintain stroke tempo and rhythm until finish
- Any deceleration → Early/false start → Disqualification

### Position in Relay

**First swimmer:**
- Forward start (freestyle relay)
- In-water backstroke start (medley relay)

**Swimmers 2, 3, 4:**
- Relay start (faster than forward start)

### The Rhythm Factor

> "Rhythm and timing go hand in hand in successful relay exchanges."

**Key:** Outgoing swimmer synchronized movements to incoming swimmer's stroke tempo and speed.

**Problem:** Incoming swimmer deceleration → Early start → DQ

**Solution:** Practice makes timing.

### Skills Progression

**Focus:** Falling into rhythm of movements.

**Result:**
- More timely exchange
- Gain momentum and power off block

#### Skill 1: Get Into Rhythm (On Deck)

**Away from pool:**
1. Stand straight up
2. One foot forward, one back
3. Bring back foot forward
4. Swing arms forward, up, around
5. Use arm momentum to jump straight up
6. Jump uses both feet (standing broad jump feel)

**Maximize height:**
- Bend knees as you swing hands
- Lower body
- Feet little less than shoulder-width for takeoff
- **Don't** bend knees past 90°

**Sequence:** Step, swing, squat, jump  
**Repeat** until comfortable with timing and rhythm

#### Skill 2: Off the Blocks

**At front of block:**
1. Toes of one foot just over edge
2. Other foot back

**Diving sequence (same as deck):**
- Use arm swing momentum
- Move center of gravity forward over water
- Leave block
- Maintain arm swing momentum
- Throw arms forward
- Fingers pointing at entry spot

#### Skill 3: Streamline Your Entry

**Clean entry with minimum frontal resistance:**
- Streamline body
- Body follows fingertips through hole created in water

**Experiment:**
- Timing of knee bend
- Depth of knee bend
- Maximize power

**Keys:**
- Keep shoulders low
- Momentum moving forward
- Resist temptation to stand up

#### Skill 4: Time the Start

**Once comfortable with rhythm:**

**Synchronize with incoming swimmers:**
1. Stretch arms out in front
2. Follow incoming swimmer with fingertips
3. Allow body to drop lower as they move closer

**Timing by stroke:**
- **Freestyle/Backstroke:** Begin step-and-swing as incoming swimmer begins final stroke to wall
- **Butterfly/Breaststroke:** More difficult to time finish (swimmers more prone to mistiming)

**If uncertain about incoming swimmer's rhythm:** Conservative relay exchange recommended.

### Team Responsibility

**Relays = TRUE team events**

Every swimmer contributes to exchange success:
1. **Incoming swimmer:** Consistent stroke rhythm to finish
2. **Outgoing swimmer:** Study incoming rhythm, time perfectly
3. **Team:** Practice together when possible

**Reality for masters:** Can't always afford extended practice time → Choose more conservative exchanges.

---

## TURNS: The Four Phases

### Beyond Just the Wall

**Turn = More than what happens at wall**

**Four critical phases:**
1. **The approach**
2. **The turn itself**
3. **The push-off and streamline**
4. **The breakout**

**All four phases critical for maintaining momentum.**

### Learning Approach

**First:** Focus on technical aspects of each phase (slower speeds)  
**Then:** Increase turning speed

**Why:** Mastering mechanics at slower speeds → Individual phases blend together smoothly.

---

## FREESTYLE FLIP TURN

### Why Learn It?

**Benefits:**
- Raises workout quality
- Improves momentum to/from walls
- Less shoulder stress than open turn (touch-and-go)
- Faster overall

**With consistent practice:** Master the flip, improve speed and flow.

### Flip Turn Progression

**Become comfortable with each skill before next.**

#### Skill 1: Bubbles

**Essential:** Must expel air through nostrils during flip turn OR water goes up nose.

**Practice:** Get comfortable blowing bubbles out nose while underwater.

#### Skill 2: Somersault

**Purpose:** Comfortable with flipping part

**Remember:** Blow bubbles from nose as body flips

**How to:**
1. Lie on water surface, face down
2. Arms at sides, palms facing pool bottom
3. Tuck into ball:
   - Nose toward knees
   - Heels toward buttocks
4. Sweep arms over head in large, powerful circle
   - Like pushing tall stovepipe hat off head
5. Keep elbows in front of face (where you can see them)
6. Flip completely over
7. End: Lying on back, fingertips pointed same direction you started

**If trouble completing flips:**
- Probably rolling to side during somersault
- Prevents hips from going straight over shoulders
- **Fix:** Focus on aligning head, neck, spine

**Alternative:** Hold pull buoy or kickboard in each hand on surface during flip (gives body sensation of coming over top)

#### Skill 3: Approach to Midpool (NOT at wall yet)

**Adds approach strokes to flip**

**Process:**
1. Swim 4-5 strokes
2. Into head-lead position
   - Arms at sides
   - Palms facing down
3. Press chest slightly under water
4. Do the flip from Skill 2

**Common error:** Rushing into flip before both hands back at sides

**Must be:**
- Submerged position at flip start
- Arms at sides
- Palms down
- Chest pressed down slightly

#### Skill 4: Approach to Midpool + Streamline

**Also away from wall + Adds streamline**

**Process:**
1. Swim 4-5 strokes
2. Head-lead position (arms at sides, palms down)
3. Submerge
4. Flip
5. Move arms into streamline (after knocking off stovepipe hat)

**Finish:** Lying on back with arms in tight streamline

**Repeat several times:** Perfect streamlined finish
- Align head, neck, spine
- Engage core muscles

#### Skill 5: Add the Wall

**Goal:** Learn to judge when to initiate flip

**Target:** When coming through flip into streamline:
- Both feet firmly planted on wall
- Knees bent to 90°
- Poised for effective push-off

**Process:**
1. Start past backstroke flags
2. Swim toward wall
3. Use underwater markings on wall to judge distance
4. Move into head-lead on final stroke
5. Submerge
6. Flip into streamline
7. Plant feet on wall

**Learning curve:**
- Requires trial and error
- Consistent practice
- Swimmers make all kinds of mistakes:
  - Come up in next lane
  - Miss wall completely
  - Hit buttocks on wall

**With practice:** All swimmers can successfully master flip turn.

#### Skill 6: Approach, Flip, Streamline + PUSH-OFF

**Once comfortable judging distance:**

**Add the push-off:**
1. Approach wall
2. Submerge chest
3. Flip into streamline
4. Drive with legs
5. Rotate body to side

**Critical moment:** Between foot plant and push-off
- Slow to streamline → Lose momentum
- Body drops lower
- Resulting push-off weak and often too deep

**Think:** "Pool wall is hot stove"
- Quick touch-and-go
- Fast transition: flip → streamline → push-off

#### Skill 7: Complete Turn (Approach, Flip, Streamline, Push-off, BREAK OUT)

**Final step:** Rotation to abdomen

**Process:**
1. Approach wall
2. Submerge into flip
3. Transition to streamline
4. Push-off
5. Rotate to side
6. **Finally:** Rotate onto front

**Add kicks (as you rotate):**
- Deep dolphin OR freestyle kicks
- Maintain momentum from push-off
- Type and amount depend on:
  - Your strengths
  - Chosen distance

**Sprinters with strong dolphin:** Maximize underwater time (several dolphin kicks)  
**Distance swimmers with weak kicks:** Better to swim on surface

**As nearing surface:**
- Keep stroke long
- Maintain strong body line
- First two strokes WITHOUT breathing
- Settle into stroke rhythm

---

### Flip Turn Tips & Ideas

**Practice opportunities:** EVERY wall approach (flip instead of open turn)

**Gradual integration:**
- Start flipping only one end of pool
- Gradually build to flipping all turns

**Focus on phases separately:** Link together slowly into faster, more elegant turn.

**Additional tips:**

✅ **Maintain/increase speed approaching wall** (Don't slow down)  
✅ **Keep head down** (Don't take final breath or lift head)  
✅ **Eyes on underwater target**  
✅ **Don't roll to side approaching wall**  
✅ **Press chest down** to lift hips, spring heels toward wall  
✅ **"Rush hips to wall"** (engage abdominal muscles)  
✅ **Sweep hands toward head BEFORE feet hit wall** (elbows close together, hands into streamline early)  
✅ **Positioned to push off moment feet contact wall**  
✅ **Start kicking** as soon as rotated onto front (5-6 feet from wall)  
✅ **Maintain momentum** by kicking through breakout into swimming  

❌ **Don't bring hands/arms down or outside body line** (delays turn, must realign before push-off)  
❌ **If push-off too deep** (feel like submarine): Starting flip too close to wall, check for 90° knee bend at foot plant  

---

## BACKSTROKE FLIP TURN

### Once You've Mastered Freestyle Flip

**Easy transition to backstroke turn**

**Difference:** Only in approach and breakout phases

**Skills progression:** Focuses mainly on these two phases + carrying stroke rhythm through flip and streamline.

### Skills Progression

#### Skill 1: Begin Approach Midpool (NOT at wall)

**Start in middle of pool:**
1. Swim backstroke
   - Left arm up
   - Right arm at side
2. Count hand hits (hand entries) as you go
3. On fifth stroke:
   - Move right arm across body over left shoulder
   - Roll onto front
   - Pull through into head-lead position of freestyle flip turn

**Practice:** Several times with BOTH right and left arms (comfortable crossing over and rolling to either side)

#### Skill 2: Know Your Finishing Stroke Count

**From backstroke flags to wall:**
- Count your strokes
- Begin swimming several strokes outside flags
- Reach normal stroke tempo when passing under flags
- Begin counting

#### Skill 3: Transition From Approach to Flip

**Process:**
1. Position outside flags
2. Swim toward wall
3. Count strokes as you pass under flags
4. Begin crossover **TWO strokes BEFORE wall**
   - Example: Stroke count = 5 strokes
   - Begin crossover and roll stroke after 3 strokes
5. Once on front and pulling through (arm that crossed over)
6. Begin flip
7. Plant feet on wall

**Check position:**
- 90° knee bend at foot plant
- Streamlined upper body
- Like freestyle flip turn

**Practice:** Stroking into wall and making flip while crossing over to BOTH left and right sides.

#### Skill 4: Transition From Streamline to Breakout

**Before combining with flip:**

**Practice alone:**
1. Push off center of wall
2. Extend arms and legs (tight streamlined position)
3. Remember: Exhale through nose
4. Choice of kicks:
   - Traditional backstroke kick OR
   - Dolphin kick
5. Maintain momentum of push-off
6. Begin first breakout stroke when head ~1 foot (3m) below surface
7. Follow with second stroke just as head about to break surface
8. Reach surface:
   - Keep kick steady
   - Maintain long body line
   - Settle into stroke rhythm

**Competition rule:** Head must break surface within 15 meters of wall (typically marked on lane lines)

**Experiment:** Find ideal amount of kicking

**Strong dolphin kickers:** 3+ underwater dolphin kicks before two breakout strokes  
**Weaker kicks:** Transition into backstroke kick more quickly

#### Skill 5: Complete Backstroke Turn (Approach, Flip, Streamline, Break Out)

**Adds breakout to complete turn**

**As in freestyle flip turn:**
- Focus on maintaining momentum into flip
- Exploding off wall
- Through underwater phase
- Breakout
- Back to swimming

**Resist temptation:**
❌ Drop chin  
❌ Lift head to look around  

**Why:** Breaking head alignment:
- Slows momentum
- Disrupts stroke count consistency during approach
- Increases resistance leaving wall

---

## OPEN TURNS (Breaststroke & Butterfly)

### Why Different?

**Rules requirement:**
- Breaststroke and butterfly: Must touch wall with BOTH hands simultaneously
- Cannot use traditional flip turns (feet touch first)
- Use open turn (nonflip turn)

**Same success factors:**
- Maintain stroke rhythm and momentum
- Reaching wall
- Throughout turn, streamline, breakout

### Skills Progression

**First steps:** Ready position (swimmer's position on wall preparing to push off)

**Important:** Leave wall from this position for EVERY repetition in workout
- Transitions smoothly into streamlined push-off
- Reinforces mechanics of good short-axis turns

#### Skill 1: Assume Ready Position

**Setup:**
1. Extend wall-side arm, hold pool edge/gutter
2. Extend other arm underwater toward opposite end (palm facing up)
3. Plant balls of feet on wall
4. Knees flexed but relaxed
5. Chin over wall-side shoulder
6. Look down arm at wall

**Visualize:** All body parts between two hands connected in single line:
- Hand on wall
- Elbow
- Chin
- Shoulder
- Elbow in water
- Other hand

#### Skill 2: Push Off and Streamline

**From ready position:**
1. Move hand on wall toward ear ("answering phone")
   - Think: "Call the police"
2. Body drops underwater
3. Slide hand past ear
4. Over head
5. Toward outstretched hand
6. Form streamlined position

**Then:**
- Drive with legs
- Push off wall

**Practice:** Transition from ready position → streamline → push-off until comfortable

**Challenge yourself:** See how much distance you gain moving from wall.

#### Skill 3: Approach

**Swim toward wall:**
- Maintain stroke rhythm and speed
- Getting close: Keep head in line with spine
- Extend arms, reach for wall
- **Ideal:** Body stretched out on water surface completing kick as hands touch wall

**With practice:** Judge distance several strokes away, adjust stroke length to achieve optimum body position at every wall (including finish).

#### Skill 4: Approach to Ready Position

**Process:**
1. Swim to wall
2. Touch with arms extended
3. Begin turn: Draw one arm back (leading with elbow) - "Elbow the robber"
4. Same time: Bring knees up toward chest
5. Position feet on wall (pointing either to side or diagonally up)

**Keys:**
- Maintain momentum and speed
- Make body small as you spin
- Keep center of gravity low
- Think: "Call the police"
- Move into ready position:
  - Turn palm up
  - Fully extend arm toward opposite end

**Practice:** Several times, FREEZE when reaching ready position.

#### Skill 5: Approach to Turn to Push Off

**Complete two-hand touch turn sequence:**

**Cues to connect pieces:**
1. Swim to wall
2. Touch with two hands
3. Pull knees up, elbow back ("Elbow the robber")
4. Move wall-side hand up ("Call the police")
5. Streamline
6. Push off

---

### BUTTERFLY BREAKOUTS

**From ready position:**
1. Push-off and streamline
2. Begin dolphin kick
3. Maintain tight streamline underwater
4. Build speed
5. Aggressive kick on breakout stroke (just before back breaks surface)
6. Keep head in line with spine on first stroke
7. Begin breathing in rhythm of stroke

**Competition rule:** Must break surface within 15 meters of wall.

---

### BREASTSTROKE BREAKOUTS (The Pull-Out)

**Competition allowance:** One pull, one breaststroke kick, one dolphin kick underwater after start and after each turn.

**Rule:** Head must break water surface before beginning sweep-in of next arm pull.

**Commonly called:** Breaststroke pull-out

**Purpose:** Smooth transition from wall into stroke rhythm.

#### The Pull-Out Sequence

**1. Push-off from ready position** (tight streamline)

**2. As momentum slowing:**
- Begin underwater arm pull (resembles butterfly pull more than ordinary breaststroke pull)
- Press/sweep arms and hands out (point slightly greater than shoulderwidth)
- Palms angled toward pool walls
- Bend elbows to 90°
- Open armpits
- Reach deep into water with fingertips
- Catch water with hands and arms

**3. From catch position:**
- Sweep hands in and down toward feet
- Maintain high-elbow position
- Straight body line

**4. After completing pull:**
- Ride out momentum in head-lead streamlined position

**5. When speed decreases:**
- Sneak arms up and over head (keep close to body)
- Lift heels to begin breaststroke kick

**6. Fire the kick:**
- Hands should be streamlined
- Body approaching surface
- **Resist:** Lifting head up for air (will drop hips, slow momentum)
- **Instead:** Keep head in line with spine, begin breathing with stroke rhythm

#### Adding the Dolphin Kick

**Once comfortable with traditional pull-out:**
- Add dolphin kick to sequence as completing underwater arm pull
- Adds underwater speed while in head-lead position

**Key to success:** Properly timing pull, arm recovery, kicks

**How:** Ride out momentum from each propulsive movement before beginning next → Reduce resistance, maintain better wall speed and momentum.

---

### Learning Curve

**Like flip turn:** Open turns with two-hand touch take time and practice.

**First:** Focus on mechanics  
**Gradually:** Add speed as comfortable

**Common bad habit:** One-hand-touch turns for breaststroke/butterfly during workouts
- Quicker, take less effort
- **BUT:** Not legal in competition → DQ
- **Recommendation:** Complete ALL turns legally in workouts → Every wall is opportunity to fine-tune

---

## INDIVIDUAL MEDLEY TRANSITIONAL TURNS

**Once mastered flip turns and open turns:** Easily progress to IM turns.

**IM order (always):** Butterfly → Backstroke → Breaststroke → Freestyle

**Requires 3 specific transitional turns.**

### Butterfly to Backstroke

**Process:**
1. Swim butterfly to wall
2. Finish with full stroke
3. Two-hand touch
4. Drive one elbow back ("Elbow the robber")
5. Draw knees up
6. Plant feet on wall (facing up)
7. Bring other arm over ("Call the police")
8. Push off on back (tight streamline)
9. Complete normal underwater-backstroke-kick sequence
10. Breakout

### Backstroke to Breaststroke

**Process:**
1. Approach wall swimming backstroke
2. Mark full stroke count from flags to finish
3. Reach wall: Touch with hand
4. Rotate slightly onto side
5. Pull knees up and around
6. Plant feet on wall
7. Move chin from one shoulder to other
8. **Result:** Ready position
9. Push off, streamline
10. Complete breaststroke pullout
11. Breakout stroke

### Breaststroke to Freestyle

**Essentially same as breaststroke-to-breaststroke turn**

**BUT:** Follow push-off and streamline with:
- Underwater kicking sequence for freestyle (not breaststroke pull-out)
- Breakout for freestyle

---

### The IM Challenge

**Most challenging:** Transitioning between stroke rhythms
- Rhythm of stroke approaching wall
- Rhythm of stroke departing from wall

**USMS competitions:**
- 100-, 200-, 400-yard/meter IM events
- Truly tests ability to make stroke transitions
- Minimize wall time (approach, at wall, streamline, breakout)

**Practice:** Mix transitional turns into workouts → Improve transitions, add variety.

---

## FINISHES

### Why Coaches Care

**Even for non-competitors:** Quality finishes benefit every repetition.

**Problems:**
- Decelerate into wall → Cheating self of full distance benefits
- Stop short → Prevent swimmers behind from finishing

### Great Finishes (Workouts & Races)

#### Single-Hand Touches (Freestyle & Backstroke)

✅ **Stretch out approaching wall** (head aligned, body streamlined, minimize resistance)  
✅ **Maintain momentum** by extending forward, keeping head down  
✅ **Don't breathe between flags and finish** (maintain stroke tempo and speed)  
✅ **Hit wall fully extended and rotated onto side** (extended arm tight against ear)  
✅ **Avoid finishing with half stroke**  
✅ **Practice leading with BOTH right and left arms** (either side feels comfortable in race)  
✅ **Backstroke:** Know stroke count from flags to wall  
✅ **Backstroke option:** Half-dolphin kick with final stroke (finish faster and slightly deeper - 8-10 inches [20-25cm] max)  

#### Two-Hand Touches (Breaststroke & Butterfly)

✅ **Prepare well in advance** (set up before reaching flags)  
✅ **Adjust strokes** so can carry momentum of final stroke to wall  
✅ **Finish fully extended**  
✅ **Keep head in line with spine** (maintain forward momentum)  
✅ **Drive fingertips into wall under water line**  

### Electronic Timing

**Competitions with electronic timing:**
- Timing pad on pool wall underwater
- Stops clock when fingertips touch

**Practice mindset:** Imagine stopping clock by shooting fingertips into wall

**After each workout swim:**
1. Look at pace clock after finish
2. Celebrate that fast time!

---

## Walls as Opportunities

**Whether workout or competition:**

**Every approach:**
- Chance to gain speed with tighter streamline or more aggressive push-off
- Refine final strokes to wall
- Perfect amount of strokes needed to carry momentum into turn

**Every flip or spin:**
- Tidy up mechanics
- Reach ready position little more quickly

**Every finish:**
- Nail the wall for victory

**Remember:** Spend 30% of pool time approaching and departing from walls → Gain lot of distance by perfecting each phase of turns.

## Assessment Checklist

**Self-evaluate your starts, turns, finishes:**

**FREESTYLE FLIP TURN**
□ Comfortable exhaling through nose during flip  
□ Can complete somersault consistently  
□ Judging distance well (feet plant at 90°)  
□ Quick touch-and-go at wall  
□ Tight streamline off wall  
□ Smooth rotation to front  
□ Maintaining momentum through breakout  

**BACKSTROKE TURN**
□ Know stroke count from flags  
□ Comfortable crossing over to flip  
□ Feet planting correctly  
□ Streamlined push-off on back  
□ Effective underwater kicking  
□ Head breaks surface before 15m  

**OPEN TURNS (Breast/Fly)**
□ Two-hand touch every time  
□ Quick transition to ready position  
□ Explosive push-off  
□ Tight streamline underwater  
□ Proper stroke-specific breakout  
□ No one-hand touches in practice  

**STARTS**
□ Comfortable with starting protocol  
□ Streamline position perfected  
□ Explosive leg drive  
□ Clean entry (minimal splash)  
□ Maintaining momentum underwater  
□ Smooth transition to swimming  

**FINISHES**
□ Accelerate or maintain speed into wall  
□ No breathing last 4-5 strokes  
□ Fully extended at touch  
□ Hit wall under water line  
□ No half-stroke finishes  

---

*Last updated: 2026-04-06*  
*Page created by: DOC (Swimnerd Wiki Builder)*  
*Source: Mastering Swimming by Jim Montgomery & Mo Chambers, Chapter 8, Human Kinetics, 2009*

# Training Steering with Lactate Testing
**Source:** *The Science of Winning* by Jan Olbrecht

## Core Concept

**Training steering** = continuous loop of planning → executing → measuring → adjusting.

Lactate testing provides **objective feedback** to verify training effects and optimize the program for each swimmer's individual response.

---

## The Steering Cycle

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1. Analyze Current Performance          │
│    (Lactate test + competition results) │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
               ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 2. Determine Training Objectives        │
│    (What needs work? What's priority?)  │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
               ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 3. Design Training Structure             │
│    (5-15 week plan)                      │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
               ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 4. Execute Training                      │
│    (Document everything)                 │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
               ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 5. Evaluate Training Effect              │
│    (Repeat lactate test)                 │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
               ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 6. Adjust Plan for Next Period           │
│    (Adapt based on actual response)      │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
               ↓
         (Return to step 1)
```

**Key Insight:** Identical training plans produce different results in different swimmers. You MUST measure to optimize.

---

## Why Lactate Testing?

### The Problem with Non-Specific Tests

**Holmer 1974 Study:**
- Elite swimmers tested on treadmill AND in pool over 6 years
- Treadmill VO2max: **unchanged**
- Pool VO2max: **clearly improved**

**Conclusion:** Testing swimmers on a bicycle or treadmill is like "taking temperature with a barometer" - both relate to weather but measure different things.

**Rule:** Tests must be swim-specific, stroke-specific, AND distance-specific.

---

## Lactate Test Protocol

### Standard Setup

**Test Structure:**
1. **Submaximal 400m freestyle** (establish aerobic endurance baseline)
2. **Complete rest** (10-20 minutes)
3. **100m or 200m all-out in personal stroke** (assess anaerobic capacity)

**Measurements:**
- **V4-400:** Speed at 4 mmol/L lactate on 400m freestyle (aerobic endurance)
- **Lamax:** Highest lactate concentration after all-out swim (anaerobic performance)

**Consistency:** Same protocol every time for same swimmer = comparable data.

---

## Key Metrics

### 1. Aerobic Endurance (V4-400)
**What it measures:** Speed at 4 mmol/L lactate threshold  
**What it tells you:** Aerobic conditioning base  
**Training response:** Improves slowly (weeks), shows benefit ~1 month AFTER volume increase  

### 2. Anaerobic Capacity (Lamax)  
**What it measures:** Peak lactate after all-out effort  
**What it tells you:** Glycolytic capacity  
**Training response:** Can drop during high aerobic volume phases if not maintained  

---

## Real-World Example: Training Analysis Case Study

### The Setup
Young promising swimmers preparing for national winter championships (October → March).

**Testing Schedule:**
- October 12 (baseline)
- December 2 (after Period I)
- January 13 (after Period II)
- March 23 (after Period III)

### Period I: High Volume Phase

**Training Completed:**
- Duration: 8 weeks
- Volume: 32.5 km/week average
- Intensive work: 10.8% of volume (3.5 km/week)
- Load frequency: 
  - 1.4 blocks/week at 1.5-2.5 mmol/L
  - 0.9 blocks/week at 2.5-5 mmol/L
  - 0.9 blocks/week >5 mmol/L
- Plus: 3x25m sprints 2-3x/week

**Results:**
- V4-400: **+12 seconds** (huge aerobic gain)
- Lamax: **-1.2 mmol/L** (anaerobic capacity decreased!)

**Analysis:**
- ✅ Volume successfully built aerobic base
- ❌ Not enough intensive work to maintain anaerobic capacity
- ❌ 2 intensive blocks/week was insufficient for this group

**Adjustment for Period II:**
- Increase intensive training frequency
- Use fall break to further boost volume
- Add more workouts >1.5 mmol/L lactate

### Period II: Adjusted Plan

**Training Completed:**
- Duration: ~8 weeks
- Volume: 43.6 km/week (+11.1 km/week vs Period I)
- Intensive work: 6.8% of volume (3.0 km/week)
- Load frequency:
  - 1.8 blocks/week at 1.5-2.5 mmol/L
  - 0.9 blocks/week at 2.5-5 mmol/L  
  - 1.4 blocks/week >5 mmol/L
- Total intensive volume: 920m/week (vs 580m/week Period I)

**Results:**
- V4-400: **Maintained** (slightly improved but too soon after volume increase to see full effect)
- Lamax: **Restored** to October baseline

**Analysis:**
- ✅ Aerobic base preserved despite more intensive work
- ✅ Anaerobic capacity recovered with proper dosing
- ✅ Key finding: ~2 intensive blocks/week minimum needed

**Adjustment for Period III:**
- Focus on power and frequency work
- Maintain aerobic base with regeneration-focused training
- Specific competition preparation

### Period III: Competition Phase

**Training Completed:**
- Lower volume, much higher intensity
- Extended peak form period with 1 small high-volume regeneration mesocycle after Flemish Championships

**Results:**
- V4-400: **Well maintained** despite lower volume + competitions
- Lamax: **Slightly increased**

---

## Key Training Insights from Study

### Finding #1: Volume vs. Anaerobic Capacity
**Increasing training volume improves aerobic endurance BUT may depress maximum lactate if not properly compensated.**

**Solution:** Maintain 2+ intensive blocks/week during high-volume phases.

### Finding #2: Intensive Block Frequency
**On average, 2 intensive blocks per week will develop anaerobic conditioning.**

**But:** Dosing is highly individual - requires evaluation for each swimmer.

### Finding #3: Volume Matters More Than Total Intensive Volume
**Improvement of anaerobic conditioning relates more to weekly NUMBER of intensive blocks than total volume of intensive work.**

**Example:** 2 blocks of 100m intensive = better adaptation than 1 block of 200m intensive.

### Finding #4: Aerobic Maintenance  
**Aerobic endurance can be maintained despite intensive training/competitions IF compensated with lots of regeneration training.**

### Finding #5: Short Intensive Bouts in Aerobic Work
**Improvement of aerobic capacity is stimulated by short intensive parts within aerobic training.**

**Practical:** "Spice" long aerobic sets with occasional fast 25s or 50s.

---

## Training Documentation Requirements

To make steering work, you MUST document:

### Daily Training Log
- Volume (total distance)
- Intensity (% in each zone: <1.5, 1.5-2.5, 2.5-5, >5 mmol/L lactate)
- Load frequency (# of intensive blocks/week)
- Load density (how quickly intensive sessions follow each other)
- Attendance (did swimmer complete full workout?)
- Health status (illness, injury, fatigue level)
- External factors (exams, stress, sleep quality)

### Weekly Summary
- Total km
- % distribution by intensity zone
- # intensive blocks by type
- Competitions

### Mesocycle Summary  
- Compare actual vs. planned training
- Competition results  
- Lactate test results
- Subjective athlete feedback

**Without documentation, steering is impossible.**

---

## Adjustments You Can Make

Based on test results, adjust:

1. **Training Intensity** (faster/slower intervals)
2. **Training Volume** (more/less total distance)  
3. **Distribution** (% in each intensity zone)
4. **Composition** (types of exercises)
5. **Frequency** (# workouts/week, # intensive blocks/week)
6. **Density** (spacing between hard sessions)

---

## When Tests Show Unexpected Results

### Scenario: Aerobic improved but swimmer slower in competition
**Possible causes:**
- Insufficient anaerobic capacity
- Technical breakdown under fatigue  
- Insufficient power work
- Mental/tactical issues

**Solution:** Look beyond lactate - analyze technique, stroke rate, race execution.

### Scenario: Lactate suggests improvement but times stagnant
**Possible causes:**
- Test environment different from competition
- Pacing issues
- Starts/turns deteriorated
- Technical regression in main stroke

**Solution:** Add technique assessment, video analysis, race simulation sets.

### Scenario: Both metrics improved but swimmer reports high fatigue
**Possible causes:**  
- Insufficient regeneration
- Accumulated stress (school, life)
- Early overtraining signs
- Illness developing

**Solution:** Insert recovery microcycle, reduce load density, check non-training stressors.

---

## Practical Guidelines

### Testing Frequency
- **Base period:** Every 4-6 weeks
- **Competition period:** Every 2-3 weeks or after major adaptation stimulus
- **Transition period:** Not needed (recovery focus)

### Interpreting Changes

**Aerobic Endurance (V4-400):**
- +5-10 sec improvement = good response
- +10-15 sec = excellent response  
- No change after 6+ weeks = insufficient stimulus or poor recovery
- Decrease = overtraining or illness

**Anaerobic Capacity (Lamax):**
- 10-14 mmol/L = well-trained
- <10 mmol/L = needs anaerobic work or recovery
- >14 mmol/L = strong glycolytic capacity
- Drop >1 mmol/L during volume phase = add intensive work

### Age Group Swimmers
- Expect slower adaptations (developing systems)
- Focus more on V4-400 than Lamax
- Don't chase high lactate numbers - chase speed at low lactate

### Elite Swimmers  
- Smaller margins of improvement
- More sensitive to small training changes
- Need more precise steering
- Mental/tactical factors matter more

---

## Common Steering Mistakes

❌ **Testing but not adjusting** - Data without action is wasted  
❌ **Changing too much at once** - Can't identify what worked  
❌ **Ignoring individual response** - Group plans need individual tweaks  
❌ **Not documenting training** - Can't link results to stimulus  
❌ **Testing in wrong stroke/distance** - Not specific to competition goal  

---

## Cross-References

Related wiki pages:
- [[Periodization Macrocycle]] - When to test within training cycle
- [[Training Intensity Zones]] - How to structure workouts based on lactate
- [[Supercompensation]] - Timing tests after adaptation windows

---

**Bottom Line:** Training steering transforms coaching from guesswork to science. Test → measure → adjust → repeat. The swimmers who improve fastest aren't necessarily the hardest workers - they're the ones whose coaches adjust smartly based on data.

# Active Rest Training: Increasing Anaerobic Threshold

**Source:** *Janet Evans' Total Swimming* (2007), Chapter 7  
**Context:** Evans' favorite training method for building "easy speed"  
**Audience:** Coaches & competitive swimmers wanting to swim faster for longer

---

## What is Active Rest?

> "Allowing the heart rate to drop (the rest part) while you're swimming (the active part), instead of while you're at the wall."

**The concept:**
- **Traditional rest:** Stop at wall → heart rate drops → push off
- **Active rest:** Keep swimming → vary effort → heart rate fluctuates → no stopping

**Result:** More time spent training at race-relevant intensities without full recovery.

---

## The Physiological Target: Anaerobic Threshold

### Definition
**Anaerobic threshold (AT):** The absolute maximum level of effort at which the body can continue to transport enough oxygen TO muscles and lactic acid FROM muscles.

**The AT Window:** Effort between aerobic (sustainable forever) and anaerobic (sustainable ~60 seconds).

| **Workout Type** | **Effort Level** | **What it trains** | **Sustainable duration** |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Aerobic (Base) | ES 4-6, 70-85% MHR | Cardiovascular endurance | Hours |
| **AT (Active Rest)** | **ES 4-8, 70-90% MHR** | **Anaerobic threshold + "easy speed"** | **Minutes to 30+ min** |
| Anaerobic (Speed) | ES 9-10, 95-100% MHR | Raw speed/power | ~60 seconds |

**Why AT training matters:**
> "It's one thing to be able to swim at ES 2 indefinitely. It's quite another to be able to swim at ES 7 indefinitely. The higher your anaerobic threshold, the less difficult it will feel to go faster."

---

## "Easy Speed" — The Holy Grail

**What is easy speed?**
- Ability to hold fast paces without feeling like you're sprinting
- Swimming at ES 7 feels like ES 5
- Race pace feels sustainable, not desperate

**How AT training builds it:**
1. Train body to clear lactate faster
2. Improve oxygen delivery efficiency
3. Raise the ceiling on "comfortable fast"
4. Make speed feel easier over time

**Evans' perspective:**
> "I consider anaerobic threshold workouts, also called 'active rest,' to be the most enjoyable."

**Why she loved them:**
- Effort levels change often → workouts never stale
- Fast swimming → heart rate up to ~90% MHR
- Slower swimming → chance to recover before next fast swim
- Develops ability to distinguish between varying effort levels

---

## Active Rest Set Structures

### Classic Active Rest Pattern

**Format:** Fast swim → easy swim → repeat

**Example 1:** Workout #1 (1,200 yards)
```
1 × 100 free on 2:20 (ES 4) ← EASY
4 × 75 free on 1:20 (ES 8)  ← FAST
Repeat 3 times
```

**How it works:**
- ES 8 on 75s → heart rate climbs to ~90% MHR
- ES 4 on 100 → heart rate drops to ~70% MHR while still swimming
- Short rest between reps, but enough to sustain effort through 3 rounds

**Example 2:** Workout #2 (1,200 yards)
```
1 × 50 free on 1:05 (ES 4)  ← EASY
1 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8) ← FAST
Repeat 8 times
```

**Key principle:** 50% or more of distance is ES 4 rest swimming, allowing you to maintain pace on the ES 8 swims.

---

### Descending Interval Active Rest

**Format:** Effort stays high (ES 6-8), but interval gets faster as set progresses.

**Example:** Workout #3 (1,500 yards)
```
15 × 100 free
  #s 1-5 on 1:55 (ES 6)
  #s 6-10 on 1:50 (ES 7)
  #s 11-15 on 1:45 (ES 8)
Repeat 5 times
```

**What makes this AT training (not pure speed):**
- No easy swimming built in
- But intervals allow 7-10 seconds rest → heart rate stays 80-90% MHR
- Teaches pacing + effort recognition
- Develops ability to hold pace with decreasing rest

**Evans:**
> "This is the kind of set that develops easy speed."

---

### Pyramid / Ladder Active Rest

**Format:** Alternate fast swims with easy recovery swims, varying distances.

**Example:** Workout #5 (1,800 yards)
```
1 × 300 free on 5:45 (ES 6) ← EASY
1 × 200 free on 3:40 (ES 7) ← FASTER
1 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8) ← FASTEST
Repeat 3 times
```

**Descending interval twist:** Base per 100 gets 5 seconds faster each repeat.

**Challenge:** 300s are "easy" but still work → you're tired by the time you hit 100s → must demonstrate endurance to repeat miniset 3 times.

---

### Build-Up Active Rest

**Format:** Gradually increase # of fast swims, with easy swims between.

**Example:** Workout #8 (2,000 yards)
```
1 × 100 free on 2:10 (ES 4)
1 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8)

1 × 100 free on 2:10 (ES 4)
2 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8)

1 × 100 free on 2:10 (ES 4)
3 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8)

1 × 100 free on 2:10 (ES 4)
4 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8)

1 × 100 free on 2:10 (ES 4)
5 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8)
```

**Strategy:**
- Each group of ES 8 100s = its own miniset
- Easy 100s = recover + prep for next group
- Start at strong (not sprint) pace that's sustainable
- Maintain pace throughout → ES 8 intervals don't get faster

**Evans' coaching:**
> "Just make sure to use the easy 100s to rest."

---

## Programming Active Rest Workouts

### Structure Components

**Every AT workout includes:**

1. **Warm-up** (400-600 yards, ES 1-2)
   - Easy swimming
   - Mix in drills/strokes
   - Get heart rate up gradually

2. **Drill set** (300-600 yards, ES 3-4)
   - Freestyle drills (One-Arm, 3-3-3, Hip-Shoulder-Forehead, Fist)
   - Focus on technique refinement
   - Prepare body for main set

3. **Pull set** (400-800 yards, ES 5-6)
   - Pull buoy + paddles
   - Long, stretched-out swimming
   - Shoulder warm-up before intensity

4. **Main set** (1,200-2,500 yards, ES 4-8)
   - Active rest structure
   - Mix of easy and hard efforts
   - Heart rate stays 70-90% MHR

5. **Cool-down** (150-300 yards, ES 1)
   - Easy swimming
   - Mix strokes/drills
   - Heart rate returns to baseline

### Distance Progression

**Evans' workout series:**
- Workouts 1-2: 2,500 yards (~50 min)
- Workouts 3-4: 3,000 yards (~60 min)
- Workouts 5-6: 3,500 yards (~70 min)
- Workouts 7-8: 4,000 yards (~80 min)
- Workouts 9-12: 4,500-5,000 yards (~90-100 min)

**Principle:** Increase by 500 yards every 2 workouts = gradual progression.

---

## Interval Guidelines for Active Rest

### How Much Rest to Build In

**Target:** Heart rate should NOT return to normal between reps.

> "The point of the rest between repeats isn't to allow your heart rate to return to normal. In fact, the converse is true. Rest should be kept to a minimum during most sets so that the heart rate stays somewhat consistent."

**Recommended rest by distance:**
- **50 yards:** 5 seconds minimum
- **100 yards:** 7-8 seconds
- **150 yards:** 10 seconds
- **200 yards:** 15 seconds
- **250 yards:** 17-18 seconds
- **300 yards:** 20 seconds

**For easy (ES 4) swims in active rest:** Can add 5-10 extra seconds to allow more recovery.

**Too much rest = defeated purpose:** If heart rate drops below 70% MHR between reps, you're no longer training AT.

---

## Effort Scale Recognition

**Active rest training is THE best way to learn effort recognition.**

**Why:** You're constantly shifting between ES 4 (moderate) and ES 8 (hard) within the same set.

**What swimmers learn:**
- **ES 4 feel:** Moderate effort, can hold conversation in short bursts, 70% MHR
- **ES 6 feel:** Definite effort, breathing harder, 80% MHR
- **ES 7 feel:** Serious effort, focused breathing, 85% MHR
- **ES 8 feel:** Very serious effort, controlled hard swimming, 90% MHR

**Application:** After mastering AT workouts, swimmers can self-regulate effort in any set.

---

## Common Mistakes & Fixes

### Mistake 1: Swimming Fast Swims Too Easy

**Problem:** Swimmer treats ES 8 swims like ES 6 → no AT training happening.

**Fix:**
- Check heart rate immediately after fast swim → should be ~90% MHR
- If it's only 80%, swimmer needs to go harder
- Coach: Give specific time standards or have them descend 3-5 seconds from warm-up pace

### Mistake 2: Not Using Easy Swims to Recover

**Problem:** Swimmer treats ES 4 swims like ES 7 → heart rate stays at 90% → can't sustain set.

**Fix:**
- Emphasize "long and stretched out" on easy swims
- Give SLOWER interval for easy swims (e.g., 100 on 2:20 vs. typical 2:00)
- Teach swimmer to feel heart rate dropping during easy swim

### Mistake 3: Taking Extra Rest at Wall

**Problem:** Swimmer takes 5-10 extra seconds beyond interval → heart rate drops below 70%.

**Fix:**
- Use pace clock discipline
- Explain that extra rest defeats AT purpose
- If swimmer NEEDS more rest, lower the base for easy swims

### Mistake 4: Starting Fast Swims Too Hard

**Problem:** Swimmer sprints first few reps → can't maintain pace through set.

**Fix:**
> "The key is to go out at a moderate pace on the [early reps] so you have something left for the [later reps]."
- Teach negative splitting within set
- Give specific time ranges (e.g., "hold 1:35-1:38 on all ES 8 100s")
- Reward consistent pacing, not fast early times

---

## Sample Active Rest Workouts (Evans-Style)

### Beginner AT Workout (2,500 yards)

```
Warm-up: 400 free easy
Drill: 6 × 50 on 1:15 (25 drill, 25 swim)
Pull: 400 free with buoy/paddles

MAIN SET (1,200 yards):
1 × 100 free on 2:20 (ES 4)
4 × 75 free on 1:20 (ES 8)
Repeat 3 times

Cool-down: 200 free easy
```

**Total: 2,500 yards, ~50 minutes**

---

### Intermediate AT Workout (3,500 yards)

```
Warm-up: 600 free easy with backstroke/breast mixed in
Pull: 4 × 200 free on 4:20 with buoy/paddles

MAIN SET (1,800 yards):
1 × 300 free on 5:45 (ES 6)
1 × 200 free on 3:40 (ES 7)
1 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8)
Repeat 3 times

Cool-down: 3 × 100 free easy with 10 sec rest
```

**Total: 3,500 yards, ~70 minutes**

---

### Advanced AT Workout (4,500 yards)

```
Warm-up: 600 free (mix drills every 4th 50)
Kick: 12 × 50 on 1:45 (odd = free, even = fly/breast) with board
200 free easy to loosen arms

MAIN SET (1,950 yards):
1 × 50 free on 1:05 (ES 4)
2 × 100 free on 1:50 (ES 8)
1 × 50 free on 1:05 (ES 4)
4 × 100 free on 1:50 (ES 8)
1 × 50 free on 1:05 (ES 4)
6 × 100 free on 1:50 (ES 8)

Pull: 6 × 100 free on 2:10 with buoy/paddles
Cool-down: 300 free easy
```

**Total: 4,500 yards, ~90 minutes**

---

## When to Use Active Rest Training

### Training Cycle Placement

**Best used:**
- **Mid-season** (after base phase, before taper)
- **2-4 weeks before target race** (builds race-specific fitness)
- **Year-round maintenance** (1-2x/week alongside base + speed work)

**Not ideal:**
- **Early season** (need base endurance first)
- **Taper week** (too fatiguing)
- **Recovery weeks** (defeats recovery purpose)

### Frequency

**Evans' recommendation:** 2-3 AT workouts per week during build phase.

**Sample week:**
- Monday: Active rest workout
- Tuesday: Base/endurance workout
- Wednesday: Drills + easy swim
- Thursday: Active rest workout
- Friday: Speed/sprint workout
- Saturday: Long base workout
- Sunday: Rest or easy swim

---

## Measuring Progress

### Performance Markers

**You know AT training is working when:**
1. Times on fast swims stay consistent through entire set (no drop-off)
2. Heart rate recovers faster during easy swims
3. ES 8 pace starts to feel like old ES 7 pace
4. You can hold race pace for longer distances in practice
5. Racing feels easier at same speed

**Evans' test:**
- **Week 1:** 10 × 100 free on 1:45 (ES 8) → hold 1:35-1:38
- **Week 6:** Same set → hold 1:32-1:35 at same ES 8 feel

**Result:** "Easy speed" — you're swimming 3-6 seconds faster per 100 at the same perceived effort.

---

## Key Takeaway

**Active rest training = the bridge between endurance and speed.**

**What it is:** Varying effort levels (ES 4-8) within a single set to keep heart rate in the 70-90% MHR "sweet spot" — the anaerobic threshold zone.

**Why it works:** Trains the body to clear lactate, deliver oxygen efficiently, and sustain fast paces longer → "easy speed."

**How to structure it:** Fast swim (ES 7-8) → easy swim (ES 4) → repeat, OR descending intervals at ES 6-8.

**Evans' verdict:** "The most enjoyable workouts" — varied enough to stay interesting, effective enough to produce results.

**Proof:** Evans used AT training throughout her 10+ year Olympic career, winning 4 golds and setting multiple world records in distance events (400/800/1500 free).

# Drill Fundamentals: The Art & Science of Practice

**Source:** *The 100 Best Swimming Drills* by Blythe Lucero  
**Context:** Core philosophy for using drills to develop efficient, economic swimming  
**Audience:** Coaches & swimmers at all levels

---

## Core Philosophy: Efficient Swimming = Art + Science

### The Art of Swimming
**"Feeling" effective movement through the water.**

- **Fishlike** — Fluid, graceful movement
- **Balance, rhythm, posture** — Like dance
- **Harmony** — Combined actions producing greater results than parts alone
- **"At one with the water"** — Not fighting it

### The Science of Swimming
**"Understanding" movement through the water.**

> "Water, brains, and guts." — Doc Counsilman, "Father of Modern Swimming"

- **Physics at work** — Study movement as an equation
- **Cause & effect** — Understand what produces propulsion with least effort
- **Applying principles** — Learn to use the science in the water

**Best practice engages both body AND brain.**

---

## Economic Swimming vs. Efficient Swimming

### Economic Swimming (Step 1)
**Managing energy resources.**

Like gas in a tank:
- Swimming requires effort
- Effort uses energy
- Energy is not unlimited
- **Less effort = more distance/speed from same tank**

**Foundation of economic swimming:**
1. **Minimize drag** — Save energy
2. **Develop "feel" for the water** — Avoid wasted effort/extra strokes
3. **Access potential power** — Engage muscle groups best suited to move forward with least effort

### Efficient Swimming (Step 2)
**Applying conserved energy to sustaining speed over time.**

**Bridge from economic → efficient:**
1. Ongoing practice of economic swimming through good technique
2. Develop conditioning & strength to apply solid technique stroke after stroke

---

## What Is a Drill?

**Definition:** A structured method of practice used to acquire a skill, procedure, or sequence.

### Swimming Drill Specifics
- Activity designed to develop and practice good stroke habits
- Repetitive exercise for improving and perfecting technique
- Way of isolating part of a complex stroke sequence to learn and perform it correctly

### Types of Drills

**1. Progression-Based Drills**
- Build stroke one piece at a time
- Start with basic skill
- Add parts until all connected
- Feel and understand in context of coordinated stroke

**2. Part Practice**
- Isolate particular action when stroke flaw identified
- Repetitive practice of just that part
- Relearn weak area
- Implement new correct technique in full stroke

**3. Contrast Drills**
- Perform skill incorrectly first
- Immediately repeat correctly
- Notice better results from correct technique
- **Controversial but effective when followed by correct practice**

**4. X Drills (eXaggeration)**
- Use eXtreme to demonstrate effect of technique
- Take skill to its full range
- Feel desired technique more clearly
- Discover how to balance various stroke actions

---

## Seven Aspects Drills Address

### 1. Body Position
**Goal:** Master horizontal balance & core stability.

- Reduce drag
- Improve feel for water
- Increase access to power
- Achieve "swimming downhill" feeling

**Key:** Spine and core strength are crucial (like standing erect on land).

### 2. Kick
**Goal:** Provide forward momentum, rhythm, counterbalance.

**Challenge:** Large leg muscles demand great oxygen.

**Develop:**
- **Productive kick** — Relieves upper body workload
- **Sustainable kick** — Economical energy expenditure

**Requirements:**
- Relaxed feet & ankles
- Fluid motion
- Feet connected to water

**Important:** Kick for forward motion, NOT to correct floating problems.

### 3. Arm Stroke (Power Phase)
**Three dimensions:** Length, width, depth

**Three basic functions:**
1. **Maximize stroke length** — Longer strokes = fewer strokes = less energy
2. **Move body forward in straightest path** — Sculling/sweeping motions that accelerate
3. **Position hands to feel the water** — Use water like a handle; anchor and advance past it

### 4. Recovery
**Goal:** Return arm to starting point; rest momentarily (except breaststroke).

**Three keys:**
- **Relax** — Important for economic swimming
- **Align** — Misaligned recovery → misaligned stroke → constant correcting
- **Balance** — Connected to other stroke actions; must complement forward motion

**Bonus:** Aligned recovery prevents shoulder pain/injury.

### 5. Breathing
**Goal:** Replenish oxygen within the line of the stroke.

**Two-part timing:**
- **Inhale** — During recovery
- **Exhale** — During power phase (summons more power, like other sports)

**Rhythm theory:** Some believe breathing rhythm should determine stroke rate, pace, and strategy.

**Applies to all distances:** 50 sprint to mile.

### 6. Leverage
**Core-centered power.**

> "Like a ceiling fan — the motor in the middle propels the blades."

**Examples in other sports:**
- Baseball pitcher — hips & shoulders snap forward
- Batter, golfer, boxer, ice skater — all use core

**In swimming:**
- Stabilize joints (elbows, knees)
- Fish don't have elbows/knees — neither should we
- Don't let joints collapse and weaken levers

### 7. Coordination
**Goal:** Fit all pieces together for fluid, graceful, powerful forward motion.

**Key principles:**
- Use kick WITH arms, not just in addition
- Breathe within line of stroke (don't interrupt forward motion)
- Use body position to access leverage
- Time each action to complement others
- Develop weakest actions
- Make most of strongest actions

**Single goal:** Move through water with less effort → faster & longer.

---

## When to Use Drills

### Every Swim Session
- **Warm-up** — Establish foundation
- **Within sets** — Remind of correct technique when working hard
- **Between sets** — Refocus on swimming efficiently

### Competition Preparation
- **Taper** — Quality swimming, less intensive, maintain feel while recovering
- **Race day warm-up** — Practice ideal stroke actions before racing

### Off-Season
- Use drills almost exclusively
- Maintain conditioning
- Practice stroke economy
- Improve efficiency for next season

---

## How to Use Drills: The Rules

### Rule 1: Know the Purpose
**Just performing a drill is not enough.**

- Doing drill without understanding = empty swimming
- Must think about AND feel what is supposed to happen
- Must notice what IS happening

**Be fully engaged.**

### Rule 2: Focus on the Point
**Certain drills target very specific parts.**

Example: Kicking drill with arms at sides
- Breathing will be difficult
- Breathing technique won't match full stroke
- **BUT breathing is not the point — ignore it**
- Focus on kicking or you defeat the purpose

### Rule 3: Follow the Correct Sequence
**Perform each step correctly to feel full effect.**

- Skipping steps reinforces incorrect technique
- Can interfere with desired outcome
- Skipped step might be the very problem area

### Rule 4: Start Slowly
**Many drills seem awkward at first.**

- Like any new thing, gets better with practice
- Spend time on each drill
- Identify problems
- Make needed modifications
- Practice until you do it right

### Rule 5: Know the Speed
**Some drills = swim speed. Some drills = not.**

- Some are done stationary
- **Concern = quality, NOT yardage**
- Once you can do drill correctly, alternate laps of drill & swim
- New technique works in context of full stroke

### Rule 6: No Rush
**The process is equally rewarding as the goal.**

- Each positive change = revelation
- Each new skill = puzzle piece discovered after long search
- Every improvement empowers you to pursue another

---

## Turning Workout into Practice

### The Problem: One-Sided Approach
**Common mistake:**
1. Learn to produce forward motion
2. Leave stroke technique behind
3. Focus on conditioning only
4. Get rapid improvement (for a while)
5. Hit wall when conditioning alone stops working

**Missing:** Workout is only HALF the formula.

### The Solution: Both Sides Simultaneously
**Regular attention to technique:**
- Develops swimming that requires less effort
- Energy saved → applied to building better swimming through efficient strokes
- Long-term improvement from technique side AND conditioning side

**Transform workout → practice:**
- Not just opportunity to swim more
- Opportunity to practice efficient swimming

### How Drills Transform Practice
**Strategic incorporation into any swim session:**
- Specific practice (drills) rather than just workout
- Build efficient swimming from both sides
- Experience long-term improvement

---

## Practice Doesn't Make Perfect

> "Practice doesn't make perfect; **perfect practice** makes perfect. Practicing the wrong action over and over doesn't improve your stroke or speed. Practicing the right technique will."

### The Fatigue Problem
**When tired, we revert to old habits.**
- Lose stroke, form, technique
- Practicing flawed technique = perfecting flawed technique

### Start Short, Add Distance Gradually
**Best way to learn:**
1. Practice new technique for SHORT distances first
2. Even experienced swimmers start short when learning new technique
3. Gradually add distance once technique is solid
4. Ingrain new technique before adding fatigue

**Goal:** Learn to hold your stroke for whatever distance you're swimming.
- Sprinter: hold for 50-100
- Distance: hold for 1500+
- Triathlete: hold for up to 2 miles

---

## History of Swimming Drills (Highlights)

### Ancient Times
- Swimming depicted in Egyptian/Assyrian art (2000 B.C.)
- Used for survival, military, leisure
- Bible mentions it
- Romans: important part of education
- Prophet Mohammed: "the highest sport"
- Medieval knights: one of the original "agilities"

### 1500s-1700s
- First books on swimming technique (European breaststroke)
- **First drills:** Diagrams for study and emulation
- **Land practice:** Moving arms/legs through air (failed — no floatation experience!)
- Explorers see alternating over-arm stroke in New World — superior in speed
- Europeans don't adopt it (grace > speed in their view)

### 1800s-1900s
- Swimming becomes official sport — speed becomes goal
- Trial & error → new strokes (sidestroke, trudgen, crawl)
- Early coaches = "professors of swimming" with secret techniques
- 1920s: Filming best swimmers as training tools
- 1950s: Four distinct competitive strokes established with rules

### 1960s: Doc Counsilman Revolution
**Changed focus from speed → efficiency.**

- Used science to explain swimming propulsion
- Focused on technique of efficient swimming
- Contributed concepts still used today:
  - Sculling
  - Leverage

**Last 40 years:**
- Training methods develop rapidly
- **Drills remain part of training at all levels**

### Modern Examples
- **Alexander Popov:** Freestyle with dolphin kick
- **Inge de Bruijn:** Hours of single-leg kicking
- **Natalie Coughlin:** Rose to world-class with no arm work training
- **USA Swimming:** "A skill done 99% correctly is 100% wrong"

---

## Actionable Takeaways for Coaches

✅ **Drills = thinking AND feeling approach** — Engage body & brain  
✅ **Economic first, efficient second** — Minimize drag/waste → apply saved energy to speed  
✅ **Know the purpose** — Empty practice = no improvement  
✅ **Focus on the point** — Ignore aspects not being targeted  
✅ **Start short** — When tired, we revert to old habits  
✅ **Perfect practice makes perfect** — Practicing wrong = perfecting wrong  
✅ **Every session should include drills** — Warm-up, sets, between sets, taper, race day  
✅ **Seven aspects to address:** Body position, kick, arm stroke, recovery, breathing, leverage, coordination

# Reaction Drills for Sprinters

**Source:** *Sprinting: A Coach's Challenge* by Samuel James Freas  
**Key Insight:** "Reaction drills are the secret weapon that gave LSU sprinters 0.1-0.3 second advantage off the blocks"  
**Application:** Daily neuromuscular training to improve fast-twitch fiber recruitment and reduce reaction time

---

## Why Reaction Drills Matter

**The Arkansas/LSU Discovery:**

When Freas took over at Arkansas with limited pool time (90 min/day), he HAD to find ways to improve sprinters on land. He knew from coaching track that **reaction time is trainable**.

**Results:**
- Arkansas/LSU sprinters consistently had **THE BEST reaction times** in the NCAA
- They were "beating everyone off the blocks" by age 2 of the program
- Relay exchanges were faster and cleaner than anyone else

**The secret:** **Daily reaction drills** targeting:
1. Visual reaction (eyes → brain → body)
2. Auditory reaction (ears → brain → body)
3. Proprioceptive reaction (feel → brain → body)

---

## The Science (Simplified)

**Reaction time = Stimulus → Brain → Muscle activation**

**Three components:**
1. **Sensory detection** (see/hear the stimulus)
2. **Central processing** (brain decides what to do)
3. **Motor response** (muscles fire)

**Can you train it?** YES.

**How:**
- Repetition (neural pathways strengthen with practice)
- Variability (train multiple stimulus types)
- Specificity (practice the EXACT movement you'll race)

**Freas philosophy:**
> "The feet cannot start moving soon enough off the wall or blocks. Practice REACTING, not thinking."

---

## Types of Reaction Drills

### 1. **Hand Reaction Drills** (Visual Stimulus)

**Purpose:** Train visual processing + hand/arm speed

**Basic drill:**
- Coach holds hand up
- Athlete in ready position (track start or grab start)
- Coach drops hand → Athlete GOES
- Focus: Minimize time between seeing hand drop and body movement

**Progressions:**
1. **Stationary reaction** (just upper body)
2. **Start position reaction** (full start position, GO on hand drop)
3. **Variable stimulus** (hand drop at random times—no predictability)
4. **Multi-stimulus** (coach drops hand OR yells "go" → athlete reacts to BOTH)

**Daily volume:** 10-15 reps

---

### 2. **Foot Reaction Drills** (Auditory Stimulus)

**Purpose:** Train auditory processing + leg/foot speed

**Basic drill:**
- Athlete in start position (on blocks or push-off position)
- Coach gives auditory signal: whistle, clap, beep, "GO"
- Athlete REACTS (drives forward, pushes off wall, etc.)

**Progressions:**
1. **Simple reaction** (single stimulus → GO)
2. **Choice reaction** (two different sounds → different movements)
3. **Delayed reaction** (stimulus → pause → GO on second stimulus)

**Key:** Make stimulus UNPREDICTABLE. If athlete anticipates, they're not reacting.

**Daily volume:** 10-15 reps

---

### 3. **Quick Feet Drills** (Ground-Based)

**Purpose:** Train leg speed, agility, fast-twitch recruitment

**Drills:**

**A. Ladder drills**
- Quick feet through agility ladder
- Patterns: one foot in each square, two feet in each square, lateral shuffle, etc.
- Goal: Speed + precision

**B. Line jumps**
- Jump side-to-side over a line
- 20-30 seconds max effort
- Count reps (track improvement)

**C. Box taps**
- Rapid foot taps on a low box (6-12" height)
- Alternate feet as fast as possible
- 20-30 seconds

**Daily volume:** 3-5 minutes total

---

### 4. **Quick Hands Drills** (Upper Body)

**Purpose:** Train arm speed, hand entry speed, stroke turnover

**Drills:**

**A. Shadowboxing**
- Stand in front of mirror
- Rapid punches (simulates freestyle arm turnover)
- Focus: Speed, not power
- 30-60 seconds

**B. Partner hand-slap drill**
- Partners face each other, hands up
- One tries to slap partner's hands
- Partner reacts and pulls hands away
- Switch roles
- 30 seconds each

**C. Wall push reactions**
- Hands on wall in push-up position
- Partner taps shoulder → Athlete pushes explosively away from wall
- Variation: Partner taps LEFT shoulder → push and rotate left, etc.

**Daily volume:** 3-5 minutes total

---

### 5. **Underwater Reaction Drills** (Pool-Specific)

**Purpose:** Train reaction in the actual environment (water)

**Drills:**

**A. Push-off reactions**
- Athlete at wall in push-off position
- Coach stands at side of pool with visual or auditory cue
- Cue → Athlete EXPLODES off wall
- Measure distance traveled (competition with teammates)

**B. Dive reactions**
- Athlete on blocks
- Random start signals (not just "take your mark... GO")
- Variation: Long hold, short hold, fake start (stay frozen)
- Builds mental discipline + reaction speed

**C. Relay exchange reactions**
- Incoming swimmer approaching wall
- Outgoing swimmer on blocks
- Practice "seeing" the touch point and timing the exchange
- Goal: Minimize exchange time (measure with stopwatch)

**Daily volume:** 5-10 reps (don't overdo—quality > quantity)

---

## Sample Daily Reaction Routine (15 minutes)

**Pre-workout (5 min):**
```
- 10 hand reaction drills (visual stimulus)
- 10 foot reaction drills (auditory stimulus)
- 2 min quick feet (ladder or line jumps)
```

**Mid-workout (5 min):**
```
- 5 push-off reactions from wall
- 5 relay exchange practices
```

**Post-workout (5 min):**
```
- 2 min quick hands (shadowboxing or partner drill)
- 10 dive reactions (on blocks)
- Flexibility (transition to cool-down)
```

**Total time:** 15 min  
**Total reps:** 40-50 reactions  
**Frequency:** EVERY DAY (including taper)

---

## Reaction Drill Equipment

**Minimal setup:**
- Whistle or clap (auditory stimulus)
- Your hand (visual stimulus)
- A line on the deck (for jumps)

**Enhanced setup:**
- **Starting device** (electronic beep, like a real start)
- **Agility ladder** (for quick feet patterns)
- **Low box** (6-12" for taps and jumps)
- **Medicine ball** (for reactive throws/catches)
- **Reaction ball** (irregular bounce for hand reactions)

**Freas philosophy:** Don't overthink it. Simple drills done DAILY beat complex drills done occasionally.

---

## Integration with Strength Training

**Freas approach:** Combine reaction drills with strength/power work

**Example:**

**Strength day (post-weights):**
```
After power cleans or box jumps:
- 10 explosive push-off reactions from wall
- 2 min quick feet
- 10 hand reactions

Why? Nervous system is already "fired up" from lifting.
Perfect time to reinforce fast-twitch recruitment.
```

**Circuit training day:**
```
Station 1: Box jumps × 10
Station 2: Quick feet ladder × 30 sec
Station 3: Hand reaction drill × 10
Station 4: Medicine ball slams × 10

Rotate through 3-4 rounds.
```

---

## Taper: Keep Reaction Drills Going

**Critical insight from Freas:**

Most coaches DROP dryland during taper.  
**BIG MISTAKE for sprinters.**

**Why:**
- Reaction speed is neuromuscular (not cardiovascular)
- It doesn't create fatigue the way weights or high yardage does
- Stopping reaction drills = losing reaction sharpness

**Freas taper protocol:**
- **Drop weights 10 days out**
- **Reduce pool volume**
- **KEEP reaction drills + flexibility**

**Daily taper routine:**
```
AM:
- 15 min flexibility
- 10 min reaction drills (hands, feet, quick movements)

PM:
- Pool workout (reduced volume)
- 5 min reaction drills (push-offs, dives)
- 10 min flexibility
```

**Result:** Sprinters arrive at championship meet with SHARP reactions and rested bodies.

---

## Measuring Progress

**Track these metrics:**

1. **Reaction time off blocks** (electronic timing if available)
   - Goal: <0.65 seconds (elite level)
   - Improvement: 0.05-0.10 sec over a season

2. **Quick feet drill counts** (how many line jumps in 30 sec)
   - Goal: 60+ (elite level)
   - Track weekly, compete with teammates

3. **Relay exchange time** (watch time vs. flat start time)
   - Goal: Outgoing swimmer is 0.2-0.4 sec FASTER than flat start time
   - Measures quality of exchange reaction

4. **Subjective feel** ("Do I feel faster off the wall?")
   - Athletes will KNOW when they're reacting faster
   - Trust the feel

---

## Age Group Application

**For 8-and-unders:**
- Make it a GAME
- "Red light, green light" (reaction to visual cue)
- "Freeze dance" (reaction to auditory cue)
- No pressure, just fun

**For 9-12:**
- Introduce basic reaction drills (5 min/practice)
- Quick feet games (ladder drills, line jumps)
- Celebrate fast reactions (positive reinforcement)

**For 13+:**
- Full Freas reaction protocol (15 min/day)
- Measure and track progress
- Competitive drills (teammate vs. teammate)

---

## Common Mistakes & Fixes

### ❌ Mistake: Anticipating Instead of Reacting

**Problem:** Athlete "guesses" when the signal will come and moves early

**Fix:**
- Randomize timing (long holds, short holds)
- Penalize false starts (do it again correctly)
- Use UNPREDICTABLE stimuli

### ❌ Mistake: Doing Reaction Drills Once a Week

**Problem:** Not enough frequency to build neural adaptation

**Fix:**
- DAILY reaction drills (even if just 5 minutes)
- Consistency > intensity

### ❌ Mistake: Only Training One Type of Reaction

**Problem:** Only practicing auditory (whistle) reactions, ignoring visual and proprioceptive

**Fix:**
- Train ALL THREE: visual (hand drop), auditory (whistle), proprioceptive (feel the movement)
- Vary stimuli daily

### ❌ Mistake: Skipping Reaction Drills During Taper

**Problem:** Reaction sharpness fades right before championship

**Fix:**
- KEEP reaction drills through taper
- They don't create fatigue
- They MAINTAIN neuromuscular sharpness

---

## The "Hammer Down" Connection

**Reaction drills feed into race mentality:**

When Freas says **"Hammer Down"** (full effort, race mode), sprinters who have trained reactions DAILY are able to:
- Explode off the blocks faster
- React to the turn faster
- Execute relay exchanges cleaner
- Finish with better timing

**It all connects:** Reaction → Power → Speed → Victory

---

## Sample Weekly Plan (In-Season)

| Day | Reaction Drill Focus | Time | Integration |
|-----|---------------------|------|-------------|
| **Mon** | Hand reactions + Quick feet | 10 min | Pre-workout (deck) |
| **Tue** | Foot reactions + Dive practice | 10 min | Mid-workout (pool) |
| **Wed** | Quick hands + Push-off reactions | 10 min | Post-weights |
| **Thu** | Hand + Foot reactions | 10 min | Pre-workout (deck) |
| **Fri** | Dive reactions (timed) | 10 min | Pre-"Hammer Down" set |
| **Sat** | Relay exchange reactions | 5 min | Meet warm-up |
| **Sun** | Light quick feet (optional) | 5 min | Recovery day |

**Total weekly reaction work:** 60-70 minutes  
**Daily commitment:** 10 minutes  
**Impact:** 0.1-0.3 sec faster reaction time = body length advantage off start

---

## Key Takeaways

✅ **Reaction time IS trainable** (neuromuscular adaptation)  
✅ **Train DAILY** (5-15 min minimum)  
✅ **Train all three types:** visual, auditory, proprioceptive  
✅ **Integrate with strength work** (nervous system already activated)  
✅ **KEEP going through taper** (don't lose sharpness)  
✅ **Measure progress** (reaction time, quick feet counts, relay exchanges)  
✅ **Make it competitive** (teammates race each other in drills)  
✅ **Start young** (age-groupers can build lifelong habits)

> "I knew as early as my coaching tenure at Arkansas that reaction drills were paying off because we were beating everyone off the blocks."

Sam Freas turned **reaction training** from an afterthought into a **daily non-negotiable**. His sprinters didn't just swim fast—they REACTED fast. And in a 50-yard race, 0.1 seconds off the blocks can be the difference between gold and fourth place.

**The best part?** It costs NOTHING. No fancy equipment. No pool time. Just 10 minutes a day of focused, purposeful reaction training.

**Start today. Your sprinters will thank you when they're touching the wall first.**
