- Values: relationship > outcomes, no blame, athlete as expert
- Communication framework
- Key turning points
- Lessons for coaches and athletes

**Core quote:**
> "I don't care how it goes. As long as I can provide an environment where you can leave the sport with a better relationship than you started with."

---

### 8. [Paris 2024 Olympic Journey](./paris-2024.md)
**The complete story**

From comeback to gold:
- The Doha learning experience
- The training decision (staying in Brisbane)
- The mental break (April-May 2024)
- The coffee 3 hours before the final
- Race strategy (rep in reserve → go for gold)
- Post-Paris: Singapore defense
- The legacy

**Historic achievement:**
- Australia's first-ever Olympic men's 50m freestyle gold
- At age 30
- Training 3-4x/week

---

## Quick Reference

### Training Week (Race-Specific Phase)
- **Monday:** Gym + suited 35m sprints (3-5 reps)
- **Tuesday:** OFF or technique
- **Wednesday:** Technique session
- **Thursday:** Gym + suited 25m sprints (4-6 reps)
- **Friday:** Technique or OFF
- **Saturday:** Technique or light work
- **Sunday:** OFF

**Total volume:** 300-500m sprint volume per week

### Strength Benchmarks
- **Pull-ups:** Progressing toward 2.0x bodyweight (77.5kg at 85kg BW)
- **Vertical jump:** 48cm → 63cm (direct correlation to 15m time)
- **Trap bar deadlift:** Heavy singles for maintenance
- **Gym frequency:** 3x/week (strength phase), 2x/week (race phase)

### Technique Anchor Points
1. **Hollow-body core activation** — never breaks through entire stroke
2. **Overhead stretch position** — hit at every full extension
3. **Rib rotation** — not just hip rotation
4. **Kick timing** — deep kick links to opposite arm entry
5. **Diagonal pressure** — down and back, not just back

### Resistance Guidelines
- **Heavy block:** 2-3x/week, 150-300m per session
- **Power phase:** 2x/week, 100-200m per session
- **Race phase:** 0-1x/week, 50-100m optional
- **Recovery:** Minimum 48 hours between resistance sessions

---

## The Results

**Timeline:**
- October 2022: 22.8 (comeback swim)
- May 2023: 21.8 (breakthrough, 8 months later)
- July 2023: 21.10 (World Champion)
- February 2024: 21.06 heat at Doha
- August 2024: 21.25 (Olympic Champion)
- August 2025: World Champion (defended)

**Key stats:**
- From 22.8 to 21.10 in 8 months
- Vertical jump: +15cm
- Time to 15m: 5.56 → 5.02
- Takeoff velocity: 4.7 m/s → 5.3 m/s

---

## Who's Following This Approach?

**Athletes using similar methods:**
- Andre Bekker: 21.9 → 21.4
- Santo Condorelli: PB in his 30s
- ASP squad (Santos Silva coaching)
- Multiple masters swimmers (40s+ with PBs)

**The growing movement:**
> "Triple digits of people have messaged me about lifetime PBs. People in their mid-40s who retired 20 years ago, coming back and loving the sport. That's massively rewarding."

---

## The Paradigm Shifts

### Old thinking → New thinking

**Volume:**
- Old: More is better (30-40k/week)
- New: Quality over quantity (1-2k/week at race pace)

**Age:**
- Old: Peak at 22-24
- New: Peak when optimized (30+ possible)

**Coaching:**
- Old: Coach writes program, athlete executes
- New: Athlete leads, coach guides

**Taper:**
- Old: Rest into trials, build to Olympics
- New: Extend race-pace exposure post-trials

**Recovery:**
- Old: Push through mental fatigue
- New: Mental breaks are part of training

---

## Cam's Background

**Early career:**
- Trained with Dennis Cottrell
- Main event at 14: 5k open water (!)
- Trained with Grant Hackett, Sun Yang
- First 50m national qualifier: Later in career
- 2013: 1:45 200m freestyle (first national title)

**Traditional training era:**
- 50m, 100m, 200m freestyle
- High volume, 9-10 sessions/week
- International success but inconsistent
- Rio 2016: Disappointment, retirement

**The comeback:**
- 18 months off (retirement)
- October 2022: Return with Tim Lane
- Completely new approach
- Rest is history

---

## Looking Ahead

**LA 2028:**
- Cam will be 34 years old
- Currently planning to compete
- Proving swimmers can perform longer with smart training

**Documentary:**
- 1-hour film coming to YouTube
- Behind-the-scenes of the journey
- Technical details of the approach
- Team interviews

**Content & sharing:**
> "I want to package this and put it out there. We need more swim content. The more people who get on board and put their own twist on it, the more we accelerate the evolution of the sport."

---

## Key Quotes

**On volume:**
> "The relevance of 30-40k to a 50 freestyle is drastically different from the relevance it has to managing a 7-9 session week."

**On technique:**
> "As you move at a certain speed, the way your body moves changes. That's a skill, like a handstand in gymnastics."

**On neural fatigue:**
> "Bulgarian method weightlifters expect 18-24 month depressed periods. It's not psychological weakness—it's biological consequence."

**On the 50m:**
> "It's no heroic feat of endurance. 17 seconds of swimming. Anyone can go hard for 17 seconds."

**On partnership:**
> "Deep down, this isn't possible without Tim."

# The Cam McEvoy - Tim Lane Partnership
**A revolutionary coach-athlete relationship that redefined collaboration**

**Source:** Brett Hawke podcast, Swimnerd interviews (2023-2024)  
**Context:** October 2022 → Paris 2024 Olympic gold  
**Result:** World title, Olympic gold, paradigm shift in coaching dynamics

---

## How They Met: June 2022

### The Setup

**Cam's situation:**
- 18 months out of swimming
- Had a vision for a new training approach
- Created a detailed document of his ideas
- Needed a space with freedom to experiment
- Sent document to several coaches — "most left on read"

**Bobby Hurley:** The connector
- Cam showed Bobby his document
- Bobby instantly thought of Tim
- Set up a meeting at Sydney Airport Pullman

**Tim's situation:**
- Club coach on Northern Beaches of Sydney
- College coaching background (UNC-Wilmington, Notre Dame, Villanova)
- Working with Bobby Hurley (who'd returned from US)
- Had no pre-existing relationship with Cam

### The First Meeting

**Cam's expectations:**
- Show Tim the document
- Get feedback and guidance
- Find a training space

**What actually happened:**

**Tim's response:**
> "I don't care how it goes. As long as I can provide an environment where, whatever happens, when it's done, you can leave the sport with a much better relationship than you started with."

**The foundation:**
- Not about medals or times
- About relationship with the sport
- About sustainable excellence
- About Cam's wellbeing first

**Cam's reaction:**
> "The grounding seeds of these last two years were from probably the best possible space you could start from."

---

## The Role Definition: CEO and Caddy

### Tim's Philosophy

**The golf analogy:**
- Cam is the professional golfer
- Tim is the caddy
- Must assemble a full support staff (swing coach, physio, mindset coach, S&C, etc.)
- But the athlete hits the shot

**Tim's approach:**
> "Cam and I are on the 18th fairway. I tell him the wind's off the right, 10 yards uphill, pin at the back—I think you should hit a 6-iron. If Cam says 'I hear you but I'm pretty committed to a 7-iron,' I'm going to pat him on the back and tell him to go get it. Because he's the guy with the shot."

### Not Coach and Athlete — Partners

**What this looked like:**

**Traditional:** Coach writes program → athlete executes  
**McEvoy/Lane:** Cam writes program → Tim provides guidance/expertise → collaborative refinement

**Tim:**
> "I challenged myself to go into this as if I know nothing. Cam knows more about the feeling of swimming fast, what it takes to swim fast, than I ever will. He's been 21.4 and 1.45. I really backed myself to just learn as much as possible."

**The dynamic:**
- Cam presents training block ideas
- Tim offers technical expertise (resistance training, pacing, biomechanics)
- Collaborative discussion on implementation
- Cam makes final call as CEO
- Tim supports and guides execution

---

## The Values That Grounded Everything

### 1. Performance First (When Merited)

**The pre-Paris decision:**
- Australian team high-performance plan: Taper into trials, then travel to Europe
- Cam/Tim's plan: Taper into trials, then train at that pace for 3-4 weeks, then rest
- Needed to train at low-21 pace right after trials
- Wanted to stay in Brisbane, not travel to Europe camp

**The conversation with Rowan Taylor (Swimming Australia head coach):**
- Cam and Tim presented their reasoning
- Not about "doing their own thing"
- About maximizing Cam's specific performance needs
- Rowan: "Performance-first approach will be taken when merited."
- Approval given

**Tim:**
> "It could have easily been viewed as the wrong choice if Paris wasn't successful. But Rowan's willingness to take that approach was greatly appreciated."

**The result:**
- Arrived in Paris 2 weeks after rest of team
- Seamless integration
- Gold medal

### 2. No Blame, Only Learning

**Tim's philosophy:**
> "We're never in a situation where if there's a negative outcome, there's a blame game. 'You didn't do this, I didn't do that.' That doesn't even factor in."

**Why this matters:**
- Values-driven relationship
- Shared goals (Cam's goals)
- Space for experimentation and failure
- Psychological safety for risk-taking

**Example: Doha 2024**
- Went max in heat (21.06 PR)
- Went max in semi (21.13)
- Went max in final (21.17) — beaten by Bokov
- No blame. Just: "What did we learn? What do we validate? What do we change?"

### 3. The Athlete Is the Expert on Their Body

**Knowing when to call it:**

**Monday before Sydney Open (May 2023):**
- Planned: Double day, 2x 35m suited
- Reality: Cam was neurally fried, mentally broken
- Tim: "Go home. Get to Sydney safely. Good luck Friday."

**The text exchange Friday morning:**
- Cam: "Not feeling great but just going to approach this like I have a bag of rocks in my head and not overthink it."
- Tim: "Cool."
- Result: 21.8 (breakthrough)

**Tim:**
> "That highlighted the importance of having somebody call it when it needs to be called—but not make a big deal about it. One session here isn't going to cost you a season."

**The flip side:**
- Cam feeling "Godly" and wanting more reps
- Tim: "Hell no, not doing another rep."
- Even when you feel good, stick to the plan

---

## The Support Network

### Beyond Cam and Tim

**The full team:**

**Biomechanics:** Nick Smith (QAS)
- Present at every suited session
- Analyzed every dive, every split
- Refined start mechanics continuously

**Strength & Mobility:** Matt Smith
- Designed strength progressions
- Developed mobility protocols
- Prevented injuries through movement quality

**QAS Staff:**
- Gan (S&C)
- Beck (sports science)
- Dan (current S&C)
- Physiotherapy team

**Tim's role:**
> "I'm the nucleus keeping everyone updated with our direction, where Cam is at each point, any new ideas, relaying back and forth."

**Why this matters:**
- No one person has all the answers
- Specialization allows excellence
- Communication is key
- Cam is still the CEO

---

## The Turning Points

### 1. May 2023: The Breakthrough

**Context:** Cam stuck at 22.0-22.2 for months

**The training block:**
- 3 weeks of doubles (Monday/Thursday)
- 2x 35m suited, AM and PM
- Week 1: Good
- Week 2: Okay
- Week 3, Monday PM: Broken

**Tim's call:** "Go home."

**Cam's Sydney Open (4 days later):** 21.8

**What they learned:**
- Neural fatigue is real and can't be pushed through
- Recovery is training
- Trust the process even when uncertain
- One session doesn't make or break a season

### 2. April-May 2024: The Mental Break

**The situation:**
- Post-Doha (Feb 2024), Cam was mentally fried
- Motivation to suit up and chase times was declining
- Trials and Olympics approaching (3-4 months out)

**The counterintuitive decision:**
- Take a mental break from timed work
- Continue physical training
- Stop putting every rep under the microscope
- Swim in regular training suit

**Tim:**
> "We had two options: grind through because it's an Olympic year, or take a step back mentally even if it feels like your back's against the wall when June comes."

**Cam's Bulgarian method parallel:**
- Weightlifters on Bulgarian method (multiple daily 1RMs)
- Expected to go through 18-24 month depressed periods
- Not psychological weakness—biological reality
- Neural fatigue has mental consequences

**The scary part:**
- "Did we make the wrong decision?" (mid-May doubts)
- Olympics in 3 months
- Everyone else grinding

**The outcome:**
- Refreshed by June
- Peak performance at trials (21.3)
- Gold in Paris

### 3. Paris: The Coffee 3 Hours Before the Final

**Cam's words:**
> "I've checked off every box I ever dreamed of in the last couple years. I'm back in an Olympic final, never thought I'd be here. I'm really okay with any outcome tonight."

**Tim:**
- "Not a company line or coping mechanism—I could feel it."
- 20 minutes of silence together
- Nothing more needed to be said

**Walking to the call room:**
- Cam: "Regardless of outcome, thanks for everything. I wouldn't be here without you."
- Tim: "I definitely wouldn't be here without you. Getting to know the man you are over the last couple years..."
- Both getting emotional
- Tim: "Quick, get in there. Time out. Go."

**Tim:**
> "Two grown men walking in all sentimental and emotional wasn't how I envisaged our last interaction, but that's just the kind of people we are."

**The result:**
- Cam hits the performance
- Olympic gold
- Regardless of placement, they would have been proud

---

## The Communication Framework

### Daily Check-Ins

**Cam's process:**
- Daily updates to Tim on how he's feeling
- Goals for that session
- Why he wants to do what he wants to do
- Reasoning behind the choices

**Tim's process:**
- Listen to Cam's ideas
- Offer technical expertise from his experience
- "What if we changed this to this?"
- "What if we approached it this way?"

**The negotiation:**
- Back-and-forth dialogue
- Cam has the vision
- Tim has the experience
- Collaborative refinement

### The Pivot

**Example scenario:**
- Planned 3-week block
- Halfway through, neural fatigue accumulating
- Need to pivot to different approach

**The psychological component:**
- Is Cam okay with the change?
- Can he back himself in this pivot?
- Will he trust the new direction?

**Tim's role:**
> "Provide the vehicle of confidence to back himself and go to that next point."

**Cam's role:**
- Trust the process
- Trust the partnership
- Execute with conviction

---

## What Made It Work

### 1. No Pre-Existing Relationship

**Tim:**
> "Cam and I hadn't met before, maybe in passing. I think that was a big part of this being successful. We had no preconceived ideas of each other."

**Why this mattered:**
- No baggage from past performances
- No "Cam always does this" assumptions
- Fresh perspective on both sides
- Open to new approaches

### 2. Complementary Expertise

**Cam brought:**
- World-class swimming experience
- Deep knowledge of strength training
- Understanding of psychology and depth psychology
- Willingness to experiment
- Ownership mentality

**Tim brought:**
- Technical swimming expertise (especially resistance training)
- College coaching experience
- Fresh perspective
- Mentorship and psychological support
- Humility and willingness to learn

### 3. Shared Values

**Both believed:**
- Relationship with the sport > outcomes
- Process > results
- Sustainable excellence > short-term wins
- Athlete wellbeing first
- No ego, no blame
- Continuous learning

### 4. Clearly Defined Roles

**Cam's job description:** Swim fast and lead the vision  
**Tim's job description:** Support Cam's goals as best as possible

**Everyone else's job:** Support Cam's goals within their specialty

**No role confusion = no conflict**

---

## Lessons for Coaches and Athletes

### For Coaches

**1. Check your ego at the door**
- You don't have all the answers
- The athlete knows things you don't
- Be willing to learn from them

**2. Build the environment first**
- Relationship with sport > outcomes
- Athlete wellbeing > medals
- Trust > control

**3. Know when to call it**
- One bad session ≠ failed season
- Recovery is training
- Trust the athlete's body signals

**4. Be the caddy, not the dictator**
- Provide expertise and guidance
- Let the athlete hit the shot
- Support whatever choice they make with conviction

**5. Assemble a team**
- You can't be the expert in everything
- Build a network of specialists
- Coordinate, don't monopolize

### For Athletes

**1. Take ownership**
- You're the CEO of your career
- Assemble the team you need
- Make the final calls

**2. Communicate relentlessly**
- Daily updates on how you're feeling
- Why you want to do what you want to do
- Be honest about doubts and fears

**3. Trust your partners**
- When you ask for advice, consider it
- They see things you don't
- But you make the final call

**4. Values > outcomes**
- Define success beyond medals
- Relationship with the sport matters
- Sustainable excellence > peak-and-crash

**5. Build a team, not a hierarchy**
- Coach, physio, S&C, sports psych, biomechanist
- Everyone has a specialty
- Collaborate, don't command

---

## The Legacy

**What Cam and Tim proved:**
- Elite athletes can thrive with athlete-led programming
- Coaches can serve without controlling
- Partnership > hierarchy
- Less volume, more quality
- Relationship-first approach produces results

**The paradigm shift:**
- From "coach knows best" → "athlete and coach collaborate"
- From "volume equals dedication" → "quality equals performance"
- From "one-size-fits-all" → "individualized to the person"
- From "outcomes first" → "process and wellbeing first"

**Tim:**
> "If I've just got 3 weeks with one of the fastest swimmers of all time, that's a blessing for me as a coach. What I can learn from someone who's been 21.4 and 1.45—I'll take whatever I can get."

**Cam:**
> "Deep down, this isn't possible without Tim. The space and freedom, the mental guidance, the mentorship—integral through the whole thing."

---

- [Cam McEvoy: Psychology and Mindset](./psychology-mindset.md)

# Cam McEvoy's Resistance Training Protocols
**How the 1080 Sprint and resistance work revolutionized his sprinting**

**Source:** Multiple interviews (2023-2024)  
**Context:** October 2022 → Paris 2024  
**Equipment:** 1080 Sprint, Destroyo Power Rack, parachutes

---

## The Starting Point: Never Done Resistance Training

**October 2022:**
- Cam had **never** done resistance swimming before
- Tim Lane asked: "Have you ever done any resistance work?"
- Answer: No

**First session:**
- 5 reps x 25m with blue/red parachute
- Went home and napped for 2 hours
- Completely neurally fried
- "I didn't know what to expect from this stuff"

**The buildup:**
- Took weeks/months to build tolerance
- Progressive overload through exposure
- Eventually built to full resistance blocks

---

## Why Resistance Training?

### The Funnel Analogy

**The concept:**
1. Build the biggest "funnel" possible (max strength in gym)
2. Open the exit hole (technique/coordination)
3. Swimming speed = flow rate through the funnel

**Why resistance specifically:**
- Can't apply max strength at bodyweight swimming initially
- Body is optimized for heavy loads after strength block
- Resistance creates the bridge between gym and pool
- Strengthens technique at specific joint angles

**McEvoy:**
> "At that point in the season, I'm so optimized for strength output that I can put a lot more into the water under resistance than I could at bodyweight. Plus, the coordination of freestyle at bodyweight will be less than under resistance."

### Force-Velocity Curve Progression

**Seasonal progression:**
1. **Gym phase:** Far left on curve (high force, low velocity)
2. **Resistance phase:** Moving right (moderate force, moderate velocity)
3. **Bodyweight phase:** Far right (low force, high velocity)

**The strategy:** Take time moving from left to right across the season.

---

## The 8-Week Resistance Block (Post-Fukuoka 2023)

### Structure

**Duration:** 8 weeks  
**Frequency:** Every single session on the Destroyo/1080  
**Volume:** ~5.6k equivalent sprint volume under tension

**Format:**
- Broken 50s at various resistances
- NO bodyweight swimming
- All work under load
- Timed electronically to 0.01

### The Intensity

**Neural load:**
- Massive neurological fatigue
- Learned about neural vs. metabolic fatigue
- Required strategic recovery planning
- Led to mental burnout by Doha (needed April-May mental break)

**The learning:**
> "The volume of time sprinting under tension equaled 5.6k of 50m sprints. That's a huge 8-week period of extreme neural load, strengthening of strength-endurance, and strengthening of technique."

### What It Develops

**Primary adaptations:**
1. **Strength-endurance** — ability to maintain high force output repeatedly
2. **Technique under load** — pattern locked in under resistance
3. **Neural efficiency** — more muscle fiber recruitment
4. **Joint angle strength** — specific positions reinforced

**McEvoy:**
> "You can't fake it under resistance. It forces perfect coordination."

---

## Resistance Equipment and Weights

### 1080 Sprint / Destroyo Power Rack

**What it is:**
- Motorized resistance system
- Straps to waist
- Provides constant resistance through stroke
- Can adjust weight precisely
- Gives 5m splits electronically

**Typical weights (in the tub):**
- **Light:** 10-15kg
- **Medium:** 20-30kg  
- **Heavy:** 30-40kg
- **Very heavy:** 40kg+

**Conversion to Destroyo Power Rack:**
- 3kg on 1080 ≈ 30-35kg in Destroyo tub
- Used for technique maintenance with fins

### Parachutes

**Colors (typical progression):**
- **Red:** Lightest
- **Blue:** Medium
- **Yellow/Green:** Heavy

**Use cases:**
- Warm-up progressions
- Travel situations (no 1080 access)
- Variety in resistance profile (different feel than belt)

---

## Resistance Training Protocols

### Warm-Up Progression (Standard)

**5-6 x 15-20m progression:**
1. Rep 1: Red parachute (~70% effort)
2. Rep 2: Blue parachute (~75% effort)
3. Rep 3: Medium 1080 resistance (~85% effort)
4. Rep 4: Light 1080 resistance (~90% effort)
5. Rep 5: Bodyweight (~95% effort)
6. Rep 6: Fins (~100% effort)

**Purpose:**
- Moves from heavy force/low velocity → low force/high velocity
- Primes nervous system
- Reinforces force-velocity curve adaptation
- Teaches body to maintain technique across resistance spectrum

### Main Set Examples

**Example 1: Broken 50s**
- 3-5 x (25m + 25m) @ medium-heavy resistance
- Rest: 3-5 minutes between reps
- Goal: Maintain target times across all reps
- Electronic timing on every rep

**Example 2: Build Series**
- 20m heavy
- 25m medium-heavy
- 30m medium
- 35m light
- 40m bodyweight
- Rest: 3-4 minutes between reps

**Example 3: French Contrast (Power Phase)**
- Weighted pull-ups (5 reps at 80-85% max)
- Immediately into pool
- 25m resisted sprint (medium weight)
- Rest 4-5 minutes
- Repeat 4-6 times

### Volume Guidelines

**Resistance block (heavy focus):**
- 2-3x per week
- 150-300m total resisted volume per session
- Example: 6 x 50m = 300m
- Rest intervals: 3-5 minutes

**Power transition block:**
- 2x per week
- 100-200m total resisted volume
- Combined with gym contrast work
- Focus on explosiveness

**Maintenance (race-specific phase):**
- 1x per week (optional)
- 50-100m total
- Keep feel for applying force
- Don't interfere with bodyweight adaptation

---

## Technique Under Resistance

### What Changes

**Bodyweight swimming:**
- Relies more on timing and rhythm
- Can "fake" technique with momentum
- Easier to hide flaws

**Resistance swimming:**
- Exposes technique flaws immediately
- Can't rely on momentum
- Must maintain perfect positions
- Forces proper force application angles

**The benefit:**
> "If you can hold perfect technique under heavy resistance, it becomes automatic at bodyweight."

### Key Technical Points Under Resistance

**1. Maintain hollow-body core activation**
- Even harder under load
- Can't let activation break
- Builds isometric endurance

**2. Hit overhead stretch position every stroke**
- Resistance forces you to find strong positions
- Weak positions collapse under load
- Reinforces optimal joint angles

**3. Press diagonally downward**
- More obvious under resistance
- Horizontal pressing doesn't work
- Teaches correct pressure angles

**4. Kick timing becomes critical**
- Mistimed kick = instant breakdown
- Resistance highlights coordination errors
- Fixes itself through practice

---

## When to Use Resistance Training

### Early Season (Strength → Power Transition)

**Timing:** After max strength block, 8-16 weeks before major competition

**Purpose:**
- Bridge from gym to pool
- Convert gym strength to swimming-specific strength
- Build work capacity at race-specific angles

**Volume:** High (majority of swimming is resisted)

### Mid-Season (Power Phase)

**Timing:** 6-12 weeks before major competition

**Purpose:**
- French contrast protocols
- Maintain strength while adding speed
- Progressive decrease in resistance

**Volume:** Moderate (some resisted, some bodyweight)

### Late Season (Race-Specific Phase)

**Timing:** 0-6 weeks before major competition

**Purpose:**
- Occasional exposure to maintain feel
- Mostly bodyweight swimming
- Minimal interference

**Volume:** Low to none

---

## The Japan Example (December 2024)

### The Setup

**Training leading into Japan Open:**
- October-December: Heavy gym block
- 1-2x per week swimming: 6 x 10m with fins + 3kg resistance
- Total weekly volume: ~120m
- No bodyweight swimming
- No suited training

**The test:**
- One week of no gym before Japan
- Slightly more technique work that week
- Suited up at Japan Open

**The result:**
- 21.70 (fastest in-season swim ever)
- 25m split = same as Olympics (21.25)
- Top speed maintained with almost zero swimming
- Died in last 25m (expected — no speed endurance work)

**The insight:**
> "Top speed (first 25m) can be kept with very little swimming. Speed endurance (last 25m) requires specific work."

---

## Resistance vs. Bodyweight Adaptation

### Why Not Just Bodyweight?

**McEvoy's reasoning:**

**After strength block:**
- Body optimized for heavy loads
- Neural pathways wired for high-force output
- Jumping straight to bodyweight = mismatch
- Need bridge to transfer strength

**Resistance provides:**
- Gradual transition from gym → pool
- Specificity at the joint angles that matter
- Overload that bodyweight can't provide at that point
- Technical reinforcement under load

### The Timing Challenge

**Too much resistance:**
- Neural fatigue accumulates
- Can interfere with bodyweight speed
- Motivation drops (Doha experience)
- Need recovery time

**Too little resistance:**
- Miss opportunity to strengthen technique
- Don't fully transfer gym gains
- Potentially leave performance on the table

**The balance:**
- Heavy resistance early (8-12 weeks out)
- Moderate resistance middle (6-8 weeks out)
- Light/none late (0-6 weeks out)

---

## Common Mistakes

### 1. Too Much Volume

**The error:** Treating resistance like aerobic training

**The fix:** Think strength training, not yardage
- Quality over quantity
- Full recovery between reps
- Stop when technique breaks down

### 2. Wrong Resistance Level

**The error:** Using weight that allows "swimming through it"

**The fix:** Resistance should:
- Slow you down significantly
- Force perfect technique
- Feel like you're pulling against a wall
- Not be sustainable for more than 25-50m

### 3. Poor Technique Under Load

**The error:** "Just get through it"

**The fix:**
- Every rep must have perfect technique
- If technique breaks, lighten the load
- Film and analyze
- No garbage reps

### 4. Not Recovering Enough

**The error:** Resistance work on back-to-back days

**The fix:**
- Minimum 48 hours between resistance sessions
- Treat like heavy squats/deadlifts
- Neural system needs recovery
- Quality sessions 2-3x/week > volume

---

## The Sprint-Float-Sprint Protocol

**Origin:** Track and field speed-endurance work

**Pool application:**
- Dive in, max effort 0-15m
- Easy speed ("float") 15-25m
- Max effort 25-35m
- Electronic timing on each segment

**Why it works:**
1. Teaches pacing and control
2. Develops coordination at speed when semi-fatigued
3. Often produces faster 35m times than all-out 35m
4. Builds race-specific endurance
5. Lower neural cost than max-max-max

**When to use:**
- Power transition phase
- Race-specific phase
- As progression from shorter reps
- When teaching race execution

**Resistance variation:**
- Can do first 15m resisted, rest bodyweight
- Or first 25m resisted, rest bodyweight
- Bridges resistance → bodyweight adaptation

---

## Periodization: When Resistance Fits

**Phase 1: Max Strength (Oct-Feb)**
- Gym: Heavy, 3-5x/week
- Swimming: Technique only, 2-3x/week, no resistance

**Phase 2: Strength → Power Transition (Feb-Mar)**
- Gym: Maintenance
- Swimming: Heavy resistance, 2-3x/week, low volume
- French contrast protocols

**Phase 3: Power → Speed Transition (Mar-Apr)**
- Gym: Maintenance
- Swimming: Moderate resistance, 2x/week, moderate volume
- Start adding bodyweight work

**Phase 4: Race-Specific (Apr-July)**
- Gym: Minimal maintenance
- Swimming: Mostly bodyweight, 4x/week, race-pace volume
- Occasional light resistance for feel

---

## Equipment Recommendations

**Essential:**
- 1080 Sprint OR Destroyo Power Rack (preferred)
- Electronic timing system (must have 5m splits)
- Video camera (technique analysis)

**Nice to have:**
- Multiple parachute sizes
- Fins (for warm-up progressions)
- Force plates (for dive analysis)

**Budget option:**
- Parachutes only
- Manual timing (less precise but functional)
- Film on phone

---

## Key Principles (Summary)

### ✅ **Resistance Bridges Gym to Pool**
- Don't go straight from heavy squats to bodyweight swimming
- Use resistance to transfer strength
- Progressive decrease across season

### ✅ **Think Strength Training, Not Yardage**
- Quality over quantity
- Full recovery between reps
- 150-300m per session is plenty

### ✅ **Perfect Technique Every Rep**
- Resistance exposes flaws
- No garbage reps
- If technique breaks, stop or lighten load

### ✅ **Periodize Strategically**
- Heavy early season
- Moderate mid-season
- Light/none late season

### ✅ **Manage Neural Fatigue**
- Resistance work is neurally demanding
- Need 48+ hours between sessions
- Monitor motivation and mood
- Take mental breaks when needed

---

- [Cam McEvoy: Technique](./technique.md)

# Cam McEvoy: The Paris 2024 Olympic Journey
**From retirement to Olympic gold — the complete story**

**Source:** Brett Hawke podcast, Inside with Brett Hawke (2024)  
**Result:** 21.25 Olympic gold, Australia's first-ever men's 50m freestyle champion  
**Age:** 30 years old

---

## The Comeback Timeline

**2016:** Rio Olympics disappointment → retirement  
**2022 (October):** Return to swimming, first 50m: 22.8  
**2023 (May):** Breakthrough 21.8 at Sydney Open  
**2023 (July):** 21.10 World Champion (Fukuoka)  
**2024 (February):** 21.06 heat at Doha (beat by Bokov in final)  
**2024 (June):** 21.3 at Australian Trials  
**2024 (August):** 21.25 Olympic gold (Paris)

---

## The Doha Learning Experience (February 2024)

### What Happened

**The races:**
- **Heat:** 21.06 (PR) — went ALL OUT
- **Semi:** 21.13 — went ALL OUT again
- **Final:** 21.17 — beaten by Bokov (21.14)

**The mistake:**
> "It doesn't matter how fit you are. If you're at 100% max and go max again 12 hours later, then max again, fitness can't repair that."

### What They Learned

**The "rep in reserve" concept:**
- Can't go max in every round
- Heat/semi: Controlled effort, qualify for final
- Final: NOW go for gold

**Ben Proud's model:**
- Always gets faster from heat → semi → final
- That's strategy, not luck
- Must have energy in reserve for the final

**Parallel to gym:**
> "You do a 5RM squat, come back 12 hours later and hit it again—you'll probably still do it. But come back the next day for a third time at 100% max? Not happening. Same in the 50m."

---

## The Pre-Paris Training Decision

### The Standard Approach (Rejected)

**Traditional path:**
1. Taper into trials
2. Rest after trials (recovery from competition)
3. Travel to Europe with team
4. Slowly build back up over weeks
5. Taper again for Olympics

### The McEvoy/Lane Approach (Chosen)

**Their path:**
1. Taper into trials
2. Hit 21.3-21.4 at trials (first exposure to low-21 speed)
3. Get back in pool 2-3 days later
4. Train AT that pace for 3-4 weeks (maximum time at race speed)
5. Stay in Brisbane (don't travel to Europe)
6. Then rest/freshen 2-3 weeks into Olympics

**The rationale:**
> "Trials is the first time we get exposure to low-21 speed. We don't want to let that go. Extend trials out for 3-4 weeks, get as much volume as we can at that speed, THEN freshen up."

### Getting Permission

**The conversation with Rowan Taylor (Swimming Australia head coach):**
- Cam and Tim presented their plan
- Not about "doing their own thing"
- About maximizing Cam's specific performance needs
- Rowan: "Performance-first approach will be taken when merited"
- Approval given

**Tim Lane:**
> "It could have easily been viewed as the wrong choice if Paris wasn't successful. But Rowan's willingness to approach it this way was greatly appreciated."

**The outcome:**
- Arrived in Paris 2 weeks after the rest of the team
- Seamless integration
- Gold medal

---

## The Mental Break (April-May 2024)

### The Problem

**Post-Doha burnout:**
- Cam was mentally fried from constant microscope on training
- Motivation to suit up and chase times was declining
- Olympics approaching in 3-4 months
- Traditional thinking: "It's an Olympic year, grind through it"

### The Counterintuitive Decision

**What they did:**
- Took mental break from timed work
- Continued physical training
- Stopped analyzing every rep
- Swam in regular suit, not tech suit
- Reduced pressure

**Tim's philosophy:**
> "We had two options: grind through because it's an Olympic year, or take a step back mentally. We chose the mental break."

**Cam's Bulgarian method parallel:**
- Weightlifters using Bulgarian method (multiple daily max lifts)
- Expected to go through 18-24 month depressed periods
- Not psychological weakness — biological consequence of neural fatigue
- Swimming max sprints has same neurological impact

### The Scary Part

**Mid-May doubts:**
- "Did we make the wrong decision?"
- Olympics in 3 months
- Everyone else grinding
- Going against conventional wisdom

**The result:**
- Refreshed by June
- Peak performance at trials
- Gold at Olympics

---

## The Coffee: 3 Hours Before the Final

### The Conversation

**Location:** Paris, 3 hours before 50m freestyle final

**Cam's words:**
> "I've checked off every box I ever dreamed of in the last couple years. I'm back in an Olympic final—never thought I'd be here. I'm really okay with any outcome tonight."

**Tim:**
- "Not a company line or coping mechanism—I could feel it."
- 20 minutes of silence together
- Nothing more needed to be said

### Walking to the Call Room

**Cam to Tim:**
> "Regardless of outcome, thanks for everything. I wouldn't be here without you. Means the world to me."

**Tim to Cam:**
> "I definitely wouldn't be here without you. Getting to know the man you are over these last couple years..."

**Both:**
- Getting emotional
- Cam: "I'm getting emotional"
- Tim: "I am too"
- Tim: "Quick, get in there. Time out. Go!"

**Tim:**
> "Two grown men walking in all sentimental wasn't how I envisaged our last interaction before an Olympic final, but that's just the kind of people we are."

---

## The Race Strategy

### Heat: 21.32 — Rep in Reserve

**Execution:**
- Controlled first 25m (NOT all-out like Doha)
- Smooth, efficient
- Qualified easily
- Kept energy for semi

**Post-race feeling:**
- Shocked at how comfortable it felt
- Same 25m split as Olympic final would be
- Validation of the approach

### Semi: 21.28 — Still in Reserve

**Execution:**
- Slightly faster than heat
- Still controlled
- Still had gears left
- Mission: Get lane in final with max energy

**Strategic goal:**
> "Hit the heat, hit the semi, and you just want to get that lane in the final with as much energy in reserve as you can."

### Final: 21.25 — Go for Gold

**Pre-race:**
- Flow-Lévy getting the crowd clapping
- Cam: "90-95% of the crowd was eyes on Flo, so I can just sit back and do what I've trained for"
- Crowd energy was incredible
- Zero pressure—all eyes on someone else

**Execution:**
- First 49 meters: "Felt pretty nice"
- Last meter: "Finish was horrific"
  - Crossed over
  - Long reach
  - Panic
  - "Pure heart and soul, just spin and get your hand on the wall"

**The result:**
- 21.25
- 0.02 ahead of Ben Proud (21.27)
- Australia's first-ever Olympic men's 50m freestyle gold

---

## The Significance

### For Australia

**Historic firsts:**
- First Australian man to win Olympic 50m freestyle
- Oldest Australian to win World Championship 50m freestyle (32 at Singapore 2025)
- Proved low-volume, high-quality approach works at highest level

### For Sprint Swimming

**Paradigm shifts proven:**
1. **Volume:** Less can be more (3-4x/week vs. 9-10x/week)
2. **Age:** 30+ swimmers can peak (with right approach)
3. **Coaching:** Athlete-led programming can work
4. **Taper:** Extending race-pace exposure works
5. **Recovery:** Mental breaks are training

### For Cam Personally

**Redemption:**
- Rewrote Olympic narrative after Rio disappointment
- Proved his approach to himself and the world
- "Took the chip off my shoulder"

**Family:**
- Got engaged post-Paris
- Had first son (Hartley)
- Balanced elite performance with life
- "I just wanted to kick back and relax"

**Legacy:**
> "There's a lot of joy in packaging this and putting it out for others to learn from. Seeing people hit lifetime PBs—people in their mid-40s who retired 20 years ago coming back and loving the sport. That's massively rewarding."

---

## Post-Paris: The Singapore Defense (August 2025)

### The Training Block

**October-December 2024:**
- Extended gym block (heaviest ever)
- 1-2x/week swimming: 6 x 10m with fins + 3kg resistance
- Total weekly volume: ~120m
- Body weight: 87kg (Paris) → 92kg (Singapore prep)
- Focus: Maximum strength and mobility

**December test:**
- Japan Open: 21.70 (fastest in-season ever)
- 25m split = same as Olympics
- Proved top speed maintained with minimal swimming

**January-July 2025:**
- Last week of March: Back to swimming "properly"
- 4x/week swimming, 3x/week gym
- Monday/Thursday: Suited sprinting
- Tuesday/Friday: Technique + bodyweight adaptation
- Total volume per session: 1-2k

### Singapore World Championships

**Result:** Successfully defended world title  
**Significance:** Proved Paris wasn't a fluke

---

## The Stats

**Career trajectory:**
- October 2022: 22.8 (comeback)
- May 2023: 21.8 (breakthrough, 8 months later)
- July 2023: 21.10 (World Champion)
- August 2024: 21.25 (Olympic Champion)
- August 2025: World Champion (defended)

**Age progression:**
- 29: World Champion (2023)
- 30: Olympic Champion (2024)
- 31: World Champion again (2025)
- Target: 34 at LA 2028

---

## Lessons from Paris

### 1. Trust the Process

**Even when it seems crazy:**
- Training 3-4x/week when others do 9-10x
- Taking mental breaks 3 months before Olympics
- Staying home when team travels to Europe
- All felt counterintuitive—all worked

### 2. Relationship > Outcomes

**The foundation:**
- Tim's initial goal: Help Cam leave sport with better relationship
- Not about medals or times
- About sustainable excellence
- About wellbeing first

**The result:**
- Got the medals AND the relationship
- Cam is still enjoying the sport
- Planning to continue to LA 2028

### 3. Strategy Matters in the 50m

**The myth:** "The 50m is a crapshoot"

**The reality:**
- Pacing strategy is critical
- Rep in reserve through rounds
- Energy management over 3 swims
- Execute the plan under pressure

### 4. The Athlete Can Lead

**What worked:**
- Cam designed the training
- Tim provided expertise and guidance
- Collaborative refinement
- Cam made final calls
- Tim supported execution

**Why it worked:**
- Clear roles
- Mutual respect
- No ego
- Shared values
- Trust

---

## The Legacy

**What Cam proved:**
- Elite swimmers can thrive past 30
- Low volume works (when done right)
- Strength-based approach transfers to pool
- Athlete-coach partnerships > hierarchies
- Mental health and breaks are part of training
- Family life and elite performance can coexist

**Who's following:**
- Andre Bekker: 21.9 → 21.4
- Santo Condorelli: PB in his 30s
- Multiple age-group and masters swimmers
- Growing movement in sprint community

**The paradigm shift:**
- From "volume equals dedication" → "quality equals performance"
- From "coach knows best" → "athlete and coach collaborate"
- From "peak at 23" → "peak when optimized"
- From "swimming is your life" → "swimming fits in life"

---

# Cam McEvoy Weekly Training Breakdown

**Source:** "How to Sprint Faster: Cam's 2026 Sprint Training Blueprint" (Cam McEvoy Episode 11), pool session video analysis

## Overview

Cam McEvoy's training is radically different from traditional high-volume programs. His weekly swimming volume **never exceeds 1,500m per week** - less than most elite swimmers do in a single warm-up session. This represents a **98% reduction** compared to traditional sprint training.

## Training Philosophy

**"If it's not race pace or faster, it's not in the program."**

This principle eliminates:
- ❌ Aerobic base work
- ❌ Threshold training  
- ❌ Middle-distance sets
- ❌ Easy swimming/recovery sets
- ❌ Traditional periodization phases

## Seasonal Structure

Cam's season is divided into three distinct phases:

### Phase 1: Strength Block (Nov-Feb)

**Duration:** ~3-4 months  
**Swimming frequency:** 3 times/week  
**Swimming focus:** Submax technique maintenance only

**Pool sessions:**
- 7-8 RPE (out of 10)
- Stroke rate: ~58 (vs. 60-63 race rate)
- Total volume: 300-500m per session
- Purpose: Maintain technique while building raw strength in gym

**Gym focus:**
- Maximum strength development
- Heavy compound lifts
- Singles only (no doubles/triples)
- Goal: Increase neural output capacity

**Key insight:** Swimming is purely maintenance during this phase. The real adaptation happens in the gym.

---

### Phase 2: Power/Transition Block (Mid-Feb to March)

**Duration:** ~4 weeks  
**Swimming frequency:** 5 times/week  
**Swimming focus:** Introduce resisted sprinting + technique

**Pool sessions breakdown:**
- **3 technique sessions** (same as Phase 1)
- **2 resisted sprint sessions** (~80-100m each)
  - 4-5 × 20m sprints
  - Moderate resistance (1080 machine)
  - Full recovery between reps

**Gym focus:**
- Transition from max strength to power
- French contrast sets (weighted pull-up → resisted swim)
- Maintain strength while adding speed

**Example session:**
- Heavy pull-ups (gym)
- → Resisted 20m sprint (pool)
- → Rest ~5-10 min
- → Repeat 4-5 times

---

### Phase 3: Race-Specific Block (April-Competition)

**Duration:** 3-4 months  
**Swimming frequency:** 4-5 times/week  
**Swimming focus:** Race-pace volume accumulation

**Pool sessions:**
- Build base of "fast fitness" (not aerobic fitness)
- Every rep at race pace or faster
- Gradually increase intensity as competition approaches
- Taper with reduced volume, increased quality

**Gym focus:**
- Maintenance only
- Minimal fatigue spillover to swimming

---

## Sample Monday Pool Session (From Video)

**Total distance:** ~300-400m  
**Total time:** ~60 minutes (includes rest, coaching, video review)

### Warmup
- 300m loosen
- 2 warm-up reps:
  - 1 × 25 @ body weight (submax)
  - 1 × 20m @ 2kg resistance (1080 machine)

### Main Set
- 1 × 25 max effort dive (body weight, no resistance)
- 4 reps with resistance:
  - 2 × 20m @ 1kg
  - 2 × 20m @ 2kg
- Optional finisher (depends on feel):
  - 1 × 25 body weight max, OR
  - 2 × 20m @ 5kg resistance (if not feeling sharp)

**Total main set distance:** ~100-140m

### Metrics Tracked (Every Rep)
- 10m split
- 15m split
- 20m split
- 25m time
- Stroke rate (target: 62+ strokes/min)
- Segment times

### Recovery
- Hot tub post-session
- Mobility work
- Strategic rest (coach shut down session when Cam started feeling *good* - saved energy for rest of week)

---

## Weekly Schedule Example (Race Prep Phase)

| Day | AM Session | PM Session |
|-----|------------|------------|
| **Monday** | Pool: Sprint speed work (~300m) | - |
| **Tuesday** | Gym: Strength maintenance (singles only) | - |
| **Wednesday** | Pool: Technique + race pace (~400m) | - |
| **Thursday** | Gym: Power work | - |
| **Friday** | Pool: Quality sprints (~300m) | - |
| **Saturday** | Recovery / mobility | - |
| **Sunday** | OFF or light technique | - |

**Weekly total:** ~1,000-1,500m

---

## Equipment & Technology

**Queensland Academy of Sport (4-lane, 25m pool):**
- 1080 Sprint resistance machines (every lane)
- Advanced timing systems
- Real-time metric tracking
- Video analysis capabilities

**Gym equipment:**
- Heavy barbells (max strength work)
- Pull-up stations
- French contrast setup (weights → pool access)

---

## Training Partners

**Regular squad members:**
- **Grayson:** Breaststroker
- **Josh:** Junior sprint freestyler (one of the best in the world)
- **I (from Japan):** Butterfly sprinter

**Coaching staff:**
- **Tim Lane:** Head coach
- **Gerine:** Sports scientist (metrics tracking)

---

## Key Differences vs. Traditional Training

| Aspect | Traditional | Cam McEvoy |
|--------|------------|------------|
| **Weekly volume** | 40,000-70,000m | 1,000-1,500m |
| **Frequency** | 9-10 sessions/week | 3-5 sessions/week |
| **Session length** | 2+ hours | ~1 hour |
| **Aerobic work** | 60-70% of volume | 0% |
| **Race pace work** | 5-10% of volume | 100% |
| **Strength training** | Supplementary | Primary focus (off-season) |
| **Periodization** | Traditional macro/meso/micro | Three distinct blocks |

---

## Volume Context

**To put 1,500m/week in perspective:**

- **Warm-up comparison:** Most elite swimmers do 1,500m+ in a single warm-up
- **Daily comparison:** Many programs do 7,000-10,000m per session (10× Cam's weekly total)
- **Reduction:** 98% less volume than traditional programs

**The McEvoy Method proves:** For sprint events (50m), quality dramatically outweighs quantity.

---

## Why This Works

**Neural adaptation over metabolic:**
- Sprint freestyle is a neuromuscular event, not aerobic
- Maximum strength → maximum power → maximum speed
- Technique quality maintained through low-volume, high-focus sessions
- Fresh nervous system = better power output

**Recovery optimization:**
- Less swimming = more time for strength development
- Minimal accumulated fatigue
- Can go max effort every session (no grinding through fatigue)

**Specificity:**
- Every meter in water is race-relevant
- No "junk volume" that doesn't transfer to 50m performance

---

## Coaching Insight

**From the video:** When Cam started to "feel good" on his last rep (9.50 split), coach Tim Lane immediately shut down the session. 

**Reason:** It's Monday. There's more training this week. Save the energy.

**Lesson:** In low-volume training, managing freshness is more important than "getting your meters in."

---

## Cross-References

- [[training-philosophy|Training Philosophy]] - The McEvoy Method explained
- [[tim-lane-partnership|Tim Lane Partnership]] - Coaching relationship
- [[resistance-training|Resistance Training]] - Gym protocols
- [[technique|50m Freestyle Technique]] - Stroke mechanics
- [[paris-2024|Paris 2024 Olympic Journey]] - Race preparation

---

**Bottom Line:**

Cam McEvoy swims **less in an entire week** than most elite swimmers do before their main set starts. Yet he's the Olympic Champion and former world record holder.

The lesson: **For sprint freestyle, quality beats quantity. Every single time.**

# Mark Schubert: Training Philosophy & Energy Systems

**Source:** *Competitive Swimming: Techniques for Champions* (1990, 1996)  
**Author:** Mark Schubert (legendary USC coach, multiple Olympic teams)  
**Context:** Comprehensive training methodology from one of swimming's greatest coaches  
**Significance:** Foundation principles for developmental swimming — aerobic base, specificity, adaptation

---

## The Big Picture

### Core Training Principles
1. **Specificity of Training** — Train the systems you'll race with
2. **Energy Systems** — Three distinct fuel tanks (ATP-CP, Lactic, Aerobic)
3. **Developmental Training** — Age-appropriate progressions (10 & under → College)
4. **Adaptation** — The stress + rest equation
5. **Training Consistency** — Long-term commitment beats short bursts

**Key Philosophy:**
> "The body responds better to frequent, stimulating stress and complementary rest than it does to a few overwhelming sessions. Once the body is in a state of considerable fatigue, going deeper into fatigue is of no benefit whatever."

---

## 1. Specificity of Training

### The Principle
**You must train the specific energy systems that your race will use.**

Sprint events (50-100) rely heavily on:
- ATP-CP system (immediate energy)
- Anaerobic glycolysis (lactic acid system)

Distance events (800-1500) rely heavily on:
- Aerobic system

Middle-distance (200-400) requires:
- **BOTH anaerobic AND aerobic systems**
- Most challenging to train properly

### Practical Application

**For Sprinters:**
- Build aerobic base first (early season)
- Transition to anaerobic work mid-season
- Emphasize pure speed work 3× per week in championship phase

**For Distance Swimmers:**
- Heavy aerobic training early season
- Maintain aerobic base throughout
- Add anaerobic work 2× per week mid-season
- Focus on pace work in championship phase

**For Middle-Distance:**
- Balance is critical
- Cannot neglect either system
- Aerobic sets with shorter rest intervals
- Anaerobic work twice weekly

---

## 2. Energy Systems

### The Three Fuel Tanks

#### 1. ATP-CP System (Phosphagen System)
**Duration:** 0-10 seconds  
**Power:** MAXIMUM  
**Recovery:** 2-3 minutes for full replenishment  

**What it does:**
- Immediate energy source
- No oxygen required
- Powers explosive starts, turns, finishes
- Depletes rapidly

**Training implications:**
- Pure speed work (12.5 - 25 yard sprints)
- Long rest intervals (3-5 minutes)
- Maximum effort required
- Quality over quantity

---

#### 2. Lactic Acid System (Anaerobic Glycolysis)
**Duration:** 10 seconds - 2 minutes  
**Power:** VERY HIGH  
**Byproduct:** Lactic acid accumulation (the "burn")  

**What it does:**
- Breaks down glycogen without oxygen
- Produces energy FAST but creates lactic acid
- Dominant system in 100-200 events
- Critical for all sprint/mid-distance racing

**Training implications:**
- Short rest intervals (work-to-rest ratio ~3:1)
- High-intensity repeats (50s, 75s, 100s)
- Tolerance training = teaching body to buffer lactic acid
- Painful but necessary

**Example set:** 8 × 100 on 1:30, holding 200 race pace

---

#### 3. Aerobic System (Oxidative System)
**Duration:** 2+ minutes  
**Power:** MODERATE (but sustainable)  
**Fuel:** Carbohydrates, fats (with oxygen)  

**What it does:**
- Produces energy efficiently using oxygen
- Sustainable for long periods
- Foundation for ALL swimming
- Allows recovery between hard efforts

**Training implications:**
- Long, continuous swims (1000-3000+)
- Comfortable interval training
- Base-building work
- Must be developed FIRST in every season

**The 75% Rule:**
> "Swimmers should be able to complete 75% of a set at the prescribed pace. If they cannot, the interval is too fast or they are not properly conditioned."

---

## 3. Developmental Training (Age-Appropriate)

### Ages 10 and Under

**Primary Goals:**
1. **Stroke mechanics** — Perfect technique first
2. **Fun & enjoyment** — Build love of sport
3. **Aerobic base** — Long, easy swimming
4. **Basic fitness** — Games, relays, variety

**Training characteristics:**
- Volume: 2,000-4,000 yards per session
- Frequency: 3-5 times per week
- Intensity: LOW (mostly aerobic)
- Focus: Learning proper movement patterns

**DO NOT:**
- Emphasize anaerobic training
- Push for times
- Create burnout through excessive volume
- Sacrifice technique for speed

**Schubert's warning:**
> "Early specialization and high-intensity training before physical maturity can lead to early plateaus and burnout. Build the foundation slowly."

---

### Ages 11-14 (Junior High Years)

**Transition period:** Increased physical capacity meets continued learning

**Training progression:**
- Volume increases: 4,000-6,000 yards per session
- Frequency: 5-7 times per week
- Intensity: Still mostly aerobic, introduce anaerobic work carefully
- Stroke refinement: Technique should be "locked in" by age 14

**New elements:**
- **Threshold training** — Swimming at or near lactate threshold
- **Pace work** — Learning to hold target speeds
- **Dry-land training** — Light resistance, high reps, flexibility
- **Racing strategy** — Learning to race smart

**Key principle:**
> "Biomechanics will change as the body grows. A 10-year-old butterfly is NOT the same as a 16-year-old butterfly. Limb ratios change, power changes. Adapt technique to the maturing body."

---

### Ages 14-18 (High School Years)

**The shift:** Physical maturity allows real strength and speed development

**Training characteristics:**
- Volume: 6,000-10,000 yards per session (depending on event specialty)
- Anaerobic work: 2-3 times per week
- Dry-land: Increased resistance training
- Power development: Sprint work becomes more important

**What changes:**
- **Strength training** becomes productive (hormonal maturity)
- **Anaerobic capacity** can be significantly developed
- **Speed work** yields better results
- **Aerobic base** must still be maintained

**Balance:**
- Early season: Aerobic foundation (60-70% of training)
- Mid-season: Anaerobic work increases (30-40%)
- Championship: Taper with quality speed work

---

### College Age & Beyond

**Peak performance years:** Physical tools are fully developed

**Focus shifts to:**
1. **Refinement** over learning
2. **Consistency** over novelty
3. **Recovery** becomes more important
4. **Mental preparation** separates good from great

**Training approach:**
- Volume similar to high school (or slightly higher)
- Intensity can be higher due to maturity
- Recovery must be monitored closely
- Stress management (academic, social) is critical

**Schubert's insight:**
> "The college swimmer faces academic stress and social temptations. The dedicated college swimmer is in a wonderful situation for personal development — if they can maintain training consistency."

---

## 4. Adaptation: The Stress + Rest Equation

### The Fundamental Principle

**Training = Stress + Rest**

- **Stress** breaks down the body
- **Rest** allows super-compensation (rebuilding stronger)
- **Imbalance** leads to overtraining OR undertraining

### Stress Sources

#### Voluntary Stress (Training)
- Pool workouts
- Dry-land training
- Competitions

#### Involuntary Stress (Life)
- **Weather:** Extreme cold or heat
- **Illness:** Even minor colds affect performance for days
- **School:** Exams, homework, academic pressure
- **Emotional:** Relationships, family issues, anxiety

**Critical insight:**
> "Stress is cumulative. You cannot isolate training stress from life stress. To maximize training adaptation, minimize unnecessary external stressors."

---

### Rest: The Key to Adaptation

**What is rest?**
1. **Sleep** — The most substantial recovery mechanism
   - Adolescents need 8-10 hours per night
   - Swimmers training hard need MORE sleep, not less
   - American society rarely allows this luxury

2. **Active recovery** — Easy swimming, stretching
3. **Complete rest** — Days off (strategic, not frequent)

**The super-compensation curve:**
```
Stress → Fatigue → Rest → Recovery → STRONGER (super-compensation)
                                   ↓
                              (next stress applied HERE)
```

**Timing matters:**
- Too little rest = chronic fatigue, poor performance, overtraining
- Too much rest = detraining, loss of adaptations
- Just right = steady improvement

---

### Work/Rest Cycles

**Weekly microcycle:**
- Hard-Easy-Hard-Easy-Hard-Moderate-Easy
- Alternate muscle groups (kick vs. pull vs. swim)
- Vary energy systems (aerobic vs. anaerobic)

**Seasonal mesocycle:**
- High volume → Moderate volume + intensity → Lower volume + high intensity
- Build → Train → Taper

**4-Year macrocycle (Elite):**
- Year 1 (post-Olympics): Low stress, recovery
- Year 2: Increase stress, World Championships
- Year 3: Moderate stress, developmental year
- Year 4: ALL-OUT effort for Olympics

**Schubert's warning:**
> "If you place too much stress on the body's systems, they will become chronically fatigued—'stale' or 'overtrained'—and will perform poorly and recover very slowly between efforts."

---

## 5. Training Consistency

### The Non-Negotiable

**Research shows:**
- Long breaks from training are detrimental to high performance
- Frequent, shorter sessions beat infrequent long sessions
- Base level of conditioning rises with successive seasons (if consistent)

**Weekly pattern:**
- Better: 5-6 moderate sessions per week
- Worse: 3 exhausting sessions per week

**Yearly pattern:**
- Better: Year-round training with strategic breaks
- Worse: On-again, off-again seasonal training

**Career pattern:**
- Better: Multi-year progression with increasing base
- Worse: Peaks and valleys, starting over each year

### Hidden Training

**The 80/20 rule:**
- 20% of your day = training in the pool
- 80% of your day = everything else

**"Hidden training" = how you live the other 80%:**
- Sleep quality and quantity
- Nutrition choices
- Stress management
- Recovery practices
- Avoiding alcohol, drugs, behavior-modifying substances

**Schubert's philosophy:**
> "Consistency demands that you do nothing during the other 80 percent of the time to undermine that positive work."

**Performance-destroying substances:**
- **Alcohol:** Long-term detriment to performance
- **Anabolic steroids:** Extremely harmful long-term effects
- **Drugs:** Behavior-modifying substances destroy consistency

---

## Practical Applications for Coaches

### Building a Season

**Early Season (Weeks 1-4):**
- Aerobic foundation
- Long, slow distance
- Easy interval work
- Stroke technique refinement
- Volume: Build from 60% → 100% of target

**Mid-Season (Weeks 5-12):**
- Maintain aerobic base
- Introduce anaerobic work (2× per week)
- Increase intensity, maintain volume
- Begin race-pace work
- Threshold sets become common

**Championship Season (Weeks 13-16):**
- Reduce volume 20% per week
- Maintain quality
- Emphasize race-specific work
- Taper begins 3 weeks out
- Focus on speed, starts, turns

---

### Setting Intervals (The 75% Rule)

**The Animal Night Test:**

If 75% of your swimmers cannot complete the set at the prescribed pace, the interval is WRONG.

**Adjust by:**
1. Slowing the interval (more rest)
2. Reducing distance per rep
3. Breaking set into smaller chunks
4. Checking if swimmers are properly conditioned

**Example:**
- Set: 10 × 100 on 1:20
- If only 3 of 10 swimmers can complete it → interval too fast
- Better: 10 × 100 on 1:30 (achievable by 8+ swimmers)

---

### Monitoring Adaptation

**Signs of positive adaptation:**
- Morning heart rate stays low
- Times improve week-over-week
- Swimmers recover quickly between efforts
- Energy levels are good
- Enthusiasm for training

**Signs of overtraining:**
- Elevated resting heart rate
- Times plateau or decline
- Sluggish recovery
- Chronic fatigue
- Loss of motivation, irritability
- Increased illness

**Schubert's remedy:**
- Reduce volume immediately
- Increase rest
- Review external stressors (school, life)
- Possibly add recovery week

---

## Mental Framework

### The Long Game

**Key mindset shifts:**
1. **Process over results** — Trust the training cycle
2. **Patience** — Development takes years, not weeks
3. **Consistency** — Small daily wins compound
4. **Adaptation** — Stress + rest = growth
5. **Individuality** — Each swimmer adapts differently

**Schubert's wisdom:**
> "No one workout, week, month, or even year will make or break you. You must have a training plan that takes into account specificity, developmental training, and adaptation, and you must carry it out in a thorough and consistent manner."

---

## Key Takeaways for Coaches

1. **Train the right systems** — Sprinters need speed work, distance swimmers need aerobic base, middle-distance needs BOTH
2. **Respect developmental stages** — Don't rush young swimmers into high-intensity work
3. **Stress + Rest = Adaptation** — The equation must balance
4. **Consistency wins** — Frequent moderate sessions beat sporadic brutal ones
5. **Monitor external stress** — School, illness, emotions all affect training
6. **Sleep is non-negotiable** — 8-10 hours for adolescent swimmers
7. **Use the 75% rule** — If most swimmers can't complete the set, you've miscalculated
8. **Build aerobic base first** — EVERY season starts with foundation work
9. **Taper is part of training** — Reduce volume 20% per week for 3 weeks pre-championship
10. **"Hidden training" matters** — What swimmers do outside the pool affects performance

---

# Mark Schubert: Sample Workouts

**Source:** *Competitive Swimming: Techniques for Champions* (1990, 1996)  
**Author:** Mark Schubert (USC coach, multiple Olympic teams)  
**Context:** Practical workout sets for sprinters, middle-distance, and distance swimmers  
**Significance:** Real training sessions from one of swimming's greatest coaches — copy these verbatim or adapt

---

## How to Use This Page

### Workout Structure
Each workout follows this pattern:
1. **Warmup** — Easy swimming, stroke drills, technique focus
2. **Main Sets** — The meat of the workout (kick, pull, swim)
3. **Loosen down** — Easy recovery swimming

### Shorthand Legend
- **K** = kickboard
- **P** = pull buoy
- **f** = fast
- **e** = easy
- **alt. br.** = alternate breathing
- **descend** = each rep faster than the previous
- **buildup** = increase speed throughout the rep
- **on [time]** = interval (e.g., "on 1:30" = 1:30 per 100)

**Example:** "16 × 50 K on 1:10, 3f, 1e"  
= 16 sets of 50 yards kick with kickboard, on 1:10 interval, swim 3 fast then 1 easy

---

## Sprint Workouts (50-100 Events)

### Philosophy for Sprinters
- Start season with aerobic foundation (long, slow swims)
- Progress to short-rest aerobic sets (weeks 1-3)
- Add pure speed work starting week 4 (25s → 50s)
- Mid-season: Increase anaerobic work to 3× per week
- Championship: Reduce volume 20%/week, emphasize quality starts/turns

---

### Early Season Sprint Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Building aerobic base, introducing speed

**Warmup:**  
600 yards easy with emphasis on stroke technique  
(Use stroke drills as warmup device)

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
16 × 50 K on 1:10, 3f, 1e

**Pull:**  
5 × 200 P on 2:50, buoy, alt. br., descend  
(Each 200 faster than the previous)

**Swim:**  
12 × 100 free on 1:30  
(Hold comfortable aerobic pace)

**Speed:**  
8 × 50 free on 1:10, 1st 25 fast

**Loosen Down:**  
300 stroke drill, easy

**Total:** ~4,000 yards

---

### Mid-Season Sprint Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Maintain speed, increase anaerobic capacity

**Warmup:**  
8 × 100 on descending intervals:
- 2 on 1:40
- 2 on 1:35
- 2 on 1:30
- 2 on 1:25

**Main Sets:**

**Kick (no breaks between sets):**  
- 4 × 75 on 1:40, last 25 fast
- 4 × 75 on 1:35, last 50 fast
- 4 × 75 on 1:30, whole 75 fast  
(Looks like 12 × 75)

**Pull:**  
"Locomotive" up to 4 laps, freestyle, on :30 intervals:
- 1 lap easy, 1 lap fast
- 2 laps easy, 2 laps fast
- 3 laps easy, 3 laps fast
- 4 laps easy, 4 laps fast  
(Total: 20 laps = 500 yards)

**Swim (broken 100s):**  
9 × 100 yards:
- First 3: Continuous, very fast (3-5 min rest between)
- Second 3: Broken at 50 (:05 rest at wall)
- Last 3: Broken at each 25 (:05 rest at each wall)

**Loosen Down:**  
8 × 100 on 1:45, alternating left arm only / right arm only, with paddles, easy

**Total:** ~4,500 yards

---

### Championship Season Sprint Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Quality over quantity, race simulation

**Warmup:**  
400 stroke drills

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
12 × 50 K on 1:15:
- 1 easy
- 1 buildup
- 1 fast (90% effort)

**Pull:**  
5 × 100 free on 1:30, last 25 strong

**Race Pace:**  
8 × 50 free on 2:00:
- 1 easy
- 1 FAST from dive start (90% of best effort)

**Neuromuscular Speed:**  
8 × 25 free on 1:00:
- First 4: Fast first 12.5
- Last 4: Fast last 12.5

**Loosen Down:**  
400 stroke drill

**Total:** ~2,800 yards  
(Volume reduced ~30% from mid-season)

---

## Middle-Distance Workouts (200-400 Events)

### Philosophy for Middle-Distance
- Larger aerobic base than sprinters (first 3 weeks)
- Aerobic work emphasized throughout season
- Anaerobic training 2× per week mid-season
- Championship phase: 3-week taper, 20% volume reduction per week
- Race-pace sets to simulate competition

---

### Early Season Middle-Distance Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Building aerobic foundation

**Warmup:**  
600 stroke drill

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
5 × 200 on 4:10, descending

**Pull:**  
Ladder (50-100-150-200-150-100-50) with :15 rest between, repeat 2×  
(Total pull: 1,400 yards)

**Swim:**  
12 × 150 on descending intervals:
- 4 on 2:15
- 4 on 2:10
- 4 on 2:05  
Descend 1-4 within each group

**Speed:**  
8 × (75, 50, 25):
- 75 on 1:10
- 50 on :50
- 25 on :30  
Last 25 of each swim is fast

**Loosen Down:**  
3 × 200 stroke drill with :30 rest

**Total:** ~5,500 yards

---

### Mid-Season Middle-Distance Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Aerobic maintenance + anaerobic development

**Warmup:**  
10 × 100:
- 5 on 1:30
- 5 on 1:20

**Main Sets:**

**Kick (with fins):**  
3 rounds of:
- 150 on 2:15
- 100 on 1:30
- 50 on :45

**Swim (descending pyramid):**  
- 4 × 200 on 2:30, descending 1-4
- 1 × 100 on 1:30
- 3 × 200 on 2:35, descending
- 2 × 100 on 1:25, descending
- 2 × 200 on 2:40, descending
- 3 × 100 on 1:20, descending
- 1 × 200 on 2:45
- 4 × 100 on 1:15, descending

**Pull:**  
6 × 300:
- 2 on 4:00
- 2 on 4:10
- 2 on 4:20  
Second of each pair faster

**Pace Work:**  
10 × 50:
- 1 easy
- 1 fast on 1:00  
Fast swims hold 200 pace or faster

**Loosen Down:**  
400 stroke drill

**Total:** ~6,500 yards

---

### Championship Season Middle-Distance Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Race simulation, reduced volume

**Warmup:**  
600 stroke drill

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
4 × 100 on 2:00, last 25 strong

**Pull:**  
4 × 200 on 3:00, buildup (going faster throughout)

**Race Pace:**  
8 × 100 on 1:45:
- 1 easy
- 1 at race pace

**Loosen Down:**  
300 stroke drill

**Total:** ~3,200 yards

---

## Distance Workouts (800-1500 Events)

### Philosophy for Distance
- Largest segment of aerobic training early season
- Long, slow swimming + easy intervals (weeks 1-4)
- Mid-season: Continue aerobic work with shorter rest, anaerobic 2× per week
- Championship: Reduce volume, maintain intensity, race-pace sets
- Speed work throughout season to retain neuromuscular ability

---

### Early Season Distance Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Over-distance aerobic base

**Warmup:**  
800 stroke drill

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
6 × 200 on 4:00, descending

**Pull:**  
2,000 continuous pull with buoy  
(Negative split: second 1,000 faster than first)

**Swim:**  
20 × 100 on 1:40, holding consistent pace

**Speed (neuromuscular):**  
8 × 25 on :45, fast

**Loosen Down:**  
400 easy

**Total:** ~6,000 yards

---

### Mid-Season Distance Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Threshold work, pace discipline

**Warmup:**  
10 × 100 on 1:35

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
5 × 300 on 5:30, descending

**Pull:**  
3 × 500 on 7:00, negative split each

**Swim (threshold set):**  
16 × 150 on 2:20, holding pace

**Anaerobic:**  
8 × 100 on 1:30, descending 1-4, repeat

**Speed:**  
10 × 50 on 1:00:
- 1 easy
- 1 fast (200 pace or faster)

**Loosen Down:**  
400 easy

**Total:** ~7,500 yards

---

### Championship Season Distance Workout (SCY)

**Focus:** Race-specific pace, reduced volume

**Warmup:**  
600 stroke drill

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
4 × 150 on 3:00, last 50 fast

**Pull:**  
3 × 300 on 4:30, buildup

**Race Pace:**  
6 × 200 on 3:00, holding race pace

**Speed:**  
6 × 50 on 1:10, fast

**Loosen Down:**  
400 easy

**Total:** ~4,000 yards

---

## Stroke-Specific Workouts

### Breaststroke & Butterfly (Senior Swimmers)

**Focus:** Stroke-specific endurance and power

**Warmup:**  
500 choice stroke drill

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
10 × 100 stroke-specific kick on 2:00

**Pull (with buoy):**  
5 × 200 stroke on 3:30, descending

**Swim:**  
12 × 75 stroke on 1:30:
- 25 drill
- 25 swim
- 25 fast

**Broken 100s:**  
8 × 100 stroke broken at 50 (:10 rest), very fast

**Speed:**  
6 × 25 stroke on 1:00, maximum effort

**Loosen Down:**  
300 easy choice

**Total:** ~4,200 yards

---

## Individual Medley Workouts

### IM Workout (Senior Swimmers)

**Focus:** All four strokes, transitions, endurance

**Warmup:**  
400 IM drill (100 each stroke)

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
4 × (4 × 50) on 1:10:
- Round 1: Fly kick
- Round 2: Back kick
- Round 3: Breast kick
- Round 4: Free kick

**Pull:**  
3 × 300 IM pull on 5:00

**Swim:**  
5 × 200 IM on 3:00, descending

**Broken IM:**  
4 × 100 IM broken at each 25 (:05 rest), fast

**Choice:**  
8 × 50 on 1:00, choice stroke, alternate easy/fast

**Loosen Down:**  
200 easy choice

**Total:** ~4,600 yards

---

## Age-Group Workouts

### Ages 10 and Under

**Focus:** Fun, technique, aerobic base

**Warmup:**  
200 kick/drill/swim (by 50s)

**Main Set:**  
10 × 50 on 1:15, choice stroke

**Game:**  
Relay races (4 × 25)

**Cool Down:**  
100 easy

**Total:** ~800-1,000 yards  
**Frequency:** 3-5× per week

**Key principle:** Keep it fun, emphasize technique, NO anaerobic suffering

---

### Ages 11-14

**Focus:** Technique refinement, increased volume, introduction to pace work

**Warmup:**  
400 stroke drill

**Main Sets:**

**Kick:**  
8 × 50 on 1:20, 2 easy, 2 fast

**Pull:**  
4 × 150 on 2:45, descending

**Swim:**  
10 × 100 on 1:45, holding pace

**Speed:**  
6 × 25 on :45, fast

**Loosen Down:**  
200 choice

**Total:** ~2,500-3,500 yards  
**Frequency:** 5-7× per week

**Key principle:** Still mostly aerobic, begin introducing threshold work

---

## Special Sets & Concepts

### The "Locomotive"

**What it is:** Increasing distances, alternating easy/fast

**Example:**  
- 1 lap easy, 1 lap fast (on :30 per lap)
- 2 laps easy, 2 laps fast (on :30 per lap)
- 3 laps easy, 3 laps fast (on :30 per lap)
- 4 laps easy, 4 laps fast (on :30 per lap)

**Total:** 20 laps (500 yards)

**Why it works:** Builds endurance while teaching pace control

---

### Broken Swims

**What they are:** Swim with brief rest at walls to simulate racing without full fatigue

**Example 1:** 3 × 100 continuous, very fast (3-5 min rest)  
**Example 2:** 3 × 100 broken at 50 (:05 rest)  
**Example 3:** 3 × 100 broken at each 25 (:05 rest)

**Progression:** More rest = faster times = neuromuscular speed training

**Why it works:** Allows swimmers to experience race pace without the lactic acid buildup that would prevent proper technique

---

### Descending Sets

**What it is:** Each rep faster than the previous

**Example:**  
5 × 200 pull on 2:50, descending:
- #1: 2:30
- #2: 2:28
- #3: 2:25
- #4: 2:22
- #5: 2:19

**Why it works:** Teaches pacing discipline and builds confidence as swimmer gets faster

---

### Negative Split Sets

**What it is:** Second half faster than first half

**Example:**  
3 × 500 pull on 7:00, negative split each:
- First 250: 3:35
- Second 250: 3:25

**Why it works:** Mimics smart race strategy, builds back-end speed

---

## Practical Tips for Coaches

### Setting Intervals (The 75% Rule)

**If 75% of your swimmers cannot complete the set at the prescribed pace, the interval is too fast.**

**Adjust by:**
- Adding :05-:10 per rep
- Reducing reps (12 × 100 → 8 × 100)
- Breaking into smaller chunks

**Example:**  
- Planned: 10 × 100 on 1:20
- Only 3 of 10 complete it
- Better: 10 × 100 on 1:30 (now 8+ complete it)

---

### Adapting for SCM/LCM

**These workouts are written for SCY (25-yard pool).**

**To convert to LCM (50-meter):**
- Reduce total reps by ~20%
- Add :10-:20 per 100m compared to 100y interval
- Focus more on underwater work (longer walls)

**To convert to SCM (25-meter):**
- Similar volume to SCY
- Add :02-:05 per 100m compared to 100y interval

---

### Workout Design Philosophy

**Schubert's principles:**
1. **Warm up properly** — Prepare joints, ligaments, muscles
2. **Progress logically** — Kick → Pull → Swim
3. **Vary intensity** — Hard sets followed by recovery
4. **End with speed** — Short, fast efforts while fatigued (race simulation)
5. **Cool down** — Help body clear lactate, prevent injury

**Weekly pattern:**
- Monday: Hard aerobic
- Tuesday: Moderate + speed
- Wednesday: Hard anaerobic
- Thursday: Moderate + technique
- Friday: Hard aerobic or anaerobic
- Saturday: Moderate or race
- Sunday: Easy or off

**Experienced coaches:**
- Use as templates
- Modify for your swimmers' abilities
- Blend concepts (locomotive + descending, etc.)

**Masters coaches:**
- Reduce volume by 20-30%
- Extend intervals
- Emphasize technique over suffering

# Jon Urbanchek - Coach Profile

**Full Name:** János Urbancsok (Americanized to Jon Urbanchek)  
**Born:** 1937, Hungary  
**Died:** May 10, 2024 (age 87)  
**Position:** Head Coach, University of Michigan Men's Swimming & Diving (1982-2004)  
**Hall of Fame:** International Swimming Hall of Fame (2008)

---

## Legacy

Jon Urbanchek stands among swimming's legendary coaches—a Hungarian immigrant who became one of America's most successful college coaches, bringing Michigan back to national prominence after 34 years and proving that distance-focused training could win NCAA championships.

**"He was a mentor, a great coach, a comedian, had a big heart."** — John Piersma

---

## Coaching Record at Michigan

**Tenure:** 1982-2004 (22 seasons)

**Championships:**
- **1995 NCAA National Championship** (Michigan's first since 1961)
- **13 Big Ten Championships** (including 10 consecutive: 1986-1995)

**Competition Record:**
- **163-34** in dual meets overall
- **100-4** in Big Ten competition

**Athlete Development:**
- **35 individual NCAA champions** produced
- **26 Olympians** representing their countries
- **10 Olympic medalists** (17 total Olympic medals)

---

## Revolutionary Training Philosophy

### The Color-Coded Energy System

Urbanchek pioneered a visual training intensity system based on physiological observation that revolutionized how coaches communicate effort levels:

- **WHITE:** Easy aerobic, warm-up pace
- **PINK:** Moderate aerobic, building effort  
- **RED:** Hard sustained effort, threshold work
- **BLUE:** Above threshold, lactate accumulation
- **PURPLE:** Maximum effort, near collapse
- **GREEN:** Lactate tolerance work (fast until you puke!)

### Distance Dominance Strategy

**Bold statement:**
> "We're making a statement by not swimming the 400 free relay. We specialize in distance. Let it be a good example for others—you can still win the NCAAs without putting emphasis on the sprint events."

**Methodology:**
- Threshold-based training with individualized zones
- 20-30 minute steady-state threshold swims
- Volume: 6,000-8,000+ yards per session
- Testing protocols: T30, 10x200 @ :20-30", 8x300 averaging
- Systematic season structure: 12-15 weeks build, 3 weeks taper

---

## Personal Background

**Swimmer at Michigan (1957-1961):**
- Swam under legendary coach Gus Stager
- Part of Michigan's golden era (4 national titles in 5 years: 1957-1961)
- The 1995 championship was deeply personal—returning Michigan to glory after 34 years

**Immigration Story:**
- Born János Urbancsok in Hungary, 1937
- Fled communist Hungary
- Came to America, swam at Michigan
- Built one of college swimming's greatest coaching careers

**Coaching Style:**
- Mental architect who pushed psychological buttons differently for each athlete
- Used humor to defuse tension among hyper-competitive alphas
- "Team over individual" culture builder
- Sometimes "pat on back while kicking in seat"
- Made it easy for elite competitors to race each other in practice

---

## The 1995 Championship Team

**Final Score:** Michigan 561.5, Stanford 475.5 (86-point margin)

**Star Swimmers:**
- **Gustavo Borges** (Brazil) - 3x NCAA Champion, 2x Olympic silver medalist
- **Tom Dolan** (USA) - 3x NCAA Champion, 2x Olympic gold medalist (400 IM)
- **Eric Namesnik** (USA) - 2x Olympic silver medalist
- **Marcel Wouda** (Netherlands) - World Champion, Olympic medalist
- **John Piersma** (USA) - Future NCAA Champion, Olympian
- 7 future Olympians total on roster

**Team Accomplishments:**
- Won ALL FIVE freestyle events (50, 100, 200, 500, 1650)
- 6 individual NCAA titles, 1 relay title
- 14 of 15 swimmers scored points (unprecedented depth)
- Rallied after 400 free relay DQ on final day (lost 40 points, won anyway)

**What Made Them Special:**
- International diversity strengthened culture
- Distance dominance philosophy
- Unprecedented depth
- Mental toughness
- Team-first culture
- Historical weight (34-year redemption story)

---

## Notable Quotes

**On the 1995 Championship (Tom Dolan):**
> "It will always go down for me as the most fun I had in swimming. Even ahead of winning gold medals and breaking world records... the most joy in the sport I got was being part of the team representing Michigan and finally winning in '95."

**On Urbanchek the Person (John Piersma):**

## Timeline

- **1937:** Born János Urbancsok in Hungary
- **1950s:** Fled Hungary, came to America
- **1957-1961:** Swam at University of Michigan under Gus Stager
- **1961:** Michigan wins NCAA Championship (last one before 1995)
- **1982:** Becomes Head Coach at Michigan
- **1986-1995:** 10 consecutive Big Ten Championships
- **1995:** NCAA National Championship (Michigan 561.5, Stanford 475.5)
- **1996:** Two swimmers win Olympic gold (Tom Dolan - 400 IM)
- **2000:** Tom Dolan repeats as Olympic Champion (400 IM)
- **2004:** Retires from Michigan after 22 seasons
- **2008:** Inducted into International Swimming Hall of Fame
- **May 10, 2024:** Passed away at age 87

---

## Impact & Legacy

Jon Urbanchek proved:
- Distance-focused training COULD win NCAA championships
- International diversity strengthened team culture
- Team depth mattered more than individual stars
- Mental toughness was systematically trainable
- Color-coding could revolutionize training communication

The 1995 Michigan Wolverines are still cited as one of the greatest college swimming teams of all time.

When Jon Urbanchek passed in May 2024, the tributes from his swimmers weren't about the championship—they were about the culture, the joy, the brotherhood he built.
